channel.title: Rob Burbea Dharma Talks
channel.description: A podcast archive of Rob Burbea Dharma talks from Dharma Seed, enriched with transcript-derived summaries, chapters, and episode artwork.
channel.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
channel.itunes:summary: A podcast archive of Rob Burbea Dharma talks from Dharma Seed, enriched with transcript-derived summaries, chapters, and episode artwork.

item.title: Perfection and Christ's Blessing
item.description:
In this short reflection, Rob Burbea rethinks perfection as something present within being itself, rather than as achievement or completion. Using the small act of washing hands while weakened and ill, he points toward a beauty or grace that can be sensed in ordinary contact, loss, and even dukkha. He then turns to Christ’s blessing as a contemplative lens for this same kind of seeing, before briefly naming the problem of theodicy and returning to the heart’s quieter knowing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 A different perfection 00:18 Hands, weakness, grace 01:12 Seeing, not achieving 02:21 Christ’s blessing 03:10 Beyond theodicy 03:58 Blessing in dukkha
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62457/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 5)
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a short preliminary practice that combines voicing, posture, and small movement as a way of training sensitivity. He demonstrates how to vary a simple sustained sound across gesture, pitch, loudness, and bodily orientation, then invites listeners to notice effects in the energy body, emotions, and soul resonance. The talk stays practical and exploratory, emphasizing repetition, subtlety, and not expecting too much from the exercise.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting Up the Exercise 01:01 Image, Voice, Movement 03:02 Voicing a Simple Sound 05:45 Open and Crossed Postures 07:04 Varying the Gesture 08:58 Pitch and Loudness 10:55 Noticing the Effects
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62456/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 4)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea revisits exercise four from a series on voice, movement, and gesture, and gently shifts it away from performing an idea of blessing toward sensing blessing in the moment. He then widens the frame through mantra, the felt mystery of sound, and the role of self-consciousness, metta, and compassion in dyadic practice. The second half turns to iconic images and moments — Christ on the Via Dolorosa, the Buddha touching the earth, Rosa Parks refusing to move, and Abraham welcoming the angels — as ways of entering bodily, ethical, and imaginal sensitivity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Blessing as lived intention 12:44 Voice beyond meaning 20:23 Relational sensitivity 39:03 Imaginal dyad variations 56:21 Christ and the earth 01:11:22 Abraham's hospitality 01:25:26 Rosa Parks' immovability 01:47:56 Tantric language and Buddha nature 01:56:08 Preliminaries that deepen
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62455/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins by using a personal period of being overwhelmed to question the ordinary idea of “centering,” then opens that term into a wider critique of selfhood shaped by interiority, boundaries, and singularity. From there he contrasts apophatic emptiness with an imaginal sense of angel, soul, image, and participation, and shows how those terms can change how difficulty, practice, and relationship are understood. The talk closes by introducing a dyad exercise in blessing through movement, gesture, and voice, first with meaning and then with a more stripped-down mode that lets expression move beyond ordinary language.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and orientation 06:11 What centering implies 17:17 Emptiness and imaginal presence 27:30 Stretch, devotion, and risk 37:24 Soulmaking and the person 52:17 Meaning, participation, and image 01:00:14 First blessing exercise 01:14:13 Beyond ordinary meaning
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62454/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a talk on preliminary Soulmaking practices, widening the frame from earlier exercises into why these small practices matter. He connects movement, gesture, and voice to sensitivity, blocked energy, and the possibility that soul can express itself more fully in practice and ordinary life. Later he introduces five versions of a third exercise and keeps returning to the question of discernment: when to concretize, when to stay with image, and what helps practice become sustainable over time.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting the frame 06:26 Why the preliminaries matter 11:31 Sensitivity and holding back 24:51 Regret and the middle way 32:00 Exercise three begins 42:00 Angels and the raging beast 46:55 Guardian and serpent 50:50 Circuits and consequences 01:05:22 Discernment in life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62453/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces a set of five preliminary practices for Soulmaking Dharma, explaining why he is offering them now and why they center on movement, gesture, and voice. Before the first exercise, he lingers on the larger question of how to listen: not only for specific instructions, but for the deeper conceptual structure that gives them meaning. The talk keeps returning to the risk of narrowing practice into a small goal, and instead points toward sensitivity, development, and a wider sense of what practice can serve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the preliminaries 01:56 Movement, voice, and development 07:27 Listening for instructions 10:02 Why this focus matters 19:54 Inertia and structure 34:47 Desire and the path 51:08 What practice is for 01:15:02 Exercise one: movement 01:28:56 Exercise two: voice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62452/

item.title: Practising with Death and Dying
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores death and dying through the lens of Soulmaking Dharma, moving away from familiar Buddhist and secular views toward a more phenomenological inquiry. After briefly setting aside impermanence, afterlife, rebirth, and even physics-based analogies, he turns to practice-based ways of seeing that can make death feel less flat and less final. The talk then opens into gifts, duty, beauty, grief, and imaginal relationship, including extended examples of a friend’s terminal illness and how death itself can become a beloved presence.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Question 03:17 Views To Set Aside 11:41 Physics And Perception 23:50 Phenomenology Of Death 43:49 What Helps Death 53:46 Gift, Duty, Beauty 01:11:35 Burying The Beloved 01:29:55 Image And Relationship 02:04:47 A Death Re-Enchanted
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61078/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 6 - An Inconclusive Conclusion, or A Prolegomenon)
item.description:
Rob Burbea closes this part of the Image of Ethics series with a deliberately open-ended reflection on ethics, precepts, and Soulmaking Dharma. He argues that the five precepts are valuable but incomplete, and then widens the frame through climate change, power, patriarchy, ceremony, and the question of whether teaching should be freely available. The talk moves from caution about misunderstanding to a broader inquiry into values, hierarchies of value, and the safeguards needed when imaginal and emptiness teachings are taken up.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the inquiry 04:06 Why precepts are not enough 09:46 Complacency and shadow 13:38 From rules to values 17:30 Ceremony and safety 19:43 Prerequisites and readiness 27:57 Hartmann and hierarchy 37:18 Nobility and the remote 46:41 Imaginal safeguards
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61077/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 5)
item.description: (Gaia House) CRUCIAL NOTE: It is highly unlikely that this talk will be properly or adequately understood without a prior very good working familiarity and competence – both in actual practice and conceptually – with Soulmaking Dharma teachings and practices, as well as with Insight Meditation. Without this background it may be that the talk will in fact be misunderstood, and it is unlikely that the talk will be helpful. Please note too that much of the material in this series of talks (In Psyche’s Orchard) is based on or continues explorations of material laid out in a particular previous series (Four Circles, Four Parables of Stone and Light). In Psyche’s Orchard was recorded by Rob at his home. Location: Gaia House Source: Dharma Seed Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61068/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his inquiry into how Soulmaking Dharma reshapes ethics by asking what kind of human being ethics presupposes and creates. He moves from lived experience, including the contrast between soul-level and bodily suffering, into a historical survey of Western thought, especially Protestantism, rationality, and the flattening of human dignity. The talk closes by asking whether ethics can be understood as something soulful and expansive rather than merely restrictive, and how that changes our sense of blessedness, duty, and what a human life is for.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:29 Soulmaking and humanity 12:13 Kinds of suffering 17:25 Flattening the human 24:46 Reformation and rupture 33:07 Grace and ethics 49:47 Telos and soulmaking 01:03:44 Shame, eros, and dignity 01:23:54 What ethics is for
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61079/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 3)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea continues the question of how ethics needs some sense of depth or dimensionality in order to feel grounded. He moves through voluntarism, the Protestant Reformation, Augustine, modern utilitarianism, and rationality, asking what each framework takes to be real and how that shapes moral life. The talk gradually turns toward Soulmaking Dharma as a way of re-seeing value, eros, hierarchy, and suffering without collapsing them into a flat, purely instrumental view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Ethics and dimensionality 09:18 Voluntarism and scripture 14:26 Reformation and modern flattening 28:37 Augustine and ordered love 39:56 Modern cosmos, participatory reality 55:42 Soulmaking and the world 01:26:59 Rationality and the Logos 01:51:08 Limits of rational ethics 02:14:16 What lies behind reduction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61075/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a wide-ranging inquiry into how ethics can be grounded without collapsing into rules, rights, or mechanical psychology. He argues for expanding ethics toward questions of value, aspiration, and what makes a life worthwhile, then turns to shame, guilt, and the Buddhist teachings of hiri and ottappa as potentially wholesome moral capacities. In the second half, he develops the idea of dimensionality: how imaginal practice, soulmaking, and older religious or philosophical frameworks can restore depth, mediation, and orientation to ethical life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Recap and ethical expansion 04:04 Worthwhile life questions 09:11 Freedom without grounding 18:56 Against mechanical ethics 26:05 Needs of an ethics 34:07 Soulmaking and imaginal practice 50:56 Shame, guilt, and conscience 01:01:15 Why ethics needs depth 01:23:57 Historical sources of dimensionality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61074/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a long argument for rethinking ethics beyond law, rights, and rule-following, using Soulmaking Dharma as a broader basis for moral life. He asks what makes life beautiful, worthwhile, and sacred, and why ethics needs emotion, eros, dimensionality, and a lived sense of value rather than only instructions about what to do. The talk moves through examples from Extinction Rebellion, Nazi Germany, the five precepts, and contemporary moral discourse to show where current frameworks feel thin or distorted.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting the frame 07:07 Why ethics matters now 14:02 Limits of law and rights 20:23 What ethics needs 28:35 Beyond duty alone 37:11 XR and public rhetoric 58:03 Omission and aspiration 01:16:38 Virtue, image, and ritual 01:33:32 Eros as ethical guidance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61073/

item.title: Pain, as Void and as Sacrament
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a practical and increasingly imaginal set of ways of working with physical pain in meditation. He begins with simple mindfulness and the easing of reactivity, then moves through insight practices, emptiness-based ways of looking, jhana-based re-perception, and finally soulmaking and ritual. The talk gradually widens from reducing suffering to asking what is most sacred, and how pain can sometimes be met as gift, offering, or divine appearance rather than only as a problem to solve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the terrain 05:01 A progressive list 08:47 Mindfulness and bare attention 15:13 How pain is fabricated 27:11 Insight ways of looking 42:25 Jhana and malleability 53:02 Why not fully fade? 01:09:38 Mahayana and Vajrayana frames 01:31:40 Sacrifice and soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61071/

item.title: The Ontology of the Soul, and the Soul of Ontology ( ... inevitably)
item.description:
Rob Burbea distinguishes the imaginal middle way from the emptiness middle way, arguing that the first is a broad, practice-based openness to images while the second is the result of a precise ontological inquiry. He then widens the frame into questions of conventional truth, erosion of reductionist psychology, eros, discipline, and the ways our assumptions shape what can be known, valued, and sustained. The talk closes by turning to time itself, suggesting that time may be dependent on the way of looking and asking what that implies for healing, liberation, and death.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:43 Two Middle Ways 02:39 Imaginal and Emptiness 06:22 Practice and Ontology 18:53 Conventional Reality 25:55 Soul and Reductionism 38:43 Eros and Disclosure 01:02:58 Conventional Truths at Work 01:31:47 What Models Open 01:51:59 Time and Liberation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61069/

item.title: Emptiness and Ways of Looking
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores emptiness through his “ways of looking” approach, arguing that perception, self, time, and suffering are all shaped by how the mind relates to experience. He pauses repeatedly to correct common misunderstandings: that emptiness is a final belief, a bare neutral state, or a license for nihilism. The talk moves from basic definitions to ethics, trauma, imaginal practice, and a careful comparison with quantum physics, while insisting that all of this must remain an experiential inquiry rather than an abstraction.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:30 Ways of looking 09:48 No final resting place 21:50 Seeing as, not through 31:59 Subtle distortions 46:42 Practice and flexibility 01:01:05 Ethics and imagination 01:21:55 Eros in practice 01:36:54 Quantum parallels and awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61070/

item.title: PS - Playing in the In-Betweens
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a practical postscript on working skillfully with jhana when experience is partial, changing, or only partly stabilized. He emphasizes a responsive rhythm of practice, the importance of posture, and ways of using imagination to meet pain, dullness, and incomplete absorption without forcing. The talk ends by widening the frame: jhana as a replenishing resource in daily life and as something that can deepen our sense of human and cosmic dimensionality.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Postscript 02:22 Responsive Practice Rhythm 08:49 Posture and Embodiment 15:56 Working with Difficulty 18:38 Partial Jhana Skills 22:31 Moving Between Jhanas 30:49 Fourth Jhana and Breath 39:52 Closing Reflections 01:00:02 Dimensionality of Being
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60866/

Rob Burbea offers a practical postscript on working skillfully with jhana when experience is partial, changing, or only partly stabilized. He emphasizes a responsive rhythm of practice, the importance of posture, and ways of using imagination to meet pain, dullness, and incomplete absorption without forcing. The talk ends by widening the frame: jhana as a replenishing resource in daily life and as something that can deepen our sense of human and cosmic dimensionality.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Postscript 02:22 Responsive Practice Rhythm 08:49 Posture and Embodiment 15:56 Working with Difficulty 18:38 Partial Jhana Skills 22:31 Moving Between Jhanas 30:49 Fourth Jhana and Breath 39:52 Closing Reflections 01:00:02 Dimensionality of Being
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60866/

item.title: Going Forwards
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a grounded closing talk on how to leave retreat well: appreciating effort without measuring success, caring for the sensitive heart, and carrying practice into ordinary life. He frames practice as a set of streams and playgrounds—jhana, insight, Brahmavihara, emptiness, soulmaking, generosity, right speech—best approached with steady intention, playfulness, and clear curiosity about what one actually desires. The talk ends with a long dedication to the welfare of others and a reminder that practice ripples outward in ways we cannot fully know.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:30 Appreciation for effort 02:57 Caring for the heart 07:24 Steady intention, playful practice 10:02 Practice streams and mandala 13:36 Balancing the streams 17:54 Withdrawal from entanglement 21:00 Tools and the bookmark 23:33 Desire, frameworks, and groups 29:22 Group intentions clarified
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60860/

item.title: The End of Time (The Cessation of Perception and Feeling)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the unfabricated as something beyond ordinary perception, time, and the familiar sense-world, then turns to how it can be approached through jhana and insight ways of looking. He distinguishes this from simply being calm, bare, or unthinking, and spends much of the talk clarifying how fabrication, clinging, emptiness, and dependent origination fit together. The talk also pauses for philosophical questions about what can be trusted as knowledge, and for a few Pali Canon passages that frame the practice in unusually detailed terms.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening context 06:00 Unfabricated and emptiness 16:19 Dependent origination 23:54 What the unfabricated means 34:39 Beyond perception 46:14 Experience and knowledge 54:11 Jhana as insight 01:07:06 Sariputta’s discernment 01:15:51 Subject, object, time
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60885/

item.title: The Eighth Jhana (The Realm of Neither Perception Nor Non-Perception)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the eighth jhana as a state where consciousness no longer lands on any object, not even nothingness. He spends much of the talk clarifying what perception means, arguing that it is not verbal labeling but the fabricating of an object for consciousness, and he uses that definition to place the realm of neither perception nor non-perception within the wider arc of meditative refinement. The talk moves from jhana theory into practice instructions, then widens into a careful warning about aversion, dependent arising, emptiness, and why the conceptual framework around insight matters as much as the experience itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Entering the eighth jhana 01:58 What perception means 04:04 Perception as fabrication 10:15 Nothingness as last object 14:45 Non-landing and release 27:50 Refinement through the jhanas 34:42 Two paths into the state 46:43 Insight without aversion 59:28 Emptiness and intimacy
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60886/

item.title: The Seventh Jhana (The Realm of Nothingness)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the seventh jhana as the dimension of nothingness, starting with the Buddha’s description and then moving into the practical problems of how it is recognized, entered, and trusted. He emphasizes that this territory is often frightening unless it grows out of earlier steadier absorptions, and he keeps returning to the difference between mere absorption and liberating insight. The talk then widens into emptiness, perception, desire, heart-opening, and finally the imaginal possibilities that can open when perception becomes more malleable.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing Nothingness 01:52 Unexpected Openings 08:54 Stepping Stones 15:53 Insight Ways of Looking 21:14 Empty of Self 27:22 Confidence in the Dimension 30:56 No Thingness and Fabrication 44:03 From Samadhi to Insight 56:22 Heart, Desire, and Limits
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60879/

item.title: The Sixth Jhana (The Realm of Infinite Consciousness)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the sixth jhana as the realm of infinite consciousness, reading the Buddha’s brief description and unpacking it as a trainable shift into awareness knowing itself. He distinguishes this from the vastness of awareness, then widens the talk into questions of provisional truth, dependent origination, and how meditation changes perception after the formal sit. The talk closes with practical notes on access, the role of desire, and a brief aside on how imaginal practice can be used tactically when a retreat calls for it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 05:51 Opening the Topic 06:32 Canonical Description 07:39 Awareness Knows Itself 11:07 How Attention Settles 17:46 Intensity and Release 20:15 Vastness Compared 26:52 After-Effects and Truth 41:40 Paths In and View 59:02 Training, Desire, and Use
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60888/

item.title: The Fifth Jhana (The Realm of Infinite Space)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the fifth jhana as the realm of infinite space, moving from the Buddha’s brief sutta description into a careful account of how perception shifts when solidity, resistance, and attention to diversity fall away. He then widens the talk into related themes: the role of metta and compassion in the higher jhanas, the practical use of insight ways of looking, and the way boundless space can change how body, world, and self are perceived. In the latter part of the talk he turns to desire, asking what a person truly wants, how that shapes practice, and how Soulmaking ideas can help sustain an authentic path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the fifth jhana 02:35 The Buddha's description 07:01 What space means 16:57 How it opens 23:36 Fourth to fifth jhana 32:53 Metta and higher realms 42:03 Insight as a way of looking 51:49 Presence and worldviews 01:06:00 Desire, authenticity, and practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60883/

item.title: Jhanas and Insight
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the relationship between jhana and insight as a single continuum rather than a simple two-step path. He questions common models of concentration and awakening, then develops a view in which perception, selfing, clinging, and dukkha all vary together across a spectrum of fabrication. The talk moves from familiar Buddhist formulas to emptiness, dependent origination, and the practical after-effects of meditative states on ordinary perception.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:04 Framing the Question 06:00 Questioning the Usual Formula 10:56 Competing Models of Awakening 20:08 Emptiness and Ways of Looking 34:39 One Spectrum of Fabrication 41:25 Practice, Agency, and Caution 51:48 After-Effects on Perception 58:51 Dependent Origination in Practice 01:09:21 Jhana and Insight Both Ways
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60870/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea moves from practical distinctions in jhana practice to a wider discussion of how attention shapes experience. He answers questions about pīti and sukha, how to work with primary nimitta versus side qualities, and how to tell when to stay with jhana and when a different practice is more appropriate. In the later part of the talk, he turns toward insight practice, dependent arising, and a reading of the Bahīya and Satipaṭṭhāna teachings that emphasizes how perception is fabricated rather than merely observed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening questions 02:08 Primary nimitta and side qualities 08:40 Soulmaking and practice scope 16:50 Pīti, sukha, and self 27:50 Insight and concentration 35:07 Opening, probing, and steadiness 44:41 Sustaining intensity 53:12 Ways of looking at pain 01:02:40 Bahīya and Satipaṭṭhāna
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60878/

item.title: The Fourth Jhana
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the fourth jhana as a state of refined stillness, equanimity, and bright whole-body awareness, moving slowly through the Buddha’s canonical description and then into lived experience. He spends time on what makes this state distinct from the third jhana, how it can feel luminous or even like another world, and why the language of purity, brightness, and imperturbability matters. Later he widens the talk toward perception, dependent origination, emotional life, and how jhana practice can support insight without being reduced to a rigid formula.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 From Third to Fourth 04:17 Canonical Description 07:04 Stillness and Brightness 13:35 Perception and Emptiness 26:33 Equanimity Deepens 44:04 Emotion and Intention 47:21 Wrapped in Stillness 01:05:44 Purity, Malleability, Insight 01:20:58 Base Practice and Maturing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60881/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
This Q&A moves from a practical request to keep silence on retreat into a long, detailed discussion of desire, intention, equanimity, jhāna, and the imaginal life. Rob Burbea repeatedly returns to the idea that Dharma terms work best as spectra rather than fixed categories, and he uses that lens to talk about clinging, self, emotion, and the energy body. The talk also distinguishes jhāna practice from Soulmaking Dharma, especially around eros, two-ness, and dissolution, while warning against spiritual bypassing and vague uses of equanimity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 04:52 Silence on retreat 06:56 Goal and intention 14:35 Desire and devotion 24:17 What equanimity means 36:01 Clinging as a spectrum 45:39 Desire, fabrication, and practice 53:58 Pīti, sukha, and the body 01:02:34 Jhāna and soulmaking 01:20:12 Bypass and closing questions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60872/

item.title: Q & A, and Short Talk
item.description:
This talk moves from a practical question about working with emotions in jhana practice into a wide-ranging set of clarifications about intention, perception, and emptiness. Rob Burbea keeps returning to the difference between what is skillful in a retreat setting and what belongs in the wider arc of practice and life, with repeated emphasis on context, lightness, and not getting locked into a narrow goal. Later he expands into jhana mastery, bodily and emotional “locks,” and the deeper point that perception itself is dependently arisen and empty. The talk ends with a quieter note on how jhana factors can spill into ordinary life, without making happiness or release into a small trick of comfort management.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 04:12 Context and intention 10:14 Tears and openness 15:26 What radical means 20:43 Steady practice and warning signs 41:58 Jhana mastery and views 01:05:30 Momentum and hidden locks 01:17:10 Pain and perception 01:21:14 Limits of bliss-making 01:34:14 Happiness beyond the cushion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60887/

item.title: The Third Jhana
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the third jhana as a state of refined peacefulness, moving carefully between canonical description, lived experience, and practical guidance. He contrasts the second and third jhanas through satisfaction, the fading of rapture, and the body being pervaded by a cool, tender stillness. The talk also widens into dependent arising and the way perception shapes the world, using that to connect jhana practice with insight and liberation.
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60891/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea works through how to relate to ringing in the ears, first as something not to fight, then as a possible object for concentration or insight. From there he turns to subtlety, pīti, and the role of primary and secondary nimittas, before widening the discussion to guilt around happiness, service, and the need for nourishment in practice. The talk closes with a broader frame: jhana as a way to open the whole range of experience, and with a practical exercise for tracing hindrances before they harden into papañca.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:42 Ringing and aversion 05:22 Sound as concentration 09:34 Sound as insight 14:42 Subtlety and refinement 25:01 Questions from practice 33:10 Joy and service 48:14 Perception and emptiness 53:07 The bandwidth of happiness 01:10:08 Hindrances and spaciousness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60874/

item.title: The Second Jhana
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a careful reading of the Buddha’s description of the second jhāna, using the talk to distinguish pīti from sukha, and to show why refinement matters more than simple stillness. He spends time on practical ways the state develops, including how attention, body sense, and emotional tone shift as practice matures. Later he widens the frame to address attachment, wrong view, and why jhanic pleasure can support rather than hinder the path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:15 Reading the सूत्र 03:18 What samadhi means 06:31 Lake and spring simile 12:29 Refinement, not force 21:19 Pīti and sukha 25:23 How it matures 28:42 Working with pīti 38:39 Thought and subtlety 46:55 What jhāna gives
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60880/

item.title: Q & A, and Short Talk
item.description:
Rob Burbea answers questions from a retreat on jhāna practice, moving through the feel of tuning attention, working with pīti, and finding the right balance between concentration and enjoyment. He repeatedly reframes practice away from force and toward receptive, responsive attention, using analogies of radio tuning, listening, and drinking from a spring. The talk closes with practical guidance for retreat life: openness with others, steadiness of intention, and reassurance about common meditation experiences.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the questions 03:03 Tuning attention 06:39 Receiver and refinement 09:17 Enjoyment before control 15:35 Not snatching at pīti 20:19 Delighting in happiness 26:14 Daily life and familiarity 32:18 First jhāna in context 42:24 Openness and restraint
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60873/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
This Q&A stays close to the practical side of jhana practice: how to work with hindrances, over-efforting, fear, and the pull toward more ease. Rob Burbea keeps returning to the same quiet refrain that difficult states are normal, not personal failures, and that skill often means softening, backing off, or changing relationship rather than forcing progress. Later questions move into the texture of second and third jhana, equanimity, and the way excitement, creativity, and subtle bodily feeling can either support or interrupt practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Q&A 04:06 Working with effort 14:46 Fear and softening 20:34 Second jhana clarity 28:09 Third jhana territory 30:37 Mapping the later states 41:12 Relationship shapes experience 52:48 Thought, noise, and drifting 01:05:01 Excitement, creativity, and intention
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60875/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
This Q&A stays close to the practical side of jhana practice: how to work with hindrances, over-efforting, fear, and the pull toward more ease. Rob Burbea keeps returning to the same quiet refrain that difficult states are normal, not personal failures, and that skill often means softening, backing off, or changing relationship rather than forcing progress. Later questions move into the texture of second and third jhana, equanimity, and the way excitement, creativity, and subtle bodily feeling can either support or interrupt practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Q&A 04:06 Working with effort 14:46 Fear and softening 20:34 Second jhana clarity 28:09 Third jhana territory 30:37 Mapping the later states 41:12 Relationship shapes experience 52:48 Thought, noise, and drifting 01:05:01 Excitement, creativity, and intention
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60875/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea clarifies how pīti, sukha, and ekaggatā function in jhāna, stressing that the most useful map is often a practical one rather than a rigid doctrinal one. He also distinguishes between reading an experience as jhāna or as imaginal/soulmaking practice, and then turns to how images, attention, and energy can support or distract from pīti. The talk stays close to lived meditation experience, with repeated emphasis on what is primary in the moment, what needs subtlety, and when to rest or change emphasis.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Pīti and sukha 01:40 One-pointedness in context 03:12 Absorption and direction 04:02 Jhāna or imaginal 05:54 Choosing the emphasis 08:59 Why maps stay partial 12:33 Energy and fatigue 14:17 Images with pīti 16:55 Soft pīti and waking up
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60876/

item.title: The First Jhana, and Playing and Working in (and out of) any Jhana
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a detailed exploration of first jhana as a bodily, suffusing experience of pīti and sukha, rather than a narrow point of concentration. He then connects that to practical questions of how to work with any jhana: how to sustain it, re-enter it, move in and out of it, and recognize when enjoyment, subtle attention, or rest is what matters most. Along the way he contrasts later Theravada interpretations with the Pali Canon, and uses those comparisons to open up a more flexible, less doctrinally fixed understanding of absorption and mastery.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:42 First Jhana Simile 08:47 Later Terms, Earlier Practice 12:08 Forget the Labels 15:40 Pīti as a Sign 19:32 What First Jhana Needs 27:05 Different Practice Grooves 34:51 Work, Play, and SASSIE 40:06 Malleability of Perception 53:02 Enjoyment and Adjustment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60882/

item.title: Developing Piti, Developing Focus, Developing Wellbeing
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores pīti as a physically felt pleasure that can arise either through concentration or through working more openly with the energy body. He keeps returning to the practical question of what to do when practice is pleasant, intense, blocked, or unstable: narrow attention, widen awareness, soften effort, or let perception change. The talk also broadens samādhi beyond technique, describing it as collectedness, integrity, and a kind of inner power that matters in ordinary life as much as on retreat.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Pīti Is 02:16 Forms of Pleasure 06:02 Pleasure and Practice 14:16 Breath and Subtlety 25:05 Collectedness and Integrity 33:15 Working with Constriction 40:03 Perception Attainments 50:29 Spreading and Settling Pīti 01:07:10 Equanimity and Awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60864/

item.title: Insight Ways of Looking, Other Energy Body Possibilities, and Summarized Instructions
item.description:
Rob Burbea lays out two broad ways of working: using insight as a deliberate way of looking, and experimenting with the energy body as a springboard for concentration. He then leads a series of short imaginative practices, showing how posture, attention, and subtle mental framing can change the felt body. The talk closes with a compact summary of how to choose a base practice, work within a learning edge, and refine concentration without forcing it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Insight as a way of looking 11:56 Using insight for practice 12:22 Energy body without breath 18:50 Opening and brightening 24:12 Lines and luminous forms 31:24 What the exercises show 42:09 Big-picture orientation 47:06 Simple concentration instructions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60868/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
In this short Q&A, Rob Burbea clarifies a set of closely related meditation capacities: sensitivity, attunement, responsiveness, and refinement. He then turns to how practice changes as objects become subtler, especially in samadhi and jhana, and how to work with piti, pain, and resolve without relying on brute force. The talk stays practical throughout, with repeated emphasis on relationship, perception, and knowing what stage of practice you are actually working toward.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:06 Four related capacities 03:47 Subtle object, subtle mind 06:07 Working with piti and pain 08:41 Resolve and letting be 11:08 What is the playground
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60877/

item.title: Attitude, Effort, Achievement, and View
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores effort, attitude, achievement, and view as practical questions in meditation rather than fixed doctrines. He argues for taking pressure off the body and the mind, widening the frame of practice, and paying more attention to the quality of attention than to whether a state has been “achieved.” The talk gradually moves toward a deeper rethinking of jhana as a matter of perception, fabrication, and emptiness, with brief references to samadhi, metta, imaginal practice, and traditional similes of balanced effort.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Frame 02:05 Pressure and Narrowness 05:17 Widening Practice 09:49 Goals Without Tightening 16:50 Quality of Attention 25:09 Imaginal Breath 28:55 Dropping the Snatch 39:58 Jhana, Eros, and Self 54:49 Perception and Emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60861/

item.title: A Hidden Treasure: The Relationship with the Hindrances
item.description:
Rob Burbea talks about the hindrances as something to work with closely, not as a sign that practice is failing. He moves from gross distractions to subtle forms of restlessness, dullness, aversion, and doubt, showing how different antidotes can be used within samadhi practice. The talk also widens into insight: hindrances as non-personal, as material for compassion, and as something that can eventually be seen more clearly without being believed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the Talk 02:49 Hindrances and Jhana 05:35 Antidotes, Not Indifference 07:52 Sleepiness and Restlessness 17:04 Sense Desire and Ill Will 22:17 Noise, Doubt, and Questions 27:39 Exchanging Self and Other 30:01 Subtle Hindrances 42:59 Patience and Insight
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60867/

item.title: Focusing on One Point (Intensity, Directionality, Subtlety) (Instructions)
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers practical instructions for working with a narrow meditation object without turning concentration into strain. He contrasts one-pointed effort with a more balanced approach: keeping whole-body awareness in the background, noticing over-efforting, and adjusting the quality of attention through subtlety, directionality, and intensity. The talk stays grounded in breath and jhana practice, but it repeatedly opens outward into a quieter, more delicate way of attending that can include delight and ease.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Context 02:35 Jhana Direction 08:48 A Narrower Spot 10:45 Samadhi Misconstrued 12:03 Whole Body Background 13:49 Effort As Art 17:02 Quality Over Quantity 18:28 Subtlety, Direction, Intensity 24:25 Delicacy And Delight
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60859/

item.title: An Introduction to the Jhanas
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers an introduction to the jhānas that starts with the Buddha’s own descriptions and then turns toward how to practice them in a useful, living way. He emphasizes that these states are not just about concentration or getting the labels right, but about well-being, sensitivity, perception, and the kind of change that can actually affect a life. The talk moves from the classical map of the eight jhānas to questions of mastery, timing, and how jhāna practice relates to insight, emptiness, and emotional development.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the territory 02:19 Buddha’s four form jhānas 09:38 The formless attainments 11:52 What really matters 24:26 Mastery and marination 33:45 Pitfalls and differing views 48:20 Why practice jhāna 55:04 Perception and emptiness 01:04:30 Walking, rhythm, and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60869/

item.title: Orienting to This Jhana Retreat
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this Jhana retreat by orienting participants to the schedule, the teaching style, and the deeper attitude he wants to support: openness rather than brittleness, and a view of practice shaped by happiness, appreciation, and responsiveness. He frames jhana as a long-term exploration, inviting each person to find their current “playground,” choose a suitable base practice, and stay attentive to what actually supports well-being and pīti. Later in the talk he widens the frame to include the hindrances, difficult emotions, grief, devotion, and the wider ethical container of the retreat, with short supporting sections from Kirsten Kratz and Sari Markkanen.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Welcome and setup 05:42 Open schedule, open mind 10:46 Listening and the playground 22:45 Base practice and pīti 28:31 Insight as support 39:50 Intention and inertia 49:45 Hindrances and emotions 58:35 Grief, devotion, resolve 01:13:42 Kirsten and Sari
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60865/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 9)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores ethics through the contrast between utilitarian consequence-calculation and a richer, virtue-based sense of moral life. He then turns to climate change and species loss, asking whether these call for a higher moral sensibility than concern with immediate suffering alone, and whether soulmaking belongs in that same register. The talk closes by returning to Hartmann’s idea that the higher values may be ‘useless’ in a practical sense while still being what gives life meaning, beauty, and depth.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Ethical frameworks 02:47 Limits of utility 07:26 Virtue and efficacy 10:19 Higher and lower values 17:17 Fear and moral depth 27:50 Species loss and care 39:24 Meaning and intention 46:36 Useless values 56:56 Foundations and beyondness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58781/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 8)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores ethics through the lens of soulmaking, arguing that moral life is not only about universal values but also about the particular callings of individual souls. He uses Hartman’s language to distinguish ordinary goodness from rarer forms such as nobility, radiant virtue, and a duty to one’s own ideal personality, then turns to hatred, powerlessness, and how blocked soul-making can harden into contraction. The talk closes by widening ethics again: toward love of the remote, responsibility to future generations, and a more open-ended view of the good life as a quest.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 05:04 Universal values and soul 16:31 Radiant virtue 32:42 Nobility and new values 42:26 Hatred and powerlessness 01:07:35 Polemic and courage 01:23:30 Love of the remote 01:35:18 Creative spirit and sacrifice 01:46:35 Narrative unity of a life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58780/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 7)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the way moral life is shaped by real tensions between values that can each matter, but cannot always be fully held together. Using Hartmann, Heraclitus, Plato, Aristotle, Mahayana ideas of the three kayas, and a long quoted passage from Jeffrey Kripal, he moves from simple oppositions into more difficult antinomies: harmony and conflict, nearest and remote, simplicity and complexity, and the limits of any single ethical scheme. The talk closes by returning to sila and to the idea that precepts are a necessary minimum, while fuller moral life still involves choice, partiality, and sometimes unavoidable guilt.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Simple oppositions 01:55 Hartmann and antinomies 10:21 Conflict as a value 17:26 Classical virtue and tragedy 26:50 Balancing practice qualities 30:03 Three kayas and beyondness 35:50 Absolutes and mysticism 53:21 Plural psyche, simple rules 01:09:44 Minimums and hard choices
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58785/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 6)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores whether moral values are subjective, culturally contingent, or in some sense objective, using Nikolai Hartmann as a guide and then testing those ideas against Dharma practice. He moves from ontology and conscience into free will, guilt, and the possibility of seeing self more flexibly, before opening into a subtler discussion of how virtues can require counterweights and syntheses. The talk closes by asking what it means to remain open to moral complexity without collapsing into flaccidity, relativism, or a rigid moralism.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Framing the inquiry 03:34 Hartmann on values 09:57 Culture and analogy 15:39 Practicing with ideas 21:41 Soul, self, and guilt 30:19 Freedom and moral being 39:42 The good as higher value 48:55 Conflicting virtues 01:03:06 Beyond easy answers
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58779/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 5)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how moral values can become part of soulmaking rather than remaining abstract rules. He moves from climate action and protest, through crisis, conflict, guilt, and remorse, into a more imaginal sense of values as living realities that can grip the heart and shape action. The talk closes by sketching several meditation approaches for working directly with values, ideals, and the sense of participating in something larger than the personal self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Values Become Alive 05:12 Climate Action and Protest 10:55 Crisis as Opportunity 16:31 Absence and Conflict 25:39 Plato and Divine Goodness 34:00 Virtue as Radiance 46:03 Mundus Imaginalis 01:11:48 Remorse and Sensitivity 01:35:37 Meditating on Ideals
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58778/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how values and virtues may be understood as real but non-physical, drawing on Plato, Hartmann, and Kierkegaard to ask what it means for an ideal to call for realization in lived ethics. He then turns toward soulmaking, showing how values can function like imaginal forms: beyond us, motivating devotion, and giving life depth even when we fall short of them. The talk closes with practical implications for guilt, healing, faith, and the possibility of orienting the heart toward what is trustworthy in moments of strain.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:59 Ontology of values 04:03 Participation and ideas 10:03 The ideal calls forth action 20:29 Mathematics as analogy 36:04 Values and transcendence 40:06 Metta near death 52:03 Dimensionality and soul 01:01:23 Faith beyond outcomes 01:06:56 Guilt, devotion, and healing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58777/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores ethics through the lens of soulmaking, asking whether moral virtues are only useful for liberation or whether they are good in themselves. He moves from a general account of values and virtues into a comparison between Buddhist paramitas, Hartmann’s ethics, and the modern emphasis on efficacy, then ends with examples of courage, activism, and self-reflexive concern for one’s own soul. The talk is reflective and conceptual, but it stays grounded in concrete questions about how to live a beautiful life when outcomes are uncertain.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 05:22 Values and virtues 11:27 Intention over result 20:23 Paramitas and release 28:52 Hartmann’s intrinsic good 40:05 Beauty, language, and soul 01:07:38 Modern shifts in virtue 01:24:26 Virtue over efficacy 01:41:01 Double intention
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58776/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how ethics, soulmaking, psychotherapy, and Buddhadharma shape what we notice, value, and act on. He argues that any framework has blind spots, and then uses examples from climate change, social justice, guilt, and ecological grief to ask what a fuller moral imagination might include. The talk gradually moves from critique toward imaginal practice, suggesting that images, virtues, and a more spacious sense of self and world can support a deeper ethical life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 04:12 Psychotherapy and Dharma 12:12 Frameworks and blind spots 18:35 An imaginal misreading 29:34 Conscience and tradition 48:35 Ethics, time, and future 01:04:58 Dharma and moral courage 01:16:16 Climate and responsibility 01:32:24 Imagining the suffering
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58775/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this first part of a talk on sīla by asking what ethics means in a modern Buddhist and Soulmaking context, and why the subject is necessarily unfinished. He contrasts inherited moral frameworks from Abrahamic religion, Western modernity, and Buddhism, then widens the frame to include climate, globalization, democracy, and the loss of moral education. In the second half, he turns toward Soulmaking Dharma, arguing that imaginal practice can deepen ethical sensibility rather than bypass it, while also acknowledging the real contradictions of moral life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the subject 03:28 Buddhist and Western frames 09:16 Precepts and simplification 13:38 Culture, guilt, and character 24:33 MacIntyre and modernity 37:30 Rorty, democracy, and power 54:33 Global ethics and moral education 01:17:58 Vinaya and meditation 01:36:46 Soulmaking and moral life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58774/

item.title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 3)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores how contemporary culture shapes the way Dharma is understood, especially the tension between modernist ideas of one truth and postmodern suspicion of universal claims. He then asks whether Buddha Dharma, and Soulmaking Dharma in particular, should be taken as universally true, or as frameworks that are partial, plural, and historically situated. The talk gradually shifts from critique of universality toward a more open view of Dharma as art, with room for borrowing, interpretation, and multiple valid stories about its place in the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Cultural context 05:58 What counts as universal 13:23 Gender and structure 17:32 Soulmaking and plurality 26:38 Borrowing across traditions 35:01 Postmodern limits 43:53 Dharma as art 51:39 Culture and spirit 58:03 Two ways to place it
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58769/

item.title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a close inquiry into how traditions are shaped by worldview, personal inclination, cultural pressure, and the kinds of tensions that keep a Dharma alive. He compares several broad cosmologies, especially different ways emptiness can be understood, and asks what each one allows or excludes in practice, ethics, beauty, and sacredness. The talk also returns to imaginal practice, suggesting that emptiness and the imaginal can support each other rather than stand in opposition.
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58768/

item.title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores what makes a tradition alive rather than merely preserved, focusing on Buddhist Dharma and the place of soulmaking practice within it. He moves from shared texts, values, and authority toward questions of mystery, cultural influence, interpretation, conflict, and creative mistakes. The talk keeps returning to a quiet challenge: how to stay faithful to what matters while allowing real development, difference, and dialogue.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 06:26 Mindfulness and tradition 26:52 What traditions share 35:13 Mystery and canonical depth 47:49 Tradition as exchange 01:02:01 Cultural conditioning 01:12:26 Mindfulness revised 01:25:10 Origins and history 01:41:44 Conflict as vitality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58767/

item.title: The Phoenix of Metaphysics (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how questions of ontology and epistemology are never as settled as they first appear, and how scientific, philosophical, and spiritual frameworks all rest on assumptions that can be questioned. He moves from critiques of positivism and scientific certainty into the imaginal middle way, then toward a more daring claim: that concepts and metaphysical ideas can be fruitful even when they are unprovable or later shown false. The talk closes by returning to soulmaking, arguing that inquiry can serve truth, but also beauty, orientation, and the deepening of the soul.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Framing the problem 08:42 Shrugging and default views 10:50 Imaginal middle way 17:31 Sacredness in emptiness 29:57 Higher truths and physics 47:41 Metaphysics in science 01:04:51 Fruitful falsehoods 01:22:20 Beauty as cause 01:53:53 Inquiry as soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58789/

item.title: The Phoenix of Metaphysics (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea traces how the classical scientific worldview came to feel like common sense, then shows how quantum physics and later philosophy unsettle that picture of objective, observer-independent reality. From there he turns toward the implications for emptiness, epistemology, and the question of how imaginal and soulmaking practices can be trusted without collapsing into either literalism or vagueness. The talk moves gradually from physics into philosophy, then into the practical problem of how to live with rigor, openness, and a more poetic way of knowing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the inquiry 06:17 Knowledge and social order 11:28 Classical worldview 25:28 Quantum fracture 34:52 What science reveals 54:43 Paradigms and truth 01:09:38 Truth, models, and realism 01:34:15 Deconstruction and assumptions 01:59:25 Epistemology as practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58788/

item.title: Soulmaking Dyad Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers detailed guidance for soulmaking practice in dyads and small groups, moving from the simplest forms of paired attention into more open imaginal and relational fields. He keeps returning to the need for skill, tenderness, and clear boundaries, especially where erotic charge, projection, or old wounds can quickly shape the encounter. The talk also develops the idea of temenos: a protected container, and even a "tail" of care after the formal practice ends.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the practice 03:59 Early exercises and scales 08:19 Why dyads are sensitive 14:06 Prerequisites for practice 20:04 Not rushing ahead 27:56 Attention, gaze, and metta 37:37 Temenos as container 49:09 After the encounter 01:03:49 Shared framework and stages
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58782/

item.title: Logoi and Leanings
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how practice is steered by subtle leanings rather than rigid formulas, especially when moving between imaginal practice, samadhi, emptiness, and the energy body. He spends much of the talk on the delicate fork between consolidating bliss and staying with imaginal depth, then broadens into pacing, balance, and the role of concepts in making practice fruitful. In the second half he turns more explicitly to logoi: how ideas can open perception, how they can also narrow it, and why even liberating experience may need the right conceptual frame to mature into insight.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the navigation 02:51 Subtle shifts in practice 09:09 The samadhi fork 18:53 Pacing and balance 26:16 Logos and causality 37:37 Energy body and ideas 48:10 Present and future causes 59:20 Water, time, and scripture 01:10:47 Concepts as support
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58772/

item.title: The Invitation of Otherness (Autonomy, Eros, and Intentionality)
item.description:
This talk explores how imaginal practice depends on two-ness, autonomy, and the living exchange between self and image. The teaching moves from clarifying what it means for an image to be other, to showing how eros, desire, and choice can deepen rather than disrupt practice. It ends by distinguishing a narrow wish for healing from a fuller intention for soul-making, where desire itself can become ensouled and more humane.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Imaginal relationship 03:25 Two-ness and otherness 06:24 Autonomy of the image 11:10 Essence and anchoring 17:54 Autonomy of self 25:51 Eros and wisdom 39:11 Being loved back 51:12 Desire and self-knowledge 01:16:10 Fullness of intention
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58784/

item.title: Dimensionality, Humility, Duty, and Love
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores several of the imaginal lattice elements, focusing on how they function in practice rather than as fixed definitions. He then turns to dimensionality, humility, duty, and love, showing how each can deepen the sense of soul, clarify responsibility, and change how a practitioner relates to self, others, and death. The talk draws on Henry Corbin and Nikolai Hartmann to frame imaginal love as a kind of perception and knowing, distinct from metta but often overlapping with it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:17 Purpose of the lattice 09:43 Two revisions 14:16 Dimensionality and depth 20:52 Anchors in depth 40:38 Humility and self 51:52 Prayer and desire 01:02:17 Duty and rightness 01:15:52 Particular love 01:33:28 Love as knowing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58770/

item.title: Daimon, Refracted
item.description:
This talk explores how a deep view of emptiness can open, rather than close down, the range of possible self-understandings. Rob Burbea then moves into the self becoming imaginal: how desire, inner criticism, creativity, and even the wish to make an imprint can be re-read through soulmaking rather than reduced to ego. He works through stories, references to Corbin, Hillman, Souriau, and others, and closes with a short meditation that brings the body, the voice, and the felt mystery of expression into awareness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Emptiness and self-views 05:45 Middle way, not nihilism 10:45 Soul’s balance and spread 19:54 Desire as entry point 25:44 The wounded blackbird 31:04 Images as shared qualities 47:07 Imprint, genius, and soul 01:00:11 Corbin, Souriau, and angels 01:13:08 The inner critic
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58766/

item.title: "Until All is Liquid" (Emptiness and Sensing with Soul)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how emptiness and imaginal practice support one another through the shared territory of loosening, liquefying, and less fabrication. He distinguishes the depth of unfabricating from the wider middle ground needed for sensing with soul, then returns to practical questions: when emptiness helps, when it becomes pushy, and how even partial fading can be fruitful. The talk moves through examples from illness, devotional lineage practice, tantric and Kabbalistic parallels, and careful distinctions about the imaginal middle way.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Until All Is Liquid 06:05 When Emptiness Helps 14:34 Curiosity Over Shoulding 23:38 Partial Fading, Real Insight 33:37 Ways of Looking 47:38 Empty Yet Soulful 01:06:12 Parallels Across Traditions 01:16:17 Emptiness in Daily Practice 01:26:50 Middle Ways and Flexibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58786/

item.title: Heat and the Material
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how emotions can either be pacified through mindfulness or become material for imaginal and soulmaking practice. He keeps returning to a central question: what kind of relationship with emotion allows image, eros, and a sense of the divine to emerge, and what kinds of habits block that process. The talk moves through caution about cultural assumptions, the alchemical crucible metaphor, and concrete examples of anger, power, grief, and inner stretch.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening cautions 05:46 Cultural assumptions 12:26 Emotional style and skill 21:58 The crucible of emotion 36:02 From mindfulness to image 51:53 Stretch and mutual dependence 01:03:40 Images and emotion 01:19:31 Wide fields of cause 01:32:51 Practice examples
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58771/

item.title: Opening the Sense of the Energy Body
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the energy body as a living field of awareness rather than a fixed thing, and shows how felt sense, image, and idea work together in practice. He then traces how opening the whole body to perception can deepen samadhi, support imaginal practice, and change how relationship is sensed. The talk gradually widens from breath and bodily attention into generosity, devotion, sexuality, and the imaginal dimension of experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 02:46 Three aspects of energy 09:43 Experimenting with form 14:05 A nose for essentials 16:57 Whole-body contact 25:31 Opening the field 33:38 Breath, generosity, and opening 48:07 Capacity and path 59:52 Energy as imaginal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58773/

item.title: The Crucible
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores imaginal practice through the image of an alchemical crucible, asking what conditions let images, meaning, and soulmaking actually take form. He emphasizes emotional relationship, vulnerability, and the need for both sensitivity and discernment, then moves through stories, scriptures, and personal examples to show how an image can deepen without being forced. The talk closes by widening the frame to receptivity, purity of vessel, and the idea that soulmaking is part art and part grace.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The crucible 02:57 Emotions and sustainment 11:12 Vulnerability in practice 21:02 Mary and Jacob 33:20 Images from life and art 47:27 How images are received 01:07:33 Art and grace 01:25:52 Finding the eros 01:38:39 Series, patience, and expression
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58783/

The talk closes by widening the frame to receptivity, purity of vessel, and the idea that soulmaking is part art and part grace.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The crucible 02:57 Emotions and sustainment 11:12 Vulnerability in practice 21:02 Mary and Jacob 33:20 Images from life and art 47:27 How images are received 01:07:33 Art and grace 01:25:52 Finding the eros 01:38:39 Series, patience, and expression
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58783/

item.title: The Way of Image
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces Soulmaking Dharma by laying out the talk's frame: what imaginal practice is, how it relates to ordinary Dharma, and why its philosophical assumptions need to be made clear. He then moves through the idea that foundations like mindfulness, samatha, metta, and emotional skill can themselves become roots, and that soul is both a mode of perception and a mode of desire. The second half turns to concrete examples of non-visual imaginal experience — music, death, sacred space, compassion, and healing presences — and ends by stressing that the imaginal is a spectrum, not a fixed achievement.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 06:57 Foundations become roots 11:40 What soul means 17:32 Dyad and boundaries 24:21 Imaginal middle way 31:32 Three kinds of image 44:30 Music as a god 01:00:46 Death, sacredness, land 01:09:10 Soft edges and refinement
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58787/

item.title: Closing Comments From Rob - Soulmaking and Sangha
item.description:
In these closing comments, Rob Burbea speaks gently about what to do after a Soulmaking retreat ends: how to care for the heart, what kinds of sharing may or may not be ready, and how to let the work settle without forcing it. He then broadens into a reflection on how imaginal practice has been carried through difficulty, illness, and teaching, and why the retreat’s energy can now be understood as something the participants help support. The talk ends with a quiet emphasis on assimilation, continued practice, and the kind of Sangha that takes real care to grow.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Thanks 01:23 Leaving Retreat Carefully 03:58 Sharing What Is Precious 07:20 The Gift of Dana 11:43 Imaginal Lineage 13:28 Assimilation and Development 15:43 Sangha and Friendship 17:11 Final Thanks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58609/

item.title: Image, Ethics, and Awakening (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how ethics, ritual, and imaginal practice belong together rather than being separate tracks. He responds to questions about precepts, ritual, sexual eros, the risks of practice, and whether soulmaking can support a wider ethical range without collapsing into complacency or intensity for its own sake. The talk closes by widening the frame to service, retreat, and the personal question of what a life actually wants to serve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 06:38 Opening Questions 10:23 Precepts as Basis 13:29 Ethics Deepens 22:42 Ritual and Sacredness 27:15 Imaginal Attention 38:56 Eros and Boundaries 58:15 Dark Night Reframed 01:07:34 The Sister Image 01:18:23 Service and the Path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58612/

item.title: Eros and Desire (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how twoness, eros, and imaginal practice shape one another: why relationship needs polarity, how eros differs from merger, and why particularity matters. He then turns to questions about eros for the self, the danger of craving, and how imaginal practice can widen into a sanctified sense of world rather than collapse into fantasy or self-absorption. The talk closes with practical advice on moving between imaginal and emptiness practices, and a short exchange on feminine figures such as Aphrodite, Tara, and Ragarati.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:30 Twoness and relationship 06:31 Eros needs polarity 13:08 Eros for the self 21:45 Imaginal practice and cosmos 37:16 Emptiness opens into image 43:14 Eros and craving 57:10 Body as beloved 01:00:26 Feminine figures of desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58611/

item.title: On Practising with Images (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores what it means for an image to become imaginal, especially when the self itself begins to feel like image. He moves between concrete practice advice and wider reflections on beauty, eros, art, shame, and the way imaginal experience can loosen fixed views of self and other. The talk repeatedly returns to a quiet caution: trust what arises, but keep refining your view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:57 A self seen as image 02:58 How image enters practice 06:27 Divinity, beauty, and culture 12:01 Repeated encounters matter 18:33 Trusting the process 24:12 Art, beauty, and violence 30:04 Boundaries and texture 43:01 Narrative and nectar 56:08 Relationship and desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58613/

item.title: Dukkha and Emotions in Soulmaking Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how dukkha and emotion can be met in Soulmaking practice without forcing a formula. He distinguishes between basic emotional repair, ordinary mindfulness approaches, and the more imaginal conditions that allow suffering to become a crucible for soul. The talk moves through anger, depression, ecological grief, and conflicting inner callings, returning again and again to patience, precision, and improvisation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 02:23 No formula for dukkha 05:48 Anger and hidden pain 08:22 Foundations first 12:02 Images after repair 14:17 Lattice and leverage 20:26 Crucible of emotion 28:20 Dharma and soul 35:50 Ecological grief and roles 48:21 Polytheistic psyche
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58610/

item.title: A Rite of Angels
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins with a short recollection practice on the Triple Gem and the ways practice is supported by lineage, teachers, and one’s own efforts. The talk then turns into a ritualized circle-walking exercise built around welcoming, metta, and an imaginal way of seeing others as angel or Buddha nature, held in chant and movement. Toward the end, he returns to the theme of blessing, describing how limitation, shame, and self-doubt can be met by a more spacious and merciful way of perception.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:02 Opening the ritual 02:15 Resting on support 11:42 Setting up the circle 19:19 Seeing the walker 27:00 Chant and form 36:40 Blessing and resonance 46:04 Angel gaze 59:54 Closing vision
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58614/

item.title: The Imaginal-Energy Body (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explains the “energy body” as a way of opening bodily experience beyond a narrow materialist frame, bringing together felt sensation, image, and idea. He returns again and again to practical questions: how to avoid overwhelm, how to read contraction and openness, and how attention itself shapes what is experienced. The talk gradually widens from basic definitions into soulmaking, relationship, and the connection between imaginal practice and jhana.
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58615/

item.title: This Retreat
item.description:
This opening talk sets the tone for a Soulmaking Dharma retreat by welcoming the full range of who is present: strengths, vulnerabilities, identities, and difficult material. It then turns to the practical shape of the retreat, explaining that the week is meant to be shaped by participants’ needs and long-term practice questions rather than a fixed curriculum. A major portion of the talk makes room for Rob Burbea’s illness and mortality, inviting the group to hold grief, love, and uncertainty as part of the retreat field, before moving into chant, paired witnessing, and a final imaginal practice of blessing the body, the room, one another, and the land.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:27 Welcoming the whole person 11:19 A retreat shaped together 18:26 Health, uncertainty, patience 21:29 Meeting mortality as practice 28:21 Compassion chant 33:34 Witnessing in small groups 42:12 Movement and blessing 01:00:08 Stillness and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58652/

item.title: Of Hunger and Humility
item.description:
In this short closing talk, Rob Burbea reflects on soulmaking as something natural rather than forced, while also emphasizing how easily practice can narrow or be diverted. He returns several times to the need for discernment: noticing what helps, what blocks, and how relationship and the wider field of practice shape what can unfold. The talk ends with a clear double instruction—be ravenous for Dharma, and be careful—framing hunger and humility as two wings of the path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:13 Opening Gratitude 02:05 Natural Soulmaking 05:17 Ranges And Openings 08:04 Careful Discernment 10:00 Beauty And Desire 11:57 The Relational Vessel 14:01 Insatiable Yet Careful 15:15 Hunger And Humility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51537/

item.title: Soul Making Dharma (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea clarifies the Soulmaking Dharma vocabulary around soul, Logos, and the create/discover dynamic, while keeping the concepts open rather than rigid. He also connects these ideas to awakening, dukkha, devotion, and the kinds of suffering that can come from soullessness or lack of meaning. The talk moves gradually from definitions into practice implications: how intention, receptivity, and imagination shape what becomes possible, and how energy-body work can be approached with care.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Questions 09:58 What Soul Means 11:15 Create and Discover 14:03 Concepts That Stay Loose 16:33 Awakening Expands 20:21 Dukkha of Soullessness 28:31 Logos and Devotion 31:50 Language and Practice 37:29 Energy and Caution
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51536/

item.title: Sharing Soul Sense (A Temenos Exercise)
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a relational exercise for experienced practitioners, beginning with grounding, metta, and simple awareness of the energy body. The practice then moves through sharing in pairs or groups, first around bodily experience, then emotions, and finally something that feels touched by soul. He returns again and again to the sense that speaking, witnessing, and being seen all change the field, and frames the whole exercise as a protected temenos held in kindness.
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51535/

item.title: The Spreading of Five Wings
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces five ways Soulmaking Dharma can widen in range: through the elements of the practice, through self-other-world-eros, through the eros-psyche-logos dynamic, through the spectrum of shadow and light, and through the texture of the energy body. He keeps returning to a simple question: where is the range already alive, and where is it still truncated by habit, fear, or preference? Near the end he adds a subtle point about how the self can become imaginal without needing to appear as a figure in an image.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Why Expansion Matters 01:55 The Five Domains 04:00 Self, Other, World 06:03 Eros and Logos 09:11 Dark and Light 11:20 Energy Body Texture 14:16 Imaginal Self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51534/

item.title: Sailing the Oceans of Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about how imaginal practice can be held without collapsing into emptiness, and how emptiness practice can be softened so images do not simply fade away. He moves through practical ways of working with attention, eros, discernment, movement, sound, and the energy body, while keeping the focus on how images become more alive, more subtle, or more universal. The talk also briefly touches on the Heart Sutra, jhana, Kuan Yin, and Jean-Luc Marion’s language of icon and idol as ways of clarifying the imaginal.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:49 Heart Sutra Reframed 01:36 Why Images Fade 03:35 Modulating the Fading 04:02 Eros Restores Form 05:06 Particulars and Fluidity 06:04 Form and Emptiness Edge 09:10 Universal Figures 14:42 Discernment Deepens Practice 21:33 Movement and Stillness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51532/

item.title: Wisdom and Desire
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores desire as a central force in shaping self, life, and world, and asks whether Dharma language has made that force too simple. He contrasts craving with eros, then develops a more nuanced picture in which imaginal perception, subtle letting go, and embodied practice can open desire into beauty, mystery, and sacredness. The talk moves from critique of reductionist views to a practical map of how desire can be worked with skillfully, without pretending it can be mastered completely.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Desire Shapes Everything 02:10 Beyond Simple Renunciation 03:58 A Wider Psychology 12:03 Practice and Soulmaking 19:29 Craving Into Eros 27:01 From Flatness to Imaginal Depth 35:41 Dependent Arising of Desire 40:39 Sacredness Beyond Objects 51:42 Closing Distinction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51531/

item.title: Sensitivity, Receptivity, and Respect in Soul Work (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice can be understood through the difference between narrative images and more timeless, iconic ones. He then turns to autonomy, receptivity, and respect: how not to force images, how to work with disappointment when intensity fades, and how eros and inspiration can deepen practice over time. The discussion widens into long-term devotion, the way life itself can become imaginal, and a reflective play with causality and participation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:14 Narrative and iconic images 06:58 The Dartmoor example 08:23 The concertina field 12:26 Intensity and jhāna 20:42 Experiment without judgment 25:43 Eros and inspiration 36:11 Devotion through difficulty 40:43 Life seen from beyond death 49:38 Autonomy and causality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51530/
