item.title: Voice, Movement, and the Possibilities of Soul (2)
item.description:
This talk is an extended set of movement and imaginal practice instructions, beginning with a practical choice about whether to stay in silence or keep exploring movement. Rob then leads a sequence of experiments with stomping, warrior-like posture, vocal hiss, subtle head movement, and pelvic rotation, each time asking what is helpful and what becomes perceptible in the body, emotions, energy, and image. Later he widens the frame to include the room, the earth, the stars, and finally a more explicit imaginal practice shaped by Catherine McGee’s framing of "sensing the soul."
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Choosing the practice 04:59 Rooted movement 08:47 Stomping and awareness 13:36 Warrior in space 18:43 Reciprocal space 24:27 Wider horizons 29:08 Voice and subtle motion 36:32 Earth and stars 45:42 Imaginal relationship
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51528/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
This talk is an extended set of movement and imaginal practice instructions, beginning with a practical choice about whether to stay in silence or keep exploring movement. Rob then leads a sequence of experiments with stomping, warrior-like posture, vocal hiss, subtle head movement, and pelvic rotation, each time asking what is helpful and what becomes perceptible in the body, emotions, energy, and image. Later he widens the frame to include the room, the earth, the stars, and finally a more explicit imaginal practice shaped by Catherine McGee’s framing of "sensing the soul."
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Choosing the practice 04:59 Rooted movement 08:47 Stomping and awareness 13:36 Warrior in space 18:43 Reciprocal space 24:27 Wider horizons 29:08 Voice and subtle motion 36:32 Earth and stars 45:42 Imaginal relationship
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51528/

item.title: Towards the Imaginal
item.description:
(Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
Location: Gaia House
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51523/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea

Location: Gaia House. Source: Dharma Seed.

item.title: Trust, Ethics, Image (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea отвечает on how to work with aggression and sexual eros without simply repressing them or acting them out. He frames the issue through Soulmaking Dharma, emphasizing healthy doubt, patience, trust, and the gradual opening of imaginal space so difficult energies can be held with more care and less pressure. The talk moves from the question of safety and ethics into examples from his own practice, then into the middle way of the imaginal: neither real nor not real, me and not me.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 The question of aggression 02:33 Healthy doubt 04:46 Beyond repression 09:23 Trusting the imaginal 12:24 Warrior and seduction 17:10 A question of navigation 21:16 Grace and receptivity 24:36 Images and ethics 28:39 Middle way of images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51520/

item.title: Voice, Movement, and the Possibilities of Soul (1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how movement, gesture, voice, and relationship can become part of Soulmaking practice. The talk moves from a framework for sensing movement as soul, through several playful embodied exercises, into pair work where witnessing, attention, and metta are used to notice how body and psyche shape each other. It is quiet and practical in tone, with repeated emphasis on sensitivity, consent, and doing only what is workable for the body.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:14 Opening the exploration 04:28 Why move this way 09:03 Finding your edge 13:34 First movement games 24:28 From gross to subtle 31:11 Small smile and hissing 41:42 Pair witnessing 53:29 Balance and metta
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51519/

item.title: Heart Art and an Introduction to Image
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins by extending energy-body practice into the work of emotion, distinguishing between easing difficulty, honoring it, and learning from it. He then turns to image, showing how imaginal experience can arise from dukkha, emptiness, memory, dream, or deliberate practice, and how the key measure is not visual clarity but whether the relationship is soul-making. The talk moves through several examples, including a deathbed-style relinquishment scene, a baby monkey on the chest, and an inward experience of being music, to show how suffering can remain present while taking on a different dimension.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:11 Two aims for the session 05:35 Samadhi and difficulty 11:27 Self, story, and healing 13:24 Image meets dukkha 22:36 Holy roots of feeling 28:30 Dynamism and movement 30:49 What image means 46:02 Not every image is imaginal 50:52 How to invite images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51518/

item.title: Growing the Energy Body (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea clarifies how the energy body can be approached through simple awareness, supportive qualities like peace or happiness, and the use of image. He then explores the practical question of when to stay with what already works and when to experiment, including the relationship between samadhi, imaginal practice, and soulmaking. Later questions widen the frame to collective practice and the subtle difference between ordinary energy-body work and the larger field of sacred, imaginal sensitivity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the inquiry 02:51 Qualities and tuning 05:28 Working at the edges 10:43 Staying or shifting 16:27 The imaginal fork 21:27 Collective field 26:08 Mysterious resonance 29:26 Wider sensitivity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51515/

item.title: The Energy Body (A Little Bit of What, Why, and How)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces the “energy body” as a way of attending to bodily experience as field, texture, vibration, and openness rather than as isolated sensations. He then shows how this sensitivity supports samadhi, metta, insight, and imaginal practice, while also warning against collapsing too quickly into fixed views of what the body or the practice “really” is. The talk moves from a short guided exploration into a wider discussion of why flexibility of perception matters, including a brief contrast with chakras and other systems.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:04 Introducing the energy body 02:11 What it feels like 06:12 Posture, space, breath 11:26 Light, metta, and emotion 16:06 Why it matters 20:56 Stillness and practice forks 25:46 A wider definition 39:30 Intention and range
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51513/

item.title: Opening Talk ('To Kiss a Falcon')
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this retreat by framing Soulmaking Dharma as a set of foundations: not a new system, but a distillation of skills, attitudes, and understandings already present in Buddhist practice. He then develops the idea of practice as an art shaped by beauty, discernment, and a willingness to question assumptions, including assumptions about truth and reality. A birthday poem, a brief Coltrane reference, and a reflection on Western culture help widen the talk toward belonging, ethics, and what it means to be human.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Foundations 03:56 Questioning Assumptions 06:39 Opening the Field 09:46 Art, Truth, and Reality 12:35 The Birthday Poem 16:51 Culture and Crisis 19:50 Discernment and Precision 25:18 Playing the Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51511/

item.title: Nothing Happens By Itself
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins with the simple refrain that nothing happens by itself, then unfolds how soulmaking depends on conditions: care, attention, and the right vessel. He introduces temenos as a protected sacred space, first in solitude and then in relationship, where images and the sensing of soul can be held and allowed to develop. The talk closes by turning this into an open question: what supports soulfulness, and what gets in the way?
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Nothing Happens Alone 01:48 The Temenos 03:01 Images and Samadhi 04:14 Soulmaking in Relationship 05:06 Creating the Vessel 06:38 Honoring What Is Precious 07:48 An Open Question
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50527/

item.title: Discernment in Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
This Q&A explores how Rob Burbea uses “imaginal” in relation to soulmaking, and how that language differs from Hillman, Corbin, and Jung. He then turns to practical questions about the active and receptive ends of image work, the role of eros and sexuality, and why ontology matters when practice begins to shift one’s sense of what is real. Throughout, he returns to a quiet criterion: what matters is not forcing a theory, but whether a way of seeing becomes more soulful, spacious, and alive.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 What Is Imaginal? 03:03 Genealogy of the Term 07:02 Image as Perception 17:01 Soulmaking and Skill 24:43 Active and Receptive 30:32 Autonomy and Trust 34:02 Eros and Sexuality 40:46 Horizontal Desire 46:52 Ontology and Reality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50525/

item.title: The Rhythms of Soulmaking and Elements of the Imaginal (23 - 28)
item.description:
Rob Burbea reflects on the rhythms of soulmaking: how imaginal practice comes and goes, depends on conditions, and is easily distorted by self-judgment or pressure. He widens the frame by placing imaginal work alongside metta, emptiness, energy-body care, desire, and letting go, then turns to several of the later nodes in the retreat notes, including meaningfulness, echoing, values, and participation. Near the end he uses examples like Aslan, music, and the world of the imaginal to show how an image can carry value beyond its form and how practice can be approached as an adventure rather than a demand.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the frame 00:57 Self-judgment and conditions 02:58 Pressure and the middle way 04:50 The palette of practice 06:15 Rhythms and blocks 12:45 Fullness of intention 17:12 Duty and its flavor 20:29 Meaning, echoes, and values 29:18 Participation and participation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50524/

item.title: A Temenos Exercise (in threes)
item.description:
This talk offers a structured pair and small-group exercise that begins with grounding in the body, then widens into sensing others, metta, and the shared field created by speaking and listening. It moves gradually from simple presence to a more imaginal and relational way of noticing what happens in the body, heart, mind, and between people. Near the end, Rob links the exercise to a Primordial Cosmic Buddha chant, emphasizing that practice can include the full range of experience, not only what feels pleasant or clear.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 01:05 Settling Into Ground 02:10 Opening the Energy Body 03:40 Sensing Self and Others 05:47 Metta in the Circle 08:36 First Round of Sharing 18:45 Dependent Origination in Dialogue 27:06 Second Round: Emotions 32:05 Reverberation and Soul 47:00 Primordial Buddha Chant
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50523/

item.title: Destiny, Autonomy, Love, and Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how duty, destiny, autonomy, and love can all arise within soulmaking rather than standing outside it as fixed facts. He distinguishes between a purely intellectual view and a participatory way of engaging an image, where what seems fated may actually be an offering that becomes meaningful through consent, trust, and attention. The talk then moves into darker and more delicate territory, including evil, fear, mental health, and the role of conceptual framework in shaping how images are experienced. It closes with a quieter exploration of loving and being loved by an image, and how that reciprocity can deepen the sense of personhood and mystery.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Question 03:29 Duty and Soulmaking 10:33 Destiny and Choice 17:48 Subtlety and Dark Images 24:59 Conceptual Framework 29:43 Loving and Being Loved 33:54 Personhood of the Image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50522/

item.title: Elements of the Imaginal (15 - 18)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a retreat teaching on the imaginal by introducing four more nodes in the lattice: non-reducibility, autonomy, twoness, and theatre. He emphasizes that soul-making is intermittent and should be met with a fluid mix of practices, not forced into one mode all day. The talk then moves into a careful critique of reductionism, a discussion of images as primary and autonomous, and a closing account of the imaginal as a middle way between real and not real.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and context 04:59 Grace as gift 06:02 Moving between practices 09:14 Nodes are interwoven 11:04 Against reductionism 15:17 Images as primary 17:58 Double autonomy 20:52 Twoness and eros 24:52 Theatre and middle way
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50520/

item.title: Soulmaking Rivers (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about God-language, divine imagery, and why certain words can clamp down on practice when they carry old religious wounds. He then widens the frame into a rigorous inquiry into fabrication, emptiness, and the possibility that imaginal practice, samatha, metta, and healing all belong in one larger field. The talk turns toward temenos, communal image-making, and examples such as Aslan and dissolving skeleton imagery to show how form can fade while particular soul-radiance remains.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 01:30 God-language and resistance 07:11 Mystery, value, and poetry 14:01 What is fabricated? 21:10 Temenos and shared image 25:40 Open image libraries 31:34 Soul-making and suffering 36:15 Paths toward the unfabricated 42:43 Images fading, essence remaining
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50519/

item.title: A Temenos Exercise (in fours)
item.description:
This talk offers a three-stage group exercise grounded in metta, bodily sensitivity, and imaginal witnessing. It begins by settling into the body and exchanging goodwill, then moves into shared rounds where each person expresses what is present in the energy body or emotions through words, sound, gesture, or movement. Toward the end, Rob widens the frame to the effects of speaking and listening, invites a softer imaginal view of the witnesses, and closes with a Pali blessing for all beings.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and Metta 05:35 Explaining the Choreography 12:18 First Round of Sharing 23:07 Second Stage: Emotional Expression 39:32 After-Effects and Resonance 45:51 Deepening the Witness 58:06 Return to Goodwill 01:01:08 Pali Blessing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50516/

Toward the end, Rob widens the frame to the effects of speaking and listening, invites a softer imaginal view of the witnesses, and closes with a Pali blessing for all beings.

item.title: Transformation in Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice, healing, and emptiness can fit together without reducing any one of them to the others. He contrasts a flattened reading of selflessness with a more dimensional view in which self-images, personality, and difficult emotions can all become part of soulmaking. The talk moves from the question of what is being healed, to the role of anger and grief, and then toward a working definition of soul as something flexible, relational, and not fully capturable by concepts.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:24 Images, healing, emptiness 02:34 What is being healed? 04:46 Divine uniqueness 08:27 Multiplicity and freedom 12:21 Emptiness and play 14:58 All directions are good 18:25 Anger, grief, image 25:30 Sharpness without cruelty 30:19 What soul means
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50515/

item.title: Soulful Movement and Elements of the Imaginal (6 - 10)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how soulmaking can unfold not only in stillness and silence, but also through movement, sounding, and subtle shifts in the body. He then turns to a set of imaginal “notes” or aspects—such as trust, dimensionality, divinity, and soft boundaries—showing how noticing or gently activating them can enliven an image and deepen practice. In the final portion he places this imaginal mode alongside more familiar Buddhist framings of emptiness and mindfulness, arguing that understanding fabrication can make room for soulful, skillful imagination.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Movement and silence 04:09 Opening practice wider 09:12 Seeds, not dogma 15:30 Trust and activation 20:16 Soulmaking and edges 26:18 Dimensionality and divinity 33:10 Three Dharma framings 37:37 Fabrication and freedom 42:57 Closing quiet
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50514/

He then turns to a set of imaginal “notes” or aspects—such as trust, dimensionality, divinity, and soft boundaries—showing how noticing or gently activating them can enliven an image and deepen practice. In the final portion he places this imaginal mode alongside more familiar Buddhist framings of emptiness and mindfulness, arguing that understanding fabrication can make room for soulful, skillful imagination.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Movement and silence 04:09 Opening practice wider 09:12 Seeds, not dogma 15:30 Trust and activation 20:16 Soulmaking and edges 26:18 Dimensionality and divinity 33:10 Three Dharma framings 37:37 Fabrication and freedom 42:57 Closing quiet
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50514/

item.title: Trusting in Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea speaks about soulmaking as a broad path that can involve samadhi, difficult emotions, desire, emptiness, the imaginal, and the body all at once. He answers questions about how different entrances into practice can open the energy body, how imaginal experience relates to two-ness and oneness, and why clarity and uncertainty both matter. The talk closes by framing soulmaking as something the soul naturally wants, while still allowing concepts to stay open and fertile.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the field 02:05 Language and energy 04:46 What can spark soulmaking 09:02 Broadening beyond one doorway 10:37 Two-ness in embodiment 17:54 Trusting and refining 19:00 Emptiness and imagination 23:20 Why it stays open 30:43 Clarity with fuzzy edges
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50512/

item.title: To Sense with Soul
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a brief clarification of what he means by “imaginal” by proposing the phrase “sensing with soul.” He contrasts this with the idea of imaginal experience as merely private inner imagery, and instead points to a way of perceiving ordinary things as filled out with extra dimensionality, richness, and aliveness. The talk then turns to the Buddha Dharma framework of the sixth sense, emptiness, and the way practice can cultivate the very instrument that senses soulfully.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Why the Term Matters 03:11 From Imaginal to Soulful 05:58 The World Filled Out 07:59 What Soul Means 09:28 Empty Words, Useful Language 10:57 Making the Instrument
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50511/

item.title: Elements of the Imaginal (1 - 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces the retreat’s use of “imaginal” by distinguishing it from ordinary fantasy, then from skillful but still ordinary uses of imagination in practice. He lays out the first set of features in a broader lattice of imaginal experience, emphasizing that the imaginal is relational, embodied, and not something to be treated as a rigid on/off category. The talk closes by showing how attention to a few nodes — especially the lattice itself and the energy body — can open the whole field in practice.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 02:58 Skillful imagination 05:16 What imaginal means 09:39 The first nodes 18:08 A constellation of qualities 23:57 Why the distinctions matter 27:42 Openings and entry points 30:12 Lattice and energy body
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50509/

item.title: Body and Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores the energy body as a broad, flexible field rather than a fixed technique or object. He traces how bodily resistance can be understood as clinging, how relaxing that clinging changes perception, and how different energetic experiences can all belong to one open range of practice. The talk then moves into the relationship between samadhi, the rupa jhanas, and soulmaking, including how body-based well-being, image, and meaning can support each other.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Q&A 01:32 Many Intelligences in the Body 04:06 Growing the Energy Body 06:51 Resistance as Clinging 09:27 Different Energetic Fields 15:12 Samadhi Direction 18:22 Jhana as Spectrum 22:26 Care and Navigation 25:08 Soulmaking, Image, and Opening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50508/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 8 - Talking with Trees)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a talk on imaginal practice by turning from body and earth to trees, asking what it might mean to sense a tree with soul rather than as a merely material object. He weaves together anecdotes, ethical practice, and philosophical reflection to show how reverence, speech, and love can change what is perceptible. The talk then widens into a meditation on values, art, and the difficulty of knowing what is true when perception is participatory and always incomplete.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Body, earth, and icon 05:57 Suzuki and paradise 08:07 How trees communicate 26:41 Speech and sensitivity 34:40 Reverence, culture, and doubt 49:40 Love sees more deeply 01:01:18 Eros, art, and knowing 01:23:09 Values and antinomies 01:46:44 Loneliness and the call
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50484/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 7 - The Scripture of Body and Earth)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea continues exploring how ideas, images, and emotions shape the way body and world are perceived. He moves from energy-body awareness into the body as imaginal, then broadens into hermeneutics, sacred sound, and the possibility of hearing the world as scripture. The talk ends by returning to praise, humility, and the healing quality of a more participatory way of listening.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Ideas and the Soul 05:04 Eros and the Imaginal Body 10:41 Playing With Body Images 17:34 Three Layers of Body 27:34 Hermeneutics of Existence 37:18 Retreat Report in Practice 50:57 Sound, Prayer, and Yoik 01:07:41 Bodies of the Buddha 01:21:55 Praise and Healing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50483/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 6 - Amitabha Buddha)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea returns to the role of ideas and images in soulmaking meditation, showing how a phrase can be held poetically until it opens real practice possibilities. He then turns to Amitabha Buddha, reading the name not only as limitless light but also as limitless appearance, and using that shift to explore imaginal perception, pure land practice, and the balance between emptiness and appearance. The talk moves through tantric deity yoga, Hakuin, and Kabbalistic readings of scripture to suggest that sacred text and sacred image can be lived as present, transfiguring ways of seeing rather than fixed doctrines.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:57 Poetic Hearing 10:38 Amitabha Reconsidered 16:10 Consort And Wisdom 22:17 Deity Yoga Stances 27:49 Pure Land Here Now 36:37 Balance With Akshobhya 46:20 Scripture As Living Land 59:19 Dharmadhatu And World 01:12:49 Yearning Opens Gates
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50482/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 5 - Bodhicitta)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores what people usually mean by “living life to the full,” and gently questions the modern assumptions hidden in that phrase. He moves from a retreat walk story into a wider critique of flat mindfulness, then opens the discussion toward meaning, image, value, bodhicitta, and Buddha-nature. The talk ends by asking how these ideals become personal, embodied, and alive in a unique life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Living life fully? 01:53 Limits of modern mindfulness 07:16 Beyond flat presence 13:32 World and transcendence 19:43 Meaning, death, and beauty 28:40 Bodhicitta and image 38:29 Buddha-nature as matrix 48:27 Personal blossoming
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50481/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 4 - The Three Characteristics)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the three characteristics—dukkha, impermanence, and not-self—not as fixed doctrines, but as meditative ways of looking that can shape perception. He moves from dependent origination and attention to vedanā into a broader argument that Dharma concepts are skillful lenses, not final truths. The talk then opens toward emptiness, soulmaking, and Vajrayana, where deconstruction can eventually give rise to a very different sense of appearance, beauty, and sacredness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Ideas Become Ways of Looking 14:15 Dependent Origination Revisited 19:33 Working with Vedanā 25:11 Relative Truth and History 29:56 Three Marks Introduced 34:12 Lenses, Not Doctrines 42:18 Turning Toward Their Opposites 50:44 Perception as Practice 01:01:15 Deconstruction and Reconstruction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50480/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how ideas about truth, knowledge, and reality shape meditation practice, drawing on Nietzsche, Isaiah Berlin, Foucault, and others to question reductionist readings of emptiness. He contrasts deconstructive practices that loosen attachment with imaginal and soulmaking approaches that open beauty, emotion, and subjectivity rather than flatten them. The talk steadily asks what kind of seeing practice is meant to cultivate, and what kind of life it is meant to serve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Pluralism and perspective 03:07 Knowing is interpretive 12:18 Practice and emotional scope 17:52 Reductionism in meditation 23:17 Beauty, soul, and humility 29:45 Power behind ideas 40:09 Truth and care of self 47:55 Opening the maps 55:09 Space for soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50479/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a wide-ranging inquiry into how meditation, meaning, and worldview are shaped by the ideas we inherit and the ways we look. Drawing on Husserl, Heidegger, Rorty, Quine, Vico, and Midrash, he questions the assumption of a single objective reality and argues that mindfulness itself always carries metaphysical commitments. The talk moves toward a quieter but sharper claim: interpretation is not detached analysis, but something lived, participatory, and bound up with what truly matters to us.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why ideas matter 05:24 Scientific inheritance 10:07 Husserl and crisis 13:10 The life-world 17:49 Apperances and reduction 35:37 Ways of looking 59:13 No raw data 01:20:39 Plurality and participation 01:30:18 Interpretation must matter
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50478/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how ideas shape perception, practice, and even what feels possible in meditation. He moves from the cultural dominance of scientific materialism into a more nuanced account of imaginal perception, hermeneutics, and the role of emotion, value, and eros in knowing. The talk is part philosophical framing, part invitation to notice how assumptions can narrow or open the field of experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Grounding Matters 06:17 Ideas in Experience 12:01 Eidos and Interpretation 20:18 Science and Reality 32:04 Pauli, Jung, Symbols 40:15 Abstraction and Reduction 51:05 The Moon Story 59:00 Epistemicide and Value 01:14:33 Dharma and Eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50477/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores awakening as an open-ended, soulmaking process rather than a fixed endpoint. He moves between emptiness, fabrication, eros, imagination, and conceptual frameworks, asking how different views of awakening shape the path itself. The talk also questions common myths of sudden enlightenment and closes with a rereading of Genesis as a call to keep leaving stagnation for wider possibility.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Fabrication and looking 03:30 A possible awakening arc 07:15 Why linear maps fail 10:05 Soulmaking joins in 16:47 The myth of sudden awakening 19:55 Freedom in wider domains 25:38 Concepts must be rebuilt 39:45 Idols and icons 54:02 Go from here
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50503/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea questions the usual desire to fix a single, historical definition of awakening and instead asks what different views of awakening produce in practice. He moves through ethics, tradition, genealogy, and the cultural shaping of Dharma, then sketches a trajectory of awakening through fabrication, emptiness, and participatory knowing. In the final third, he turns personal, using a cancer diagnosis and later imaginal reflections on death to describe what actually brings freedom, equanimity, mystery, and a sense of sacredness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Questioning the Bar 05:21 Ethics and Awakening 15:11 What Awakening Means 16:53 Tradition and Genealogy 27:29 Western Dharma Questions 33:44 Fabrication and Emptiness 40:30 Open-Ended Awakening 48:15 Facing Death Personally 57:20 Doors and Gates
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50502/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea examines how different Buddhist traditions define awakening, and why he thinks the first task is not to settle the terminology by argument. He moves from a critique of easy readings of stream-entry to a close unpacking of the fetters, self-view, dependent origination, and the unfabricated. The talk gradually shifts from textual debate toward a more experiential question: what kind of practice and what kind of person does a given view of awakening actually produce?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Many views of awakening 05:20 Against easy definitions 11:18 The first fetters 23:56 Opening the Dharma eye 27:14 The unfabricated and emptiness 36:57 Cessation and discernment 48:25 How insight can move 58:16 Raising the bar 01:10:17 What a soul question asks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50501/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the question “what is awakening?” by first unsettling easy answers. He looks at how ideas of liberation are shaped by ego, authority, and cultural conditioning, then turns toward a more open, curiosity-driven understanding of practice. The talk moves from Buddhist doctrine and attainment language into a quieter invitation to examine what one actually wants, and whether awakening might be less a final status than an open-ended field of freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 05:17 Beyond the Usual Definition 16:24 Authority and Achievement 20:28 Kindness and the Inner Critic 28:00 Jhana and Motivation 38:06 What Do You Want? 51:46 Training and Rebirth 59:56 Happiness and Finality 01:07:25 Open-Ended Awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50500/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 8)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea continues exploring how dukkha and soulmaking shape each other, focusing on the question of what counts as a cause of suffering. He uses a student’s early images, the Persephone myth, and several psychological frameworks to open up the possibility that difficult or erotic images may be read not only as damage or pathology, but also as an initiation into deeper soul-life. The second half broadens into a careful argument for conceptual flexibility: different theories of causality, development, and truth may reveal different aspects of experience without any one of them exhausting what is real.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 05:09 The student example 15:37 Persephone and the underworld 19:41 Opening the lens 29:37 Causality as perception 42:43 Beyond the private past 50:27 Competing psychologies 01:02:35 Truth and theory 01:14:47 Plurality of frameworks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50492/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
In this talk, Rob Burbea continues exploring how dukkha and soulmaking shape each other, focusing on the question of what counts as a cause of suffering. He uses a student’s early images, the Persephone myth, and several psychological frameworks to open up the possibility that difficult or erotic images may be read not only as damage or pathology, but also as an initiation into deeper soul-life. The second half broadens into a careful argument for conceptual flexibility: different theories of causality, development, and truth may reveal different aspects of experience without any one of them exhausting what is real.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 05:09 The student example 15:37 Persephone and the underworld 19:41 Opening the lens 29:37 Causality as perception 42:43 Beyond the private past 50:27 Competing psychologies 01:02:35 Truth and theory 01:14:47 Plurality of frameworks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50492/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 7)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how sensing with soul can relieve and even reframe dukkha, not by explaining suffering away, but by opening imaginal depth, reverence, and relationship. He moves from the pain of being seen too narrowly into a broader claim: that soulmaking can re-enchant what feels flat, truncated, or depressed. In the second half he compares this view with Hegel, Kabbalistic language about the divine need, and several ways of reading the Four Noble Truths.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Imaginal relief 02:33 Being seen fully 07:57 Flatness and depression 12:52 Re-enchanting dukkha 19:45 Imaginal and divine 30:07 Hegel and the negative 42:20 Questions of why 57:37 Four Noble Truths revisited 01:13:02 Soulmaking as the worthwhile
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50491/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 6)
item.description:
This talk explores how dukkha can be met through imaginal perception rather than only through impermanence, acceptance, or analysis. Using his own illness as a sustained example, the speaker shows how emptiness, vulnerability, humility, and a fuller intention can open the way for suffering to be sensed with soul. He also cautions against facile spiritual ideas of perfection, and keeps returning to the ethical question of what a difficulty may be asking of us in life and action.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Dukkha Meets Image 02:26 Sensing With Soul 06:20 Tool Among Other Tools 14:31 Second Arrow And Intention 25:37 Perfection, Gift, And Assent 40:10 Mundus Imaginalis Opens 57:00 Illness As Threshold 01:12:02 Healing And Action 01:26:37 Image, Fantasy, And Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50490/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 5)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea continues developing the relationship between dukkha and imaginal practice, showing how being intimate with suffering can sometimes allow an image to arise that changes the whole feel of a situation. He distinguishes this from avoidance or forced visualization, and he keeps returning to the idea that the imaginal can bring harmony, clarity, and a sense of duty without erasing difficulty. The talk moves through examples from work, relationships, and teaching encounters, then closes by suggesting that some forms of pathology or fixation may be healed by uncovering an underlying imaginal image.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 From Dukkha to Image 04:03 Intimacy With Suffering 09:28 Alive in the Moment 12:28 Foucault as Spark 16:53 Situation, Duty, and Soul 25:57 Mutual Arising 29:52 Imaginal Communication 33:41 Eros and the Beloved 44:56 Image at the Core
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50489/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 4 - 'Mastering the Fire')
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores desire, eros, craving, and the conditions that let the soul-making path open without turning into dukkha. Using fire as a central image, he moves from discernment and authenticity into questions of intensity, discipline, containment, and the limits of control. The talk ends by widening the metaphor through ritual, social, and spiritual images of fire, then gently qualifies the whole project with the reminder that the soul’s fire will never be fully mastered.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the theme 02:34 Eros and craving 06:38 Four kinds of desire 15:26 Discernment and context 26:43 Authentic desire 33:20 Tolerance and intensity 46:05 Discipline and containment 01:07:45 Ritual and symbolic fire 01:30:24 Limits of mastery
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50488/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of imaginal practice by asking how realism, reification, and identification can quietly turn soulmaking into dukkha. He moves from common distortions around images and self-view into a more nuanced account of how images, eros, intention, and conceptual frameworks interact, including a personal reflection on shifting archetypal figures over time. In the second half, he opens into larger questions of healing, liberation, ontology, and whether imaginal practice is best understood as discovering truth, creating meaning, or participating in something more relational.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Realism and dukkha 07:20 Polarized swings 13:28 Creative loneliness 27:17 Images evolve 33:02 Images ask for images 38:10 Healing and liberation 45:17 Impossibility and duty 01:04:48 Full intention, less craving 01:31:47 Auden and ontology
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50487/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 2)
item.description:
In this second part of the talk, Rob Burbea focuses on how soulmaking and imaginal practice can be sustained without simply increasing dukkha. He emphasizes pacing, steadiness, and the need to balance opening with consolidation, including the roles of samadhi, metta, emptiness, mindfulness, and the erotic imaginal. The talk also addresses common fears: that these practices are dangerous, too fanciful, or spiritually destabilizing, and it returns again and again to discernment, realism, and care.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the path 03:40 Pacing and steadiness 07:10 Retreat balance 10:54 Two practice currents 14:27 Working with fear 21:22 Imaginal figures and autonomy 26:38 Breaking and rebuilding 31:42 Questioning and sexual imaginal 37:23 Darker images and realism
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50486/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how dukkha and soulmaking meet, focusing on how imaginal practice and skillful engagement with desire can relieve suffering without becoming forced or premature. He spends much of the talk on the foundations that make this work sustainable: emotional awareness, samadhi, emptiness, relational sensitivity, and the ability to work gently with self, image, and story. The talk gradually widens into questions of being seen, vulnerability, and the need for a sacred, careful relational field he calls temenos.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the subject 03:45 Why care is needed 09:59 Foundational skills 14:06 Sustainable practice 19:55 Working with emotion 47:25 Self, story, and view 01:02:08 Seven instructions revisited 01:09:53 Relationship and vulnerability 01:30:31 Temenos and sacred space
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50485/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 7)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how modern assumptions about knowledge shape what we allow ourselves to say about beauty, love, soul, and imaginal perception. He moves from ordinary examples like potatoes and measurement into the harder question of whether we can truly know value, whether through inner images, a tree, a loved one, or the wider imaginal field. The talk closes by widening the frame to ontology, epistemology, and participation, suggesting that soulmaking may depend on keeping these questions alive rather than closing them down.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the epistemic question 03:50 Values and modern skepticism 09:01 Inner images, outer knowing 13:30 Love, beauty, and judgment 19:23 ESP and imaginal contrast 27:14 Middle way in practice 32:56 Black cat metaphor 36:23 Culture and hidden assumptions 46:51 Participation and soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50499/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 6)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how modern ideas about reality have narrowed what counts as knowledge, especially around values, imagination, and sensing with soul. He traces a line from Galileo, Locke, Descartes, and later physics to show how scientific ways of knowing can become overextended, then turns to how religious and Dharma cultures can carry their own hidden hierarchies. The talk closes by showing, through a personal retreat example and a reflection on the body, how deeply our images and concepts shape perception.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Questions 03:02 Values in Imaginal Perception 12:15 Galileo to Descartes 17:30 How Cultures Rank Knowing 25:20 Modes of Being 36:22 Jung and the Prison of View 40:46 Literalism and Interpretation 50:53 Body, Fantasy, and Perception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50498/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 5)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea reviews the conditions that support sensing with soul and imaginal perception, then shifts into examples showing how that mode of seeing can arrive through relationship, touch, grief, beauty, and ordinary life. He emphasizes that the work is not about forcing technique, but about play, inquiry, humility, and a gradual openness to what is received from beyond oneself. The talk moves from personal anecdotes into a wider reflection on values, image, and the ways a life situation can become fully imaginal rather than merely a problem to solve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the review 03:41 Conditions for sensing 09:49 Grace and receptivity 14:51 A creative project opens 20:19 Pain re-seen 25:02 Nakedness and blessing 36:34 What people transmit 46:13 When fantasy becomes imaginal 01:04:46 Values and next steps
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50497/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of “sensing with soul,” looking at how imaginal practice matures as life, image, and sacredness begin to interpenetrate. He lays out four conditions that help experience open in this way, then illustrates them with examples from emptiness practice, ritual, and everyday eating. The talk moves from careful conceptual clarification into richly observed retreat stories, ending with a reflection on how intention can either support or narrow the imaginal.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 09:35 Four Conditions 12:21 Intimacy and Energy Body 15:38 How the Process Works 20:54 Vedana Becomes Holy 25:48 Tangerines and Gold 42:44 A Meal of Interdependence 01:03:51 Two Perceptions, One World
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50496/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 3)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea looks closely at how perception can shift into sensing with soul, and how ordinary experience becomes imaginal. He moves through the dynamics of change, the balance among Self, Other, and World, and the need at times to linger with the energy body, desire, or emotional life rather than treating them as distractions. Near the end, he returns to reification as a common source of difficulty, while also warning against becoming rigid or self-policing about it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 02:33 Soulmaking as dynamism 06:00 Different tempos of change 08:00 Self, Other, World 11:37 The energy body 15:11 Desire and eros 17:29 Habit or soul's call 23:39 Reification and looseness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50495/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues clarifying what he means by “sensing with soul,” focusing on how soulmaking works as both a receptive and an active process. He moves carefully through the roles of Eros, Psyche, and Logos, showing how image, longing, and concept feed each other and widen experience. The talk also turns to the value of conceptual understanding itself, including a brief Feynman story and a discussion of how finer discernment supports practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What sensing with soul means 05:13 Making and receiving 09:39 What soul names 14:23 Eros, Psyche, Logos 20:40 Beyond and transgression 28:05 Why frameworks matter 33:15 Agile thinking and Feynman 39:52 Undefinable but fertile
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50494/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces “sensing with soul” as a broader way of describing imaginal practice: not just inward image work, but a way of perceiving self, others, objects, and the world so they become meaningful, sacred, and alive. He distinguishes this from both ordinary sense contact and from modes that simplify experience through mindfulness or dissolve it into oneness, while insisting that the different modes can all be skillful in different contexts. The talk moves slowly from cosmopoiesis and soulmaking to examples from music, touch, and illness, and ends by tying sensing with soul back to mindfulness, discernment, reverence, and play.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:30 From Image to World 05:36 Cosmopoiesis and Oneness 10:40 Introducing Sensing with Soul 17:56 Why It Matters Most 25:29 Philosophy and Examples 41:17 Soul in All Senses 57:22 Four Modes of Contact 01:08:28 Mindfulness and Dependence 01:19:33 Reverence and Play
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50493/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 6)
item.description:
In this part of the Imaginal series, Rob Burbea revisits the basic aim of the teaching: to refine how the imaginal is noticed, amplified, and supported in practice. He moves through several qualities that help an image become more fully imaginal, including meaningfulness, timelessness, the imaginal middle way, and participation. The talk stays close to practice, returning again and again to how concepts, perception, and the way of looking shape what is experienced.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the Teaching 01:49 Amplifying the Image 05:58 Subtlety and Intention 10:31 Bodies of Light and Jewel 13:33 Meaningfulness and Devotion 17:01 Timelessness and Sacred Time 28:09 Holding Impermanence 32:27 Imaginal Middle Way 50:54 Way of Looking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50476/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 5)
item.description:
In this installment, Rob Burbea turns to what he calls fullness of intention, arguing that imaginal practice matures when it moves beyond self-improvement toward soul-making, divinity, and Buddha-nature. He then develops two linked themes: the autonomy of the imaginal other within dependent arising, and the way imaginal perception preserves two-ness rather than collapsing into oneness. In the later part of the talk, he connects indefinite boundaries, infinite echoing, meaning, and values, showing how images become fertile when they are not reduced to a single interpretation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:35 Fullness of Intention 18:07 Autonomy and Dependent Arising 23:18 Dartmoor Tentacles 27:45 Two-ness and Free Choice 35:00 Ethics and Freedom 40:26 Soft Edges of Being 52:03 Echoing and Resonance 01:05:11 Meaning and Values
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50475/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues unfolding imaginal practice by treating it as a constellation of elements rather than a single faculty. He moves through how different nodes can ignite the whole field, including eros, beauty, divinity, grace, reverence, humility, trust, and duty, while staying careful not to turn any of these into rigid doctrines. The talk keeps returning to a middle way: not reducing the imaginal to psychology or imagination, and not reifying it into a fixed metaphysics.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Imaginal constellation 05:51 Ignition and grace 11:44 Switches and dials 19:36 Elements of the field 27:54 Dimensionality and divinity 40:27 Grace, reverence, humility 50:30 Humility as image 01:09:35 Trust in the image 01:15:04 Duty to consolidate
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50474/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Rob Burbea continues unfolding imaginal practice by treating it as a constellation of elements rather than a single faculty. He moves through how different nodes can ignite the whole field, including eros, beauty, divinity, grace, reverence, humility, trust, and duty, while staying careful not to turn any of these into rigid doctrines. The talk keeps returning to a middle way: not reducing the imaginal to psychology or imagination, and not reifying it into a fixed metaphysics.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Imaginal constellation 05:51 Ignition and grace 11:44 Switches and dials 19:36 Elements of the field 27:54 Dimensionality and divinity 40:27 Grace, reverence, humility 50:30 Humility as image 01:09:35 Trust in the image 01:15:04 Duty to consolidate
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50474/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 3)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea continues refining the idea of the imaginal by stressing that it is relational rather than something any image or object possesses on its own. He develops the image of a lattice or constellation of linked factors—especially energy body awareness, soul-making, and love—showing how attention to one node can awaken the others. The talk stays practical throughout, balancing disciplined attention with a sense that imaginal life also involves grace, newness, and something not fully under technique.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Recap of the framework 06:07 Relational, not inherent 10:33 The imaginal lattice 19:10 Wiggling a node 26:53 What energy body means 37:17 Soul-making and jhana 43:18 New territory and play 52:22 Love as a key node
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50473/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues clarifying what he means by the imaginal: not just inner pictures, but a way of looking that softens fabrication and opens perception to meaning, beauty, and soul resonance. He distinguishes imaginal practice from helpful but non-imaginal uses of imagination, then names duty, trust, and selfless intention as part of the terrain. The talk then turns practical, with many ways into the imaginal, a long personal example, and guidance for noticing when ordinary life is already being seen imaginally.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Recapping the terrain 05:35 Duty and trust 09:11 Opening the gates 11:26 Ways into image 19:39 The black coat story 23:39 Vivid but not imaginal 30:36 Patient transformation 35:42 Technique and patience 41:31 Life already imaginal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50472/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a new short course on the imaginal by situating it as a clarification of earlier soulmaking teachings rather than a new system. He distinguishes ordinary imagination, papañca, metta imagery, and healing images from what he means by "imaginal," then introduces "sensing with soul" as an alternate way in. The talk ends by outlining a careful spectrum from ordinary imagination toward the fuller imaginal, with attention to the role of love, eros, beauty, autonomy, humility, grace, and soul-making.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Course framing 05:29 What imaginal means 11:57 Different practitioner entry points 15:39 Ontology and knowing 18:28 Imagination in practice 23:55 Naming the tradition 31:30 Core qualities of the imaginal 39:24 Why lists matter 47:34 A dynamic spectrum
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50471/

item.title: Shaping Vessels
item.description:
Rob Burbea closes a retreat by asking what actually supports practice once people leave the retreat container. He reflects on loneliness, vulnerability, and the difficulty of sharing what feels most alive, then explores vessels for practice: silent retreat, study, dyads, imaginal sharing, and careful speech. The talk gradually turns toward how intimacy and mutuality can sanctify experience and help practice keep growing in ordinary life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Thanks 00:41 Practice Beyond Retreat 02:27 Loneliness In Dharma 05:58 Vulnerability And Simplicity 07:55 Kinds Of Vessel 09:45 Temenos For Images 11:34 Right Speech Reconsidered 13:10 Closing Question
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43947/

item.title: Beauty and the Buddha
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores beauty as more than pleasant sense experience, tracing it from the Pali Canon and the fourth jhana into eros, soulmaking, imaginal perception, and divinity. He contrasts that wider field with views that reduce beauty to impermanence, tragedy, or mere craving, and argues that both emptiness practice and imaginal practice can open perception rather than narrow it. The talk moves quietly but insistently toward a nondual, participatory understanding of self, world, and sense experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 02:54 Beauty in the Canon 06:07 Sense Pleasures and Limits 08:54 Radiance and Vividness 15:02 Tragedy or More 21:09 Eros and Resonance 43:09 Emptiness and Dimensions 01:00:04 Divinity and Buddha Nature 01:17:30 Soulmaking Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43934/

item.title: Modes of Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
This Q&A explores how emptiness, samadhi, and imaginal practice can converge without collapsing into a reductive view. Rob Burbea argues that emptiness need not be deflationary; it can open into mystery, divinity, and a wider field of mystical perception. The conversation then moves through cosmopoiesis, the spread of imaginal qualities, the role of eros, and how this approach differs from more prescribed forms of Tantra.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 02:19 Emptiness and divinity 07:14 Samadhi and less fabrication 10:39 Cosmopoiesis explained 13:04 The tree example 16:52 Not making it happen 21:02 Trusting eros 23:42 Otherness and merging 26:14 Soulmaking and Tantra 28:59 Hillman and influence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43944/

item.title: Longing, Vulnerability, Anteros
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how longing and devotion can include vulnerability rather than bypassing it, using his own struggle with book-writing and a later creative project as examples. He introduces Anteros, the idea of reciprocal eros, to suggest that what we love also loves us back, and that devotion can be held by something larger than the self. The talk then moves into imaginal and deity practice, sacredness as something participated in, and a closing image of tangerines becoming sacrament.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 02:02 Book-writing as doorway 05:50 Anteros and reciprocity 07:24 Anxiety into image 14:59 Deity and vulnerability 17:07 Alchemy of desire 20:06 Devotion and image 24:23 Sacredness made visible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43943/

item.title: Awakening and Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about whether awakening depends on long retreats, sitting practice, or a particular kind of formal meditation. He broadens the frame to include walking, daily life, imagination, devotion, and the body, while also pushing back on rigid ideas about jhana, insight, and authority. The conversation then turns toward imaginal practice, eros, and how different starting points can open into soulmaking without claiming to be the final truth.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:55 Beyond the cushion 09:34 Jhana without dogma 13:52 The unfabricated nearby 15:52 Image into samadhi 24:35 Body and image 28:30 No single framework 30:07 Eros and devotion 37:19 Closing exchange
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43933/

item.title: On Blessed Ground: Fantasies of the Self on the Path
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how fantasy, image, and eros are not side effects of practice but part of its ground. He contrasts the familiar medical frame of Buddhism with other possible self-images on the path — researcher, artist, adventurer, lover — and asks which ones actually enliven practice. The talk closes by suggesting that a freer relationship to fantasy may open a deeper kind of liberation, one that is less realist and more soulmaking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 06:18 Medical model of practice 09:30 Religion and soul-making 13:27 Researcher and artist 16:39 Adventure and lover 19:50 Fantasy as ground 24:56 Reconstruction and freedom 27:48 What enlivens practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43945/

item.title: Visions of the Beyond
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins by asking what listeners love about the path and what they want from practice, then widens the inquiry to what kind of path-conception is actually soulmaking. From there he contrasts several ways of imagining awakening: the classical move toward the unfabricated, a flat one-dimensional chase for experience, a view shaped by resignation to life’s limits, and tantric or imaginal approaches in which beauty, divinity, and eros all have a place. The talk keeps returning to the question of whether our ideas of practice enlarge the soul or narrow it into mere coping or craving.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions 07:22 Culture and Self 12:32 Soulmaking Test 20:02 Eros and Beyond 29:26 Horizontal Craving 42:11 Four Path Imaginations 56:06 Tantra and Imaginal 01:04:40 Open-Ended Awakening 01:23:56 Questions to Carry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43949/

item.title: Energy Body and Image (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea treats the energy body not as a fixed inner thing, but as a way of sensing and relating that changes with attention, emotion, and concept. He moves from subtle-body distinctions into practical questions of how to work with restlessness, contraction, and breath, then into how imaginal perception arises and can be nourished without being reduced to a message. The closing discussion returns to samadhi, pīti, and the simple point that relationship conditions what experience becomes.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:16 Opening Question 03:18 Energy Body as Relation 07:00 Maps and Dependent Arising 10:06 Working with Restlessness 16:03 Meeting Drowning Sensations 19:45 How Imaginal Perception Arises 24:34 Loosening Conceptual Grip 40:23 Samadhi and Fabrication 43:27 Pīti and Relationship
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43936/

item.title: Opening to Desire and the Imaginal (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about working with the energy body, images, and the imaginal, starting from the simple problem of contraction around an image and widening into a broader map of practice. He then turns to desire, depression, the inner critic, and whether openness to imagery risks flooding, distinguishing worldly pain from imaginal experience and returning again and again to how eros moves with conditions. The talk closes by tracing how desire practice can shift into imaginal presence, rapture, or simple well-being, and by emphasizing subtlety, sensitivity, and the changing forks of practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:31 Contraction Around Image 01:57 Holding Both Experiences 04:11 Images and Soulmaking 06:08 Desire, Depression, Reification 13:02 Fear Of Flooding 17:27 Pain and Compassion 20:12 Opening To Desire 27:53 Finding The Deeper Wanting 33:59 Eros, Conditions, Subtlety
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43946/

item.title: Instructions: Opening to the Current of Desire
item.description:
(Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
Location: Gaia House
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43941/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea

The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some. Location: Gaia House. Source: Dharma Seed.

item.title: Clinging, Craving, Eros (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea distinguishes craving from eros and clinging, treating clinging as a spectrum ranging from obvious push-pull to very subtle contractions in perception. He then turns to practical questions: whether clinging is always unwholesome, how craving can be transformed through the energy body and imaginal practice, and how emptiness work can be balanced without forcing a permanent dissolved state. The talk closes with examples of working with anger and with images, showing how imaginal practice can become relational, creative, and alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Clinging and eros 06:07 Fabrication and suffering 10:10 Transforming craving 19:28 Emptiness without forcing 24:13 Soul and self images 25:49 Anger as alchemy 33:54 How to recognize imaginal 41:11 Relational images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43935/

item.title: Instructions: Letting Go
item.description:
Rob Burbea frames renunciation as one useful thread among several, alongside nourishment, emotional care, and imaginal practice. He then lays out four ways of working with desire: returning to what nourishes, attending to the feeling underneath craving, giving craving space, and reflecting on whether the object will really satisfy. The talk ends with a concrete exercise for mealtimes, using the lunch queue and the sensations of eating as a place to study craving, fear, and the mind’s constructions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Expanding the path 02:58 Renunciation as a thread 06:20 Return to nourishment 11:23 Care for hidden pain 13:42 Give craving space 17:37 Reflect on desire 19:50 Impermanence and dukkha 24:06 Eating as practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43940/

item.title: Eros and Equanimity in the Movements of Soul
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores eros as a force within soul-making: a desire for more contact, more intimacy, and more openness that can enliven imaginal practice when it is not constrained by rigid views or aversion. He then traces how eros, imaginal perception, and conceptual understanding can mutually fertilize one another, widening experience of self, other, and world. The talk closes by placing eros in conversation with equanimity, emphasizing balance, non-reification, and the middle way of seeing image as image.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Terms 02:51 What Soul-Making Means 10:08 The Shape of Eros 24:51 Eros-Psyche-Logos 33:45 Otherness and Beyondness 43:50 Self, World, and Eros 57:34 Staying Balanced 01:10:02 Eros and Equanimity 01:17:44 Seeing Image as Image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43937/

item.title: Faith in Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about staying with one imaginal image, widening to many images, and what it means to be faithful to a practice that is meant to stay alive and responsive. He frames imaginal work as relational rather than objectifying, and keeps returning to the role of eros, devotion, and the energy body in making an image more vivid or more real. The conversation also moves into the relationship between analysis and receptivity, and how soulmaking can include the intellect without reducing the image to a fixed interpretation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:47 One image, many ways 04:19 Manifold imaginal life 07:11 Eros in the path 10:24 Working with vague images 16:53 Intellect and image 21:13 Desire, autonomy, heart 24:51 Analysis as practice 27:17 From craving to eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43939/

The conversation also moves into the relationship between analysis and receptivity, and how soulmaking can include the intellect without reducing the image to a fixed interpretation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:47 One image, many ways 04:19 Manifold imaginal life 07:11 Eros in the path 10:24 Working with vague images 16:53 Intellect and image 21:13 Desire, autonomy, heart 24:51 Analysis as practice 27:17 From craving to eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43939/

item.title: The Problem of Desire
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this retreat talk by questioning the usual Buddhist habit of treating all desire as a problem. He distinguishes craving, clinging, and eros, then shows how a narrower view of practice can miss beauty, mystery, sexuality, and the deeper movements that support soulmaking. The talk moves from criticism of simplifications to a provisional definition of eros as a desire for deeper contact and opening, with emptiness and ways of looking as the background to that exploration.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 04:04 Culture and confusion 06:47 Wholesome and unwholesome 09:41 The path is not thin 16:30 Beyond relief 20:06 Three kinds of desire 25:49 Clinging and emptiness 36:55 Defining eros 50:59 Eros, path, and sacredness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43948/

item.title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea traces a path from mindfulness into a more expansive phenomenology of soul, where ways of looking shape what appears, and where less fabrication can open into oneness, emptiness, and divinity. He then turns to the imaginal and the role of eros, arguing that fantasy, image, and soulmaking are not side issues but part of how practice unfolds. The talk closes by bringing in Kabbalah, Christian, and tantric images to show a double movement between transcendence and immanence, while keeping the emphasis on direct experience rather than abstract doctrine.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Phenomenology of practice 04:26 Stages of divinity 09:31 Opening the imaginal 14:19 Eros and soulmaking 25:16 The self as image 33:15 Beauty and divine mind 48:57 Two movements of eros 01:04:57 Dependent origination and matter 01:19:17 Eternity and time
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40177/

item.title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how careful mindfulness can reveal the inevitability of eros, fantasy, and soul-making rather than trying to eliminate them. From there he traces how imaginal practice changes the way self, world, practice, and devotion are experienced, and how that can open questions about divinity, emptiness, and the limits of modern ideas about reality. The talk keeps returning to the tension between reducing suffering and nourishing soul, and argues for a more patient, phenomenological way of knowing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros and soul-making 04:30 What image reveals 10:13 Subtle fantasies in practice 17:55 Life as image 24:48 The Buddha as fantasy 32:14 Ways of looking 39:31 Pathos and suffering 46:52 Suffering and interpretation 59:20 What divinity means
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40176/

item.title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of desire by inviting listeners to question the assumptions that usually shape it: that desire comes from lack, that it is mainly craving, or that it must be simply let go. He develops a quieter alternative in which eros, imagery, and inquiry work together, sometimes turning frustration into spaciousness, peace, and soulmaking. The talk also revisits teacher-fantasy, boundaries, and the way desire can open into a wider sense of world rather than staying fixed on a single object.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Inquiry as invitation 02:11 Reframing desire 05:15 Eros and the imaginal 10:53 Peace without flattening 14:51 Teacher fantasy and soulmaking 23:09 Psyche loves itself 29:17 Desire beyond lack 53:41 Ways of looking 01:07:51 Alchemy of desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40183/

item.title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 1)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea revisits a practice for working with desire that begins by asking what desire really wants, then clearing away default Dharma assumptions about desire, and finally feeling the movement of desire in the body and energy body. He uses personal stories and teaching examples to show how desire can open into something more general and life-giving, rather than collapsing into craving or self-division. Later, he places the practice alongside emotional pain, dependent origination, and his broader work on eros and emptiness, while cautioning that the method is not the same as generic acceptance.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing the inquiry 02:12 Origin story 06:45 Three-step practice 17:32 Examples of craving 32:16 Desire becomes inquiry 40:02 Desire and lack 45:11 Experiment, don't flatten 01:02:36 Relation to imaginal practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40182/

item.title: Eros and the Brahmaviharas
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the relation between eros and the Brahmaviharas, carefully distinguishing erotic imaginal practice from mettā, compassion, joy, and equanimity while also showing where they overlap. He argues that eros adds desire, beauty, particularity, and complexity, whereas the Brahmaviharas tend toward universality, simplicity, and less fabrication. The talk then turns practical: how erotic imaginal practice can be joined with compassion, how that may open a more spacious and energized heart, and why receiving love can be difficult for many practitioners.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:39 Eros and imaginal love 08:12 Shared ground with the Brahmaviharas 13:26 How eros differs 19:47 Particularity and the modern self 26:31 Receiving love 35:15 Less and more fabrication 48:14 Combining practice 58:32 Eros as fuel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40170/

item.title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a wide-ranging exploration of how body, sexuality, and self are shaped by what he calls the soulmaking dynamic. He first pauses to define “shadow” as the blind spots created by any act of insight, then turns to the risk of reducing Buddhism to simplism and non-engagement. From there he develops a richer account of eros, imaginal perception, gender, and the body as something that can keep opening into new modes of knowing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Shadow of Insight 05:44 Sexuality and Complexity 09:11 Against Simplism 16:04 Gender and Opening 20:33 Body as Expressive Field 41:33 Erotic Perception 49:25 Freud and Polymorphous Eros 53:18 Infinite Modes of Knowing 01:03:35 Body, Soul, and View
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40186/

item.title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores sexuality through the lens of dependent origination, asking how views, assumptions, and cultural inheritances shape what sex seems to be. He then moves into a wider imaginal territory, using a long reading from the myth of Inanna and Dumuzi to suggest a more reverent, cosmic, and soulmaking understanding of eros. The talk closes by examining modern identity and the ways sexuality can become over-defined, while returning to a quiet question: what actually opens experience, and what contracts it?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Sex as a view 04:17 Reduction and reaction 10:21 Inherited moral frames 14:07 Questioning the binaries 22:46 Inanna and Dumuzi 29:24 Cosmic sexuality 41:16 Sex and soulmaking 53:21 Foucault and identity 01:07:12 Kindness in inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40185/

item.title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how our views of body, sensuality, sexuality, and beauty are shaped by hidden assumptions about moderation, health, and what counts as a legitimate spiritual path. He contrasts modern habits of flattening experience with a more open sense of eros, soulmaking, and the imaginal, where sensuality can be understood as dimensional rather than merely pleasant. The talk moves from religious longing and monastic renunciation into questions of art, nature, and ecology, asking what becomes possible when beauty is not reduced to a simple material or psychological function.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:08 Intensity and pathologizing 07:32 Merton and total commitment 11:31 Moderation and its limits 18:06 Eros and the senses 28:42 Three views of practice 52:53 Beauty, depth, and belonging 58:44 Practice styles and flatland 01:20:18 Fabrication and sacredness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40184/

item.title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how eros, imagination, and ritual can be read as central to spiritual life rather than peripheral or suspect. Moving through Buddhist tantra, Christian Sophia devotion, Gnostic and Hermetic texts, and Kabbalistic practice, he asks whether these traditions point toward a re-sanctified world in which perception itself becomes participatory and soulmaking. The talk keeps returning to the tension between literalism and a more dimensional, symbolic, and cosmopoetic way of looking. It closes by holding together transcendence and the sacredness of the world, with human longing as a thirst that the limited world alone cannot satisfy.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros at the Center 06:19 Origins or Telos 09:48 Tradition as Growth 14:28 Sophia and Imaginal Prayer 20:37 Ambiguous Sacred Sex 27:56 Three Tantric Readings 36:41 Sun, Moon, and Cosmos 44:06 Sex as Divine Mystery 55:50 Ritual, World, and Thirst
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40194/

item.title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues weaving together eros, emptiness, and imaginal practice, using tantric and Kabbalistic parallels to show how desire can either fabricate a constricted world or open into sacred, non-realist participation. He revisits the insight tradition as an evolving and selective inheritance, then reads a tantric verse about passion and Vajrasattva as a compact teaching on dependent origination, divine appearance, and the transformation of perception. The talk then turns to a long reflective example from imaginal practice, before broadening into Nietzsche, Vajrayana iconography, and mystical traditions that hold transcendence and immanence together.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Weaving the threads 02:08 Insight as an evolving tradition 07:44 Tantric verse and Vajrasattva 16:41 Desire and world-making 23:23 World rejection and transcendence 29:21 A living imaginal sequence 41:13 Inexhaustible eros 56:50 Nietzsche and the senses 01:07:13 Buddha mind and Kabbalah
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40193/
