item.title: In Love with the Way: Images of Path and of Self
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how practitioners image the path, awakening, and themselves in relation to tradition. He contrasts the usual medical-patient and religious fantasies of practice with the scientific researcher and artist fantasies, then opens the question of what is most authentic, fertile, and soulmaking. The talk moves toward a gentler inquiry: noticing where fantasy is alive, where it collapses into ego-measurement, and how different areas of practice may call for different ways of engaging.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:37 Eros and path fantasy 09:57 Medical model of practice 17:12 Bones need flesh 25:04 Researcher fantasy 37:59 Artist fantasy 47:50 Permission and scope 01:07:17 Five areas of practice 01:28:14 Open-ended path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40178/

item.title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores eros for the path: what it means to be genuinely drawn to practice, and how that desire shapes the way awakening is imagined. He contrasts a medical model of Dharma with scientistic, religious, and artistic models, then turns toward soulmaking as a more open-ended and personally fertile way to understand practice. The talk gradually widens from critique of attainment to a fuller account of beauty, mystery, and the movement between transcendence and immanence.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Eros and the path 04:07 The medical model 08:31 Tradition and replication 12:00 The artistic possibility 13:38 Open-ended practice 22:30 What the soul wants 34:45 Outgrowing fixed images 45:04 Immanence and transcendence 58:17 Icons, not idols
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40189/

item.title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 2)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea continues exploring eros in relation to the path, asking what kinds of awakening can actually support desire, imagination, and soulmaking. He contrasts deflationary models of practice with more expansive ones, moving through papancha, emptiness, Vajrayana imagery, and the way different views shape what feels possible on the path. The talk gradually widens from a Buddhist analysis of sense experience into a broader inquiry into psychology, culture, and the limits people place on eros.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:05 Pleasure and reduction 11:15 Papancha reconsidered 17:34 Cooking and devotion 22:49 Vajrayana and emptiness 29:40 Eros in the path 47:43 History and deflation 01:02:23 Fear and limitation 01:17:33 A larger path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40188/

item.title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 1)
item.description:
(Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
Location: Gaia House
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40187/

Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Location: Gaia House. Source: Dharma Seed.

item.title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a close examination of doubt, asking how it can become a workable instrument of discernment rather than a source of paralysis. He contrasts eros and craving, then extends the same kind of discrimination to rest, metta, imaginal practice, and jhana, showing how each can be present in ways that are either soulmaking or merely comforting. The talk keeps returning to emptiness and dependent origination as a basis for letting images, longing, and practice be taken seriously without being reified.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Practice as preparation 02:07 Eros or craving 05:46 Wise effort 09:58 Questioning and experiment 14:08 Imaginal autonomy 19:09 Not realism, but relation 32:27 Emptiness as basis 47:05 Rest and jhana 01:01:37 Longing and devotion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40169/

item.title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores doubt around erotic imaginal practice by asking whether it is escapism, wish fulfillment, or a deeper way of meeting life. He turns those questions into direct inquiry, distinguishes eros from craving, and traces how imaginal practice can change engagement, perception, and the sense of the world. The talk moves from conceptual discernment to personal examples, then toward a wider view of the imaginal as relational, creative, and alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the doubt 03:30 Image asks for translation 06:44 Non-engagement or depth 09:10 Fleeing reality revisited 11:57 Eros versus craving 19:03 Fire, risk, and unbinding 25:59 Doubt, suffering, and path 32:45 Personal movement in practice 49:01 Living relationship and infinity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40168/

item.title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea uses the analogy of musical hearing to ask whether the more imaginal dimensions of experience are “real,” or whether a better question is whether they are important and soul-making. From there he critiques flat cultural assumptions about science, realism, and mindfulness, and builds a case for a participatory view in which perception, mind, and world are not separate in the way modernity often assumes. The talk then widens into a careful sketch of soulmaking, Eros, and Logos, including implications for psychology, pathologies, developmental theory, and the limits of any conceptual framework.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Music and limited hearing 01:14 Real or soul-making 08:17 Culture and realism 18:15 Frameworks that support 26:02 Soulmaking and participation 34:47 Valid because valuable 46:41 Eros and development 59:27 Self, image, and pathology 01:10:33 Cracks in theory
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40181/

item.title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how any Dharma framework also shapes what we can notice, value, and become. He argues that a useful logos must support soulmaking: imagination, eros, freedom, beauty, and the capacity for frameworks to break open rather than harden into dogma. In the second half, he turns to interpretation itself, questioning claims of neutral access to the Pali Canon and tracing how modern, scientific, and secular assumptions can quietly narrow Dharma and erase other ways of knowing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What a logos must hold 04:00 Eros and interpretation 09:07 When Dharma narrows 11:58 Whose Pali Canon? 14:58 Scholarship and tradition 20:26 Origins and dialogue 26:29 Epistemicide in Dharma 31:57 Mindfulness is not enough 40:18 Training ways of looking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40180/

item.title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the role of logos, or conceptual framework, in Dharma practice: not as abstract theory, but as something that shapes navigation, balance, and the very texture of experience. He moves from trellis and tree metaphors to emptiness, fabrication, and ways of looking, then widens into soulmaking, embodiment, relationship, death, ecology, divinity, and the limits of modernist views. The talk ends by asking for a framework that can include plural ways of knowing without flattening them into a single dogma.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:34 Logos as support 05:19 Trellis and tree 12:00 Concept beyond thought 19:12 Fabrication and emptiness 23:51 What the frame must hold 36:33 History and conditioning 39:14 Modern self and soulmaking 44:44 Holy bodies and Christ 51:19 Plural knowing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40179/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 5)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a talk on balance in imaginal practice, focusing on how attention can become lopsided and how to restore fertility without suppressing experience. He distinguishes intensity from potency, then shows how sexual energy, erotic image, and the energy body can be worked with more skillfully. The talk closes by widening the map: shifts among desire, image, body, heart, head, and conceptual framework, and how a practice can be deliberately steered.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Balance in Practice 01:31 Heart and Image 04:31 Intensity and Potency 06:17 Sexual Energy 10:24 Union or Participation 15:55 Shifting the Emphasis 21:23 Degrees of Fabrication 23:16 Framework as Vessel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40199/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how desire can be worked with as part of imaginal practice rather than simply being suppressed or rejected. He moves from the question of balance into a series of examples: an unreachable beloved, a retreatant’s forest experience, and several ways of re-perceiving longing so that self, other, and world gain depth. The talk ends by contrasting an anatta reading of desire with ways of seeing eros as sacred, alive, and already participating in a larger divine or imaginal field.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Eros and balance 02:16 The unreachable beloved 07:34 Beyond 'let go' 16:11 A transforming story 22:49 Include the self 28:13 Forest and benefactors 36:07 Love in the imaginal 47:06 A balanced constellation 01:04:49 Desire, divinity, and time
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40198/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his discussion of mastery in practice by treating it as a fluid art of navigation rather than a fixed ideal. He moves through several ways of working with desire and craving: letting go, noticing vedanā and pressure, meeting underlying emotion with kindness, and skillfully redirecting toward nourishment or spaciousness. In the second half he turns to imaginal practice, looking at how to stay with erotic images, how to meet what feels hard to bear, and how shifting emphasis can open the practice without losing its tension.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Mastery as navigation 04:27 Why range matters 08:15 Letting desire pass 12:35 Vedanā and craving 17:26 Kindness for emotion 22:02 Subtle skill in practice 25:21 Skillful redirection 30:01 Opening desire's depth 40:35 Working with eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40197/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 2)
item.description:
In this second part of the talk, Rob Burbea continues exploring how to build a vessel that can hold eros and imaginal practice without being overwhelmed by them. He emphasizes samadhi as nourishment and balance rather than just concentration, then broadens the frame to include rootedness, spaciousness, strength, and inquiry as supports for practice. The talk closes by turning to mandalas and paired images such as Akshobhya, Amitabha, Ratnasambhava, and Amoghasiddhi as ways of bringing emptiness, feeling, and action into a living balance.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Picking up the thread 01:07 Samadhi beyond focus 03:43 Nourishment and balance 07:01 Why understanding matters 11:21 Rootedness and earth 13:53 Spacious equanimity 16:58 Strength, libido, vitality 22:00 Dynamic balance 25:29 Range and inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40196/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores two alchemical sayings as a way of thinking about eros, practice, and the conditions that support a fuller path. He returns again and again to the need for a vessel that is strong and flexible enough to hold intensity, and to the question of how to work with fire without either repressing it or being overwhelmed by it. The talk then widens into preliminaries for eros practice: ethics, emotional and energetic skill, trust, and a more nuanced relation to equanimity, freedom, and duty.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Two alchemical maxims 01:54 Eros needs a vessel 04:41 Elasticity and shattering 08:56 Fire and its dangers 15:59 Dionysus and dismemberment 20:44 Preliminaries for practice 37:34 Ethics and rootedness 42:34 Energetics and trust 48:28 Equanimity and duty
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40195/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 5 - Otherness and the Art of Disappearing)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of eros as part of soulmaking, contrasting it with older ideas of eros as a drive toward union. He argues that eros preserves otherness even while meditative practice can open real experiences of oneness, and then turns to the question of participation: how images, relationships, and Dharma itself become more alive when seen as co-created rather than fixed. The talk widens into questions of imaginal knowing, sacredness, and what it might mean for the Dharma to become endless rather than narrowly therapeutic or narrowly liberationist.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Eros and otherness 02:02 Oneness and its limits 06:45 Meditative dissolution 10:52 Eros keeps tension 15:38 The birth of us 23:18 Not the art of disappearing 27:28 Participation and sacredness 40:59 Ways of knowing 57:05 Endless Dharma
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40175/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 4)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores eros as a force that opens perception, interpretation, and soulmaking rather than simple desire. He moves from Plato and Kabbalistic images of infinite meaning into Buddhist language, then turns to the ways eros can be narrowed by literalism, greed, or reification. The talk closes with a careful account of containment: how boundaries and non-acting can become a temenos, a sacred vessel in which eros deepens into imaginal and spiritual creativity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros Opens Meaning 02:32 Infinite Interpretation 07:04 Buddha Nature Language 09:38 When Eros Narrows 12:29 Dream of the Door 17:17 Relationships And Limits 24:10 Icon, Idol, Concept 29:14 Temenos And Containment 35:36 James On Desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40174/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of eros, psyche, and logos by showing how soulmaking depends on ways of looking that leave room for depth, mystery, and further unfolding. He contrasts rigid materialist or reductionist views with less-fabricating contemplative perspectives, then turns to examples from sexuality, pornography, relationship, and difficulty to show how perception can either flatten experience or re-enchant it. The talk closes by returning to the practical question of how concepts can be lived, flexibly used, and allowed to open rather than close the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 02:46 Eros as open-ended 07:57 Three ways of looking 10:49 Mystery and depth 15:19 Eros and logos 21:43 When view flattens 28:31 Pornography and greed 33:56 Multidimensional soulmaking 41:14 Relationship and re-enchantment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40173/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 2)
item.description:
In this second talk on eros, Rob Burbea develops the idea that erotic life is not just about a subject wanting an object, but about a larger soul-making movement in which self, other, world, and eros co-arise. He moves from basic distinctions between eros and metta into examples from imaginal practice, sexual and non-sexual desire, and cosmopoiesis, showing how perception can open into beauty, divinity, and a living world. The talk ends by widening that frame further, suggesting that eros can be understood as a cosmogonic force that helps make worlds.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Trajectory 03:41 Dependent Co-Arising 08:50 Images That Expand 14:07 Eros and Metta 20:40 Desire and Interpretation 29:52 Cosmopoiesis Opens 35:43 Examples in Practice 46:42 Samadhi and Sacred Time 58:33 Expanding Sacredness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40172/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores eros as a soulmaking force, asking why erotic imagery and attraction are so central in imaginal practice. He develops eros as desire for contact with the beloved other, then widens the frame to include beauty, otherness, longing, and the way image, self, and world are co-fabricated. The talk moves from conceptual clarification to long, concrete examples, including subtle and overt erotic images, before returning to the practical question of how trust, guilt, or reduction shape what the image can become.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 17:22 Why eros appears 24:52 Desire for contact 44:57 Soul loves soul-making 59:17 Subtle erotic images 01:03:01 Samadhi and transformation 01:18:40 The dark goddess rite 01:33:33 Trust or collapse
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40171/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 5)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores Emerson’s “two handles” aphorism as a way of approaching desire, eros, emptiness, imagination, and delineation with care. The talk moves from the power and danger of concepts into a subtler argument: distinctions can mislead, but they can also open practice, refine perception, and support soul-making. He closes by stressing that this is not a closed system, but an open field meant to be extended and revised.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Two Handles 02:51 Eros As Fire 10:19 Diotima and Meaning 16:51 Limits of Understanding 29:29 Dangers of Delineation 44:28 When Distinctions Help 57:26 An Open Path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40167/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a careful argument for why Dharma needs a richer psychology than simple formulas about craving, pleasure, or self-protection. He distinguishes eros from craving, love, sex, and metta, and suggests that imaginal practice can open perception rather than solidify it. The talk moves from cultural assumptions and the need for direct inquiry into a more detailed account of the eros–psyche–logos dynamic, with examples from illness, pilgrimage, and earlier Dharma teachings.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Unfamiliar ideas 03:50 Fantasy and image 09:03 Holiday or pilgrimage 13:30 Find out for yourself 15:51 Eros in practice 21:17 Distinguishing love and eros 28:01 Eros and perception 31:59 Small and large eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40166/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens by placing this talk inside a much larger body of teaching on emptiness, dependent origination, imaginal practice, and re-enchantment, and then uses the first part of the session as a kind of glossary. He moves carefully through phenomenology, ways of looking, fabrication, emptiness, image, soul-making, logos, cosmopoiesis, theophany, divinity, and Buddha nature, showing how these terms relate to one another rather than treating them as fixed doctrines. The talk is especially concerned with how perception is shaped, how images become irreducible and meaningful, and how meditative experience can open into a more participatory and sacred sense of the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Entering the framework 05:33 Why these words matter 18:09 Phenomenology and looking 24:27 Emptiness and freedom 29:02 What counts as imaginal 38:00 Soul-making and eros 45:06 The eros-psyche-logos dynamic 58:55 Cosmopoiesis and theophany 01:06:53 Divinity and Buddha nature
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40165/

Rob Burbea opens by placing this talk inside a much larger body of teaching on emptiness, dependent origination, imaginal practice, and re-enchantment, and then uses the first part of the session as a kind of glossary. He moves carefully through phenomenology, ways of looking, fabrication, emptiness, image, soul-making, logos, cosmopoiesis, theophany, divinity, and Buddha nature, showing how these terms relate to one another rather than treating them as fixed doctrines. The talk is especially concerned with how perception is shaped, how images become irreducible and meaningful, and how meditative experience can open into a more participatory and sacred sense of the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Entering the framework 05:33 Why these words matter 18:09 Phenomenology and looking 24:27 Emptiness and freedom 29:02 What counts as imaginal 38:00 Soul-making and eros 45:06 The eros-psyche-logos dynamic 58:55 Cosmopoiesis and theophany 01:06:53 Divinity and Buddha nature
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40165/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins by situating eros within a larger practice framework, using the hermeneutic circle to explain why this term cannot be understood in isolation. He then offers a working definition of eros as a desire for more contact, intimacy, and knowing, while carefully distinguishing it from sexuality, pleasure-seeking, love, or simple bliss. The talk moves into the felt markers of eros—attraction, tension, juiciness, arousal, delight, and its link with imaginal practice and soulmaking—before ending with a distinction between bliss and the more relational delight of soulmaking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Why This Delineation 02:20 Definitions and Context 05:46 Hermeneutic Circle 10:12 Eros as Desire 15:56 The More and Otherness 24:10 Recognizing Eros 30:10 Juiciness and Arousal 37:03 Delight and Bliss
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40164/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins by situating the retreat as a careful inquiry into eros, desire, and their place within Dharma practice. He recounts a teachers’ meeting where the term proved surprisingly unclear, then uses that confusion to argue that eros needs a distinct and more precise delineation than desire, craving, or love. The talk moves from Buddhist and psychoanalytic references into a larger frame of soulmaking, sacredness, and a more flexible Dharma psychology that can hold both letting go and the creative force of desire.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Inquiry 03:40 Why Context Matters 10:02 Many Meanings of Eros 13:56 Desire on the Path 18:14 Previous Explorations 30:17 A Monk’s Dilemma 44:48 Eros Beyond Sex 52:50 Relationship and Practice 01:05:24 Toward a Fuller Psychology
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40163/

item.title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 3)
item.description:
In this third part of the talk, Rob Burbea returns to non-clinging by asking what the teaching is really for, and whether a simple ideal of “letting go” can hold the full range of human life. He moves through art, family, romantic love, politics, and spiritual practice, arguing that some forms of desire can’t be understood only as craving or delusion. The talk then widens into a distinction between ordinary craving, deep inquiry into emptiness, and the different movement he calls eros, which he describes as soulmaking and as opening a fuller sense of existence.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Returning to non-clinging 02:37 What the ideal leaves out 10:26 Concepts and Dharma fantasy 15:39 Why simple frames fail 22:48 Three directions of inquiry 27:41 What practice is for 37:09 Mindfulness and poetry 47:40 Eros and soulmaking 55:02 Two broad paths
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40192/

item.title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores non-clinging as a practical way of looking, not as a simple command to stop wanting. He traces how craving, clinging, self, objects, time, and even attention are fabricated together, and shows how practice can use those connections to lessen fabrication and open toward emptiness. The talk moves from ordinary meditative release into Mahayana questions about perception, appearance, and the limits of “just letting go.”
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:03 Craving and subtle dukkha 10:47 Self, world, and fabrication 18:04 Ways of looking 24:44 Spectrum of fabrication 30:56 Impermanence and the map 38:49 Empty appearances 46:35 Perception and Mahayana 54:52 Progressive letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40191/

item.title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea uses the language of craving, clinging, and non-clinging to question a too-simple view of liberation. He works from the Pali Canon and dependent origination, then shifts into ordinary examples like cigarettes, chocolate cake, pain, and the urge to check a phone, showing how desire, self-view, and suffering get bound together. The talk ends by opening toward a deeper possibility: practice can reveal not just less clinging, but a wider freedom in which the unfabricated and the relationship between appearance and non-appearance become part of the path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the context 04:36 Pali terms and fetters 13:59 Dependent origination map 16:52 Everyday craving examples 19:20 Anatomy of clinging 40:44 Simple letting go 52:56 Working with dukkha 01:04:04 Impermanence and its limits 01:11:32 Beyond simplism
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40190/

item.title: Tending the Flame
item.description:
In this closing talk from retreat, Rob Burbea turns toward what happens after silence ends: how to re-enter ordinary life with care for the heart, the energy body, and the people around us. He emphasizes Sangha, reading, listening, and conversation as ways of keeping practice alive, then broadens into the retreat’s imaginal themes, especially the question of realism, the middle way, and whether a view is helpful. The talk ends by placing imaginal work inside a wider, flexible “buffet” of practices and encouraging listeners to keep the fire, intelligence, and play of practice alive in daily life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Leaving Retreat Gently 02:29 Sharing With Care 04:58 Sangha Keeps the Fire 08:32 The Imaginal Middle Way 10:51 Check the Soul-Making 14:57 Practice Beyond Retreat 16:07 A Wider Practice Tapestry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37017/

item.title: This Fire, This Longing (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice lives beyond the cushion: in reflection, writing, creative action, subtle reverence, and the way an image colors ordinary life. He then turns to questions about healing, devotion, longing, and how practice can be held for oneself without losing sight of something larger. The talk moves on to image and idea, the place of artistic striving, and finally to the role of effort, non-striving, and energy body practice in deeper meditation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:46 Images Off Retreat 06:49 Healing and Devotion 11:59 From Mind to Heart 15:12 Image and Idea 23:32 Dukkha and Soulmaking 25:16 The Artist's Dilemma 29:02 Striving and Result 33:16 What Is Helpful 39:37 Energy Body and Responsibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37027/

item.title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 3)
item.description:
In this third part of The Gift and the Artifice of Self, Rob Burbea continues exploring how meaningfulness, enchantment, and selfhood are woven together. He moves from psychology and poetry into theology and practice, drawing on Einstein, Jung, Hammarskjöld, Nietzsche, Kabbalah, and Orthodox Christianity to suggest that our ways of seeing help create the world we inhabit. The talk keeps returning to a practical question: how do we translate expansive ideas about soul, divinity, and image into an actual way of looking, living, and loving one’s fate?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Meaning and the self 02:49 Images need embodiment 07:30 Yes to the more-than-human 11:37 Many roots, many worlds 16:53 Loving fate and divinity 23:50 Seeing truly 30:08 Creating and shaping God 36:15 Levels of self 41:31 Infinite soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37021/

item.title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 2)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores how the self, desire, suffering, and imagination can be re-enchanted rather than reduced to psychology alone. He moves through a careful distinction between emptiness practice and imaginal practice, arguing that both can be skillful ways of looking, and that each can loosen fixed views of self and dukkha. The talk also turns to causality, dreams, and archetypal images, asking whether our lives are shaped only by the past or also by future callings and timeless dimensions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Why disenchantment happens 04:14 What needs re-enchanting 08:33 Desire, eros, and legitimacy 17:24 Mind and emptiness 21:46 The dark and the good 26:07 Suffering and re-storying 36:49 Frames of causality 46:34 Dreams, future, and imaginal life 01:00:01 Artifice and soul-making
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37020/

item.title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea reflects on how modern Western culture, Buddhist teachings, and psychology can all narrow or confuse our sense of self. He contrasts reductionist views with traditions that hold a deeper sacredness in personhood, then argues for a flexible, imaginal way of understanding self and other that can make room for both freedom and difficulty. The talk moves toward a view in which suffering, personality, and mystery are all part of a fuller soul-making process.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Cultural confusion 03:11 Empty self, wary culture 06:43 Fantasy of origins 10:25 Re-enchanting self 14:16 Secular humanism 20:15 What cultures make room for 26:41 Sacred depth of person 37:13 Process and flexibility 42:04 Dukkha and soul-making
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37019/

item.title: The Imaginal and the Middle Way (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice can be held without sliding into belief or dismissal. He moves from encouragement about practicing in ordinary life, to the distinction between self-oriented practice and practice for something larger, and then into a wider middle way that holds multiple views, archetypes, and callings at once. The later questions turn toward renunciation, sacredness, and the role of darker imaginal material, with Rob emphasizing trust, flexibility, and a broader path that includes cooling, equanimity, and soul-making.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Q&A 02:40 Support off retreat 06:15 Practice and intention 09:34 A spectrum of views 15:32 Middle way and wobble 20:37 Renunciation as imaginal 28:39 Permission and practice 33:13 Cooling intense experience 38:35 Making the sacred
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37022/

item.title: Sensing Divinity
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores two modes of divinity: a universal sense that can arise through love, silence, awareness, and emptiness, and a more personal, imaginal divinity that retains personhood and particularity. He traces how these modes shape desire, eros, self-perception, and the way practice can re-enchant ordinary life without denying suffering or ethical responsibility. The second half turns into a paired exercise in metta and eye contact, bringing the teaching into direct relational practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Enchantment and divinity 01:51 Universal oneness 04:02 Desire and equanimity 06:50 Personal and imaginal 11:02 Re-enchanting the self 18:32 Perfection and surrender 25:27 Buddha nature and tantra 33:36 Divine fire in particulars 40:10 Metta and the gaze
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37016/

item.title: Creating the Path (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice can make the world feel sacred without collapsing into either rigid metaphysics or mere self-projection. He moves from Tara and other forms, to art and dependent arising, to sexuality, eros, and the ways an image can retain autonomy while still being part of practice. The talk closes with a sober reflection on risks, path-specific dangers, and the question of what one most deeply wants from Dharma life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:19 Imaginal forms 04:54 Art and dependence 06:37 Practice and sacredness 14:26 Cosmopoesis 18:49 Eros and otherness 23:33 Texture and autonomy 29:17 Emptiness and fluidity 32:37 Paths and dangers 40:50 What matters most
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37005/

item.title: From Inertia, Towards the Solutio (A Fragment)
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a short retreat talk on loosening the mind’s inertia through wider sensory awareness and a more imaginal way of perceiving. He connects the energy body to walking, standing, and the sense of a sacred landscape, suggesting that meditation can include prayerful relation to the world rather than only stillness. The talk moves gently from habitual perception to softness, liquidity, and the possibility of a more creative, soulful way of being.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the senses 01:10 Habit and inertia 02:21 Softening perception 03:31 Walking prayerfully 04:49 Body and self-consciousness 06:05 From loosening to creation 07:27 Returning to practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37028/

item.title: Matter, Bodies, Worlds (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a close, practice-oriented exploration of matter, bodies, and worlds, contrasting modern reductionism with older top-down cosmologies and the Buddhist emphasis on dependent origination. He keeps returning to a central question: how do our conceptions shape what we actually experience, and what becomes available when we loosen those assumptions? The talk moves from many-worlds ideas and Kabbalistic and Buddhist references into image, body, mandala, and alchemy, ending with a pragmatic reminder to ask what is helpful in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Bottom-Up Assumptions 08:16 Many Worlds, Many Traditions 14:08 Quantum and Mind-Matter 21:01 Mind-Made Body 33:40 Phenomenology and Plotinus 45:28 Energy Body and Alchemy 52:49 Mandala and Mantra 58:40 Time and Timelessness 01:08:55 Pragmatic Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37012/

item.title: Matter, Bodies, Worlds (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this talk by using a bird-garden story to question reductionist and scientific ways of seeing, then widens into how worldviews shape experience of self, body, and matter. He explores re-enchantment through imaginal practice, theophany, and a careful distinction between useful models and dogmatic claims of reality. Later he turns to Nietzsche, Horkheimer, and Adorno to examine scientism, measurement, and the cultural consequences of a mechanistic worldview, while briefly pointing back to emptiness and dependent origination as contemplative alternatives.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Birds and reductionism 03:53 Self and world together 09:46 Re-enchantment and devotion 18:43 Objects as theophanies 27:26 Matter, mater, origins 31:53 Mature imaginal vision 36:48 Nietzsche on scientism 45:24 Horkheimer, Adorno, and limits 53:19 Science, space, and openness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37011/

item.title: Neither 'Is' nor 'Is Not': Of Emptiness and the Imaginal (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how truth, emptiness, and imaginal practice relate without collapsing into either rigid realism or indifference. He uses climate change, Zen’s wartime history, art, and meaningfulness to show that some situations call for clear response while others can be entered imaginally. The talk then turns to the plurality of soul-voices and to different ways of working with images, including when an image is used for growth and when it is experienced as primary, alive, and world-forming.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Truth Demands Response 02:48 Ethics And Commitment 04:56 Climate Change And Fantasy 10:19 Art Into Emptiness 14:19 Ways Of Looking 17:15 Plural Souls 25:16 Images And Divinity 30:03 Cosmopoiesis
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37014/

item.title: Hearing All Sounds as Mantra: A Jewelline World
item.description:
Rob Burbea frames this talk as a practical experiment in imaginal practice: first he recaps several overlapping directions of meditation, then he leads a chanting meditation with Om Mani Padme Hum. From there the practice widens into listening—first to the mantra, then to all sounds, then to the felt world itself as something creative, sacred, and inclusive. The talk ends by emphasizing that these approaches are not formulas, but invitations to play, adapt, and discover what helps.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat directions 10:29 Mantra as experiment 15:53 Settling into chant 23:12 Spell and blessing 28:16 Imaginal listening widens 36:21 Walking as mandala 48:14 All sounds as mantra 53:32 Sound, meaning, and play
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37008/

item.title: Healing the Imagination (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores what it means to “follow what works” by trusting the felt sense of the energy body rather than fixed ideas about what practice should look like. He moves from moment-to-moment meditation choices into imaginal work, hand gestures, and the way images can settle, deepen, or ask for steadiness. Later, he broadens the frame into a careful discussion of soul and spirit, classical Buddhist purification, Tantra, and how different practices fit different temperaments and different times in a life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:33 What works in practice 01:55 Energy body as compass 04:26 Release and relief 06:21 Working with images 13:00 Gestures and posture 16:58 Tantra and emptiness 24:10 Soul, spirit, and fit 32:04 Metta as energy
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37007/

item.title: The Movement of Devotion (Live and Shortened Version)
item.description:
Rob Burbea outlines a devotional approach to imaginal practice, emphasizing openness rather than concentration as the guiding priority. He explains how lessening clinging, softening the heart, and allowing sensory blending can open imaginal experience and a more enchanted sense of the world. The talk then becomes more practical, moving through prayer, blessing, deity practice, and simple bodily gestures as ways of letting practice come alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the retreat 02:27 Openness over concentration 05:29 Experimenting with view 10:19 Flexibility and imaginal work 13:29 What opens the imaginal 16:46 The heart's posture 19:47 Divinity and devotion 25:19 Prayer, receiving, and union 31:30 Body, movement, and discernment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37023/

item.title: The Movement of Devotion (Part 2)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores prayer as more than supplication: as praise, blessing, devotion, surrender, and a way of sensing the world through the imaginal. He then turns to the felt relationship between body, energy body, and consciousness, offering careful experiments with posture, mudra, and subtle movement. The talk closes by asking when outer enactment helps and when a more inward devotion is the more alive practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Prayer beyond asking 02:40 Praise and blessing 07:58 Widening perception 15:19 Flavors of devotion 18:54 Body and samadhi 24:48 Subtle movement and mudra 30:29 Touching the earth 36:49 Inner or outer devotion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37025/

item.title: The Movement of Devotion (Part 1)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea revisits the retreat’s main directions of practice and argues that what matters most is not suppressing thought or forcing concentration, but the quality of relationship: with thinking, with attention, with the senses, and with the energy body. He then turns to imaginal practice, describing how less fabrication, a receptive heart, and a changed mode of perception can open into cosmopoetic experience. The talk ends by mapping devotional and tantric forms of imaginal practice, including examples from Avalokiteshvara, Yamantaka, and Eucharist as analogies for transubstantiation in perception.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Practice Directions 09:12 Beyond Concentration 15:45 Experimental Practice 23:00 Imaginal Opening 25:09 Four Supporting Factors 33:43 Humility and Surrender 40:16 Preliminaries and Devotion 43:57 Three Modes of Relation 51:28 Transubstantiated Perception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37024/

item.title: The Art of Perception
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how modern habits of knowing have narrowed the imaginal, and how emptiness can reopen perception as something creative, soulful, and alive. He moves from historical shifts in philosophy and religion into a practical account of image, body, heart, and meaning, arguing that perception is never neutral but always shaped. The talk ends by linking imagination, art, and Dharma practice to a broader freedom from literalism and modern disenchantment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The imaginal realm 03:54 Religion and reading 09:27 Knowledge shifts 14:41 Truth and play 19:11 Art of perception 30:40 What makes the imaginal 44:32 Importing meaning 55:04 Imagination as salvation 01:05:55 Questioning the familiar
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37018/

item.title: Energy and Transubstantiation: The Alchemy of the Body (Q & A)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea clarifies how to work with the energy body as a way of attending, not a fixed thing to find. He moves through pīti, samadhi, jhana, and then into imaginal practice, showing how pleasure, agitation, and appreciation can each be met differently. The talk ends by widening the frame to re-enchantment: perception as something creative, poetic, and responsive to the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:58 What the Energy Body Means 03:21 Getting Familiar 06:16 Awareness, Space, Core 09:03 What Is Pīti 12:03 Pleasure and Samadhi 20:51 Working With Jhana 23:41 Love Figure and Cosmopoiesis 30:14 Agitation and Care 41:04 Re-Enchantment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37006/

item.title: On Orientations in Practice (Live and Shortened version)
item.description:
Rob Burbea frames this retreat around the energy body as a practical sensibility rather than a fixed experience, and explains why it matters for samadhi, emotional life, and discernment in practice. He then turns to the habits we bring to Dharma—continuous mindfulness, narrow focus, atomistic attention, and hidden reality claims—and suggests replacing them with flexibility in how we look. The talk closes with concrete guidance for sitting and walking practice, emphasizing experiment, resonance, and what feels alive in the soul.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 01:21 What the energy body is 04:23 Why it matters 09:46 Listening beyond the box 13:47 Loosening entrenched habits 21:08 Flexibility and precision 25:40 Subtlety and soulfulness 32:29 Practice as art 40:02 Sitting and walking instructions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37015/

item.title: "I Sing The Body Electric": On Orientations in Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces the retreat by asking practitioners to emphasize the energy body: a subtler, more fertile mode of mindfulness that can support samadhi, metta, emotion work, imaginal practice, and insight. He then turns to five common Dharma assumptions he thinks can narrow practice, and contrasts them with flexibility, re-enchantment, and a more aesthetic, inventive way of looking. The talk closes by reframing the point of practice as loosening fixed views so that beauty, timelessness, and sacredness become more available in ordinary life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening: Energy Body 02:25 What Energy Body Means 08:31 Useful Across Practices 15:20 Body Electric 20:01 Walking in the Field 23:26 Five Crass Assumptions 36:53 Crass to Scars 42:45 Precision and Subtlety 54:31 Loosening Into Beauty
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37010/

item.title: Mindfulness and Myth
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how mindfulness changes when it is grounded not in hidden realism, but in emptiness, dependent origination, and the flexibility of ways of looking. He contrasts this with secular, psychological, and religious forms of realism, and argues for a wider, more plural field of practice that can include imaginal and re-enchanting approaches without losing rigor. The talk also offers practical guidance on how to listen to Dharma: for relevance, for the bigger picture, and for what may not fit existing conceptual boxes.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the spectrum 02:33 Personal framework 05:18 Realism everywhere 09:26 Secularism and disenchantment 18:25 Scientism and flat cosmos 26:36 Ways of looking 38:16 Emptiness as basis 01:05:31 Listening structurally 01:16:25 Teaching in chunks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37013/

item.title: Beyond the Search for Freedom
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores a question beneath the usual Dharma frame: is practice mainly about reducing suffering, or can it also be about beauty, sacredness, and soulmaking? He moves from a reflection on re-enchantment and the modern disenchanted worldview, through a close look at mindfulness as “coping,” into a broader vision of emptiness, meaningfulness, and open-ended freedom. The talk ends by turning to Jung for support in the idea that neither freedom nor self-realization is a finished project.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat frame 05:04 Matthieu Ricard question 09:25 Coping or opening 16:18 What practice is for 24:25 Buddhism and modern models 31:41 Self, God, and meaning 39:54 Priority reversal 48:03 Who practice serves 55:13 Jung and open-ended freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37003/

item.title: The Nature of Enchantment
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this retreat talk by defining what he means by re-enchantment and by laying out the assumptions the retreat will rely on: ways of looking, fabrication, emptiness, the imaginal, samatha, metta, and the energy body. He then uses a Guardian article about trees in cities as a foil for questioning modernist and scientistic habits of thought, arguing that real re-enchantment requires depth, dimensionality, and a subtler sensitivity to experience. The talk moves gradually from critique into a more spacious account of enchantment as poetic, imaginal, and open to different kinds of freedom, knowing, and soul-making.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat framing 03:42 Emptiness as basis 08:23 Trees and critique 14:54 Depth and scientism 20:03 Subtlety in perception 25:11 Kinds of enchantment 29:45 Imaginal and soul 36:51 Poetic language 44:29 Open frameworks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37026/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how love, eros, metta, and Dharma shape one another, with a strong focus on our view of the earth and the sacred. He looks at how self-image, Buddhist ideas about non-clinging, and cultural habits of materialism can narrow perception or activism, and he argues for a broader, more imaginal field. The talk closes by returning to ecology as a lived relationship between perception, love, emptiness, and action.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros and self-view 01:57 Berry and Confucius 04:20 Images of nobility 07:07 Duty and archetype 13:46 Dharma and engagement 18:40 The Four Noble Truths 24:49 Clinging and fabrication 30:27 Science and materialism 38:52 Two kinds of activism
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32198/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of an “ecology of love,” focusing on how eros, psyche, and logos can deepen one another rather than collapse into craving. He contrasts a one-dimensional materialist view with a more spacious way of knowing in which nature, the earth, and experience can be felt as richer, more dimensional, and even sacred. The talk also answers the traditional Dharma worry that eros is just greed, and closes by contrasting eros with metta as different but complementary modes of love.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros Opens Nature 01:55 Discovery and Creation 03:34 Against Naive Realism 05:52 Eros Or Greed 08:32 Limits to Growth 11:20 Infinite Inner Expansion 12:35 Travel, Fantasy, and Images 19:51 The Middle Sections 33:34 Metta, Eros, Mystery
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32200/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores love as two intertwined currents: metta, as intention that becomes action and can open into universality, and eros, as a desire to connect that keeps widening perception. He then turns to the way deep eros can make nature feel dimensional, imaginal, sacred, and alive with image, fantasy, and soulfulness. Along the way he contrasts horizontal ideas of interconnectedness with a more vertical sense of the divine expressed through matter and perception.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:20 Metta as intention 03:43 Universal love, equanimity 10:10 Eros and pothos 13:26 Boundaries opening 19:47 Nature becomes multidimensional 30:16 Spirit and matter 36:52 Stories from the garden 45:26 Horizontal and vertical 53:11 Poetic sensibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32201/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 1 - Introduction)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a talk on love, nature, and sacredness by asking what we actually mean when we say we love the earth. He gradually moves from beauty, wonder, and environmental concern into a wider claim: our views shape our love, and our love shapes what we can perceive and value. The talk then turns to modern secular culture, the eclipse of sacredness in public life, and the possibility that climate and ecological crisis ask for more than technical or political response.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening Questions 04:21 Beauty and Wonder 10:32 Sacredness in View 18:31 Modern Secular Culture 24:50 Climate as Values Crisis 30:06 What Activism Needs 37:27 Ecology of Love 44:21 Views Are Not Fixed
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32199/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 5)
item.description:
In this talk, Matthew Brensilver returns to the relationship between conception and perception, arguing that even the most ordinary experience already includes subtle fabrication. He then turns to the imaginal divine and cosmopoiesis, using personal examples from Christianity, Jewish mysticism, bird song, gardens, studying, and jazz to show how perception can open into sacredness without becoming rigid belief. The talk moves gently but insistently toward a wider range of seeing, where the world may be encountered as layered, expressive, and alive.
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31524/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
In this talk, Matthew Brensilver returns to the relationship between conception and perception, arguing that even the most ordinary experience already includes subtle fabrication. He then turns to the imaginal divine and cosmopoiesis, using personal examples from Christianity, Jewish mysticism, bird song, gardens, studying, and jazz to show how perception can open into sacredness without becoming rigid belief. The talk moves gently but insistently toward a wider range of seeing, where the world may be encountered as layered, expressive, and alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Perception and conceiving 02:16 Vague sacred concepts 07:04 Where God is found 10:19 Not one true divine 15:09 Jesus, mythos, and belief 21:15 Tzedakah and the gift 28:40 Theophany in sound 39:53 Sacred text and sacred world 51:26 Music, grief, and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31524/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 4)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice changes the way a person perceives self, others, and world, with special attention to the relationship between concepts and direct experience. He develops the idea of a “vertical spectrum of the imaginal,” where things can be seen as theophanies or as expressions of the divine without collapsing into fixed belief. The talk moves between Buddhist fabrication, eros, soulmaking, and mystical references from Tillich, Kabbalah, Tantra, Blake, Corbin, and the Zohar, all in service of a flexible, non-literal way of seeing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Concepts as openings 04:01 Circular understanding 08:27 Structures that break 11:27 Perception is central 20:06 Vertical imaginal spectrum 31:18 From image to world 40:20 Eros stretches the logos 51:15 Dynamism and emptiness 01:05:51 Meaningfulness and lightness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31523/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 3)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores what might be meant by words like God, divine, and gods, while warning against the literalism and baggage that often cling to them. He then lays out several different ways the divine can be experienced in practice: as the unfabricated and deathless, as universal love or awareness, and as something personal and particular, especially through imaginal and theophanic experience. The talk keeps returning to emptiness and fabrication, asking how a wide enough conceptual framework can support both deep insight and meaningful, soulful life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 03:33 Language and Conceptual Frames 10:06 Why Frameworks Matter 15:27 The Unfabricated 22:34 Less Fabricated States 28:15 Universal and Particular 31:13 Fabrication and Insight 43:38 Buddha Nature and Gnosis 53:19 Luria and Divine Contraction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31522/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 2)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice depends on what we assume is real, and why the usual objections to images collapse under a deeper Dharma understanding of fabrication and emptiness. He moves through modernist assumptions about matter and shared reality, then into Corbin, Avicenna, Blake, and Buddhist Tantra to show how perception can become more fluid, individualized, and sacramental. The talk gradually widens into a quiet claim: emptiness, samadhi, metta, and imaginal practice can all open the world as divine without fixing it into a single worldview.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Questions of reality 02:25 Concepts as support 04:53 Fabrication and emptiness 09:46 What counts as real 13:43 The imaginal realm 20:16 Perception follows being 25:27 Avicenna and malleability 30:41 Unfabricating practice 36:02 Tantra and divine appearance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31521/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how cultural assumptions shape the way we understand personhood, reality, and the imagination. He contrasts modern materialist views with Buddhist emptiness, imaginal, and theophanic ways of seeing, asking not which philosophy is true but what each way of looking opens or closes in practice. The talk moves toward a non-realist account of Dharma as insight into the fabrication of experience, ending with the possibility of cosmopoiesis: inhabiting a more magical, soul-making cosmos.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:19 Modern selfhood 05:40 Self as a view 10:02 Ways of looking 17:01 Person and expression 23:26 The imaginal real 28:20 Reality and quantum questions 38:47 Non-realist Dharma 48:56 Cosmopoiesis
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31520/

item.title: Theophany and Cosmopoesis: Instructions
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a detailed exploration of imaginal practice, focusing here on how attention can be shaped into different modes of perception. He contrasts bare mindfulness, emptiness practices that reduce fabrication, and a third range of skillful fabrication in which images, poetry, mantra, eros, and even distraction can open into a felt sense of holiness or a newly perceived world. The talk is especially concerned with how to recognize, tune to, and gently sustain a theophanic or cosmopoetic way of looking without turning it into a rigid belief.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the ground 03:03 Soulfulness and holiness 05:15 Cosmopoesis and theophany 09:39 Three avenues of practice 15:33 How to steer practice 28:27 Examples from practice 40:55 Mantra and distraction 47:15 Personhood and poetry 53:32 Breath, birdsong, and completion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31540/

item.title: The Love and Demands of the Imaginal (Part 2)
item.description:
In this second part of the talk, Rob Burbea returns to the question of what imaginal practice is for: not personal self-completion, but a larger movement that can be understood as for God, for the divine, or for something beyond the narrow self. He moves through Hasidic and Kabbalistic sources, the Pali Canon, Vajrayana Buddhism, and other mystical traditions to show a recurring pattern: dissolving fixed appearance, opening perception, and letting the world become more vivid, more malleable, and sometimes more sacred. Near the end, he turns to the practical demands of imaginal work — widening view, valuing multiplicity, and recognizing that image and myth already shape ordinary life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reframing the Aim 02:25 Hasidic Twofold Movement 07:20 God Beyond Belief 17:03 Beyond Being and Nonbeing 24:12 Buddha Nature and Tantra 29:30 Emptiness Makes Perception Malleable 38:39 Healing World and Purpose 52:35 Demands of the Imaginal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31537/

item.title: The Love and Demands of the Imaginal (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores what he means by an image in imaginal practice: something more alive than metaphor, with its own autonomy, love, and demand. He then works carefully through the middle way between literalism and nihilism, showing how meaning is shaped by a way of looking and why emptiness and liquidity matter here. The talk moves through healing examples, compassion practice, erotic and artistic images, and ends by widening the question of what the self is actually here to serve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Image, not metaphor 02:25 Love and demand 04:27 Middle way, not literalism 08:30 Healing old wounds 11:37 Authority and holiness 17:22 Compassion and eros 22:47 Being stretched 28:11 What is healing? 34:57 Sculptor and Coltrane
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31536/

item.title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea clarifies how walking meditation can serve either samadhi or imaginal practice, then turns to how images are related to: how long to stay with them, what they seem to want, and how love, eros, and reverence shape the practice. He shares several vivid examples — a lion, phoenix, fox, unicorn, artist’s studio, Quan Yin, and Christ — to show that imaginal figures can heal, instruct, and reframe how life is seen. The talk keeps returning to a simple test: does this feel helpful, nourishing, and soulful?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Walking as Practice 04:12 Walking with Images 07:02 What the Figure Wants 09:53 Healing Imaginal Encounters 16:37 Love and Eros 21:45 Images and Life 28:35 Literal and Nonliteral 33:02 Doubt and Experiment 59:52 Octaves and Portability
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31532/

item.title: Soulmaking (Part 2)
item.description:
In this second talk on soulmaking, Rob Burbea asks how imaginal practice relates to the Dharma without reducing either one to a fixed doctrine. He distinguishes between ways of looking that fabricate less, ways that dissolve perception more deeply, and ways that deliberately and skillfully fabricate image and self, then explores how that third mode opens room for fantasy, mythos, and soulfulness. The talk moves from the Four Noble Truths and dependent origination toward a more open-ended view in which concepts are used as lenses, images are honored as real forces in practice, and eros helps perception deepen.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Ways of Looking 05:24 Three Practice Avenues 07:39 Fantasy in Dharma 12:02 Self and Emptiness 15:14 Images and Dukkha 20:01 Soulmaking as Priority 26:21 Dharma on Imaginal Ground 38:28 Concepts as Lenses 53:13 Love, Eros, and Opening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31535/

item.title: Soulmaking (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces soul and soulfulness as active, relational qualities rather than as a fixed inner entity. He distinguishes soulmaking from panic, delusion, and bare attention, then opens a wider map of Dharma as many ways of looking, including skillful fabrication, image, myth, and fantasy. The talk moves through Buddhist, Kabbalistic, Jungian, and mythic references to show how imagination can be empty and still deeply meaningful.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the term 04:10 Soul as making 11:29 Soulfulness and papanca 17:15 Traditions and comparators 24:50 Three modes of practice 30:31 Culture and modernity 36:04 Hospitals and mythos 46:21 Emptiness frees imagination 01:00:21 Mythic life and soul story
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31534/

item.title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a practical talk on imaginal practice, defining it as mindfulness with images rather than daydreaming. He then lays out the main threads of attention: how an image is sensed, how it may fill out, how its character and specificity can be felt, and how emotional and energy-body resonances help guide the work. The talk closes by shifting from what an image can give to what it wants, with examples of reverence, compassion, gratitude, and a careful attention to relationship rather than interpretation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Mindfulness of Images 05:20 How an Image Is Sensed 15:05 Specificity in Experience 21:46 Detail and Resonance 27:14 Signals in the Energy Body 40:25 Posture and the Wounded Bird 46:21 Views and Conceptual Frames 56:18 What the Image Wants 01:12:58 Three Ways to Relate
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31533/

item.title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea outlines how imaginal practice in this retreat will be approached through meditation rather than concretizing images, with a strong emphasis on tuning to the energy body. He then lays out several ways images can arise: from samadhi, emptiness, emotional upset, deliberate invocation, dream and daydream material, and even the world seen as image. The talk keeps returning to a quiet practical question: how to meet images without literalizing them, and how to let them deepen practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice context 04:03 Energy body emphasis 09:18 Seven entry points 13:25 Liquidity and emptiness 20:28 Emotion as doorway 25:47 Deliberate imagining 30:49 Places and daydreams 36:25 The world as image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31531/

item.title: What is an 'Image'? (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a careful unpacking of what he means by “image,” moving from simple definitions into a more subtle view of how images shape perception, identity, and practice. He contrasts imaginal seeing with analytic reduction and with bare attention, and he uses examples from Jewish mysticism, Lurianic Kabbalah, Greek myth, and tantric practice to show how images can be both fabricated and deeply real. The talk gradually opens into a broader claim: that the way we look participates in what appears, and that this can support soulfulness rather than flatten experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What image means 02:36 Poetic and archetypal 06:22 Against flattening 14:39 Fabrication and divinity 20:08 Shards and views 25:06 Telos and destiny 33:44 Myth, memory, papanca 44:01 Stages and cross-pollination 51:59 Seeing as image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31539/

item.title: What is an 'Image'? (Part 1)
item.description:
(Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
Location: Gaia House
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31538/

Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order. item.title: A Little Exercise for the Imagination
item.description:
In this short guided talk, Rob Burbea leads a series of simple imaginal exercises using a peach, an apple, a rose, a bedroom, a childhood home, a loved one, the seaside, a forest, and a piece of music. The point is not to produce vivid visual pictures, but to notice the felt sense, emotional tone, and energetic resonance that can come with an image. He then reflects on why this matters, especially for people who think they “don’t have images,” and suggests that imaginal sensitivity can be trained.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Settling In 01:59 The Peach Exercise 04:27 Apple and Rose 07:21 Rooms and Home 10:34 Childhood House 13:15 Someone Loved 19:03 Seaside and Forest 24:54 Music in Imagination 27:08 What the Exercise Shows
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31519/

item.title: Energy Body: Instructions (2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea lays out two core practices for the retreat: developing the energy body as a basis for samadhi, and learning to meet emotions directly in the energy body, especially along the midline. He then shows how these practices relate to a wider Dharma arc: cultivating wholesome heart qualities, understanding how emotions are fabricated, and using attention skillfully with difficult states. Near the end, he begins to connect this emotional work with imaginal practice and explains how images can be held, tuned, and noticed for their effects on the body.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Energy body as foundation 05:06 What emotions include 11:29 Sensitivity and handling 15:17 Midline attention practice 19:57 Allowing and easing 25:41 Opening to images 31:21 Three aspects of attention 42:33 Two practices, fluidly
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31526/

item.title: Energy, Image, Emotion (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how difficult emotion, psychic energy, and image interact in imaginal practice. Using anger, loneliness, and artistic examples, he shows how an image can release trapped energy, give suffering a place, or transform a feeling without simply erasing it. The talk then widens into a discussion of views: how different ways of looking shape experience, and how Dharma practice can cultivate flexibility rather than a single fixed approach.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Emotion and trapped energy 01:39 Anger and release 04:04 Default moods and hidden energy 08:58 Coltrane and image 12:20 Meaning beyond emotion 16:52 Transformation and assumptions 20:30 Causes and transpersonal feeling 24:04 Mutual conditioning 28:51 Flexible Dharma views
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31528/

item.title: Energy, Image, Emotion (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how emotions, energy, image, and view interrelate in imaginal practice, with an emphasis on the felt life of the energy body. He distinguishes between cultivating calm and pleasure, and simply attending to emotions as they arise, then lays out a larger framework for working with emotions: feeling them, tolerating them, cultivating wholesome qualities, letting go of what is unfruitful, and understanding how they are conditioned. The talk moves into practical guidance on attention, allowing, and the use of helpful images, including several vivid retreat examples where imaginal figures transform pain and shift the energy body.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Nexus 10:41 Why Train the Energy Body 20:38 Two Practice Directions 23:25 Five Aims With Emotion 29:51 Working With Attention 41:05 Helpful Images in Practice 50:46 Lion, Divinity, and Energy 55:40 Options and Otherness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31527/

item.title: Energy Body: Instructions (1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces the retreat’s central practice as working with the energy body: a whole-body field of felt experience that can support samadhi, emotional skillfulness, and imaginal practice. He distinguishes sensation-based body awareness from more conceptual body contemplation, then spends most of the talk surveying practical ways to sense, shape, and play with the energy body through attention, breath, and imagery. The talk ends with concrete advice for working with pain, discomfort, and pleasantness, and with a reminder that the practice is meant to be light, experimental, and gentle.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the course 02:16 Three body modes 09:37 What energy body means 14:37 Samadhi and well-being 23:15 Playful practice 29:34 Opening the field 34:05 Breath and centers 54:43 Lines and light 01:07:16 Survey of possibilities
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31525/

item.title: Imaginal Practice: Doorways and Directions (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea reflects on imaginal practice as more than working with images: he argues for a broader framework in which imagination can function as a way of knowing, and in which self, Dharma, and cosmos are all open to reinterpretation. The talk moves from cultural history and philosophy into practical guidance for the retreat, including why the energy body will be central and why the aim is to learn ways of working rather than collect experiences. He repeatedly returns to questions of emptiness, fantasy, soulfulness, and the range of what can count as sacred.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Recalling the framework 06:11 Why imagination matters 13:52 Ways of knowing 23:29 Key themes ahead 32:35 Self and Dharma 42:48 Ways of looking 49:55 Learning the tools 56:36 Energy body and pace
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31530/

item.title: Imaginal Practice: Doorways and Directions (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces imaginal practice by mapping the many ways imagination already functions in Dharma, psychology, and culture: as planning, rehearsal, devotion, self-cultivation, and symbolic inquiry. He then turns to the historical suspicion of images in Western thought, contrasting that with a more open view in which images can help loosen fixed ideas of self, world, and practice. Toward the end, he names a deeper aim behind the retreat: not just using images skillfully, but allowing them to participate in a re-enchanting of experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the terrain 02:59 Traditional uses of image 06:22 Devotion and qualities 09:42 Helpful and unhelpful fantasy 14:08 Images and frameworks 20:42 Cultural suspicion of images 36:03 Why people resist 44:59 Plural selfhood 55:19 Re-enchanting the cosmos
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31529/

item.title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part Three)
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores the role of image, fantasy, and mythos in Dharma practice, suggesting that these are not side effects of practice but may be central to how we are moved, oriented, and made soulful. He questions the assumption that Buddhism is mainly about reducing suffering, and instead opens a wider view in which beauty, enchantment, devotion, and soul-making also matter. Along the way he draws on Henri Corbin, Jung, Coleridge, Keats, and the Four Noble Truths to ask how different metaphysical assumptions shape what practice means.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Image Before Meaning 06:12 What Drives Us 13:33 Corbin and the Imaginal 21:20 Buddha, Devotion, Fantasy 28:09 The Four Truths Reframed 37:26 Beauty and Enchantment 46:22 Image as Epiphany 54:34 Broadening Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/27491/

item.title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part Two)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores why imaginal practice can matter in Dharma, while also acknowledging the fears it raises around literalism, sanity, and drifting away from reality. He moves from early Buddhist examples like death contemplation and recollecting the Buddha to a wider critique of modern assumptions about what counts as real. The talk then opens into a threefold view of practice: simple mindfulness, deeper practices that fabricate less, and a third path that respects image, fantasy, and mythos as part of spiritual life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting Up the Question 04:19 Healthy and Unhealthy Imagining 06:35 Metaphor, Canon, and Presence 09:47 Reality and Assumptions 14:05 Modernism and Matter 19:04 Reading the Pali Canon 25:54 Three Paths of Practice 29:59 Mythos, Love, and Meaning
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/27492/

item.title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part One)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens by framing image, fantasy, and mythos as useful, non-pejorative parts of practice, then turns to a series of examples showing how imaginal material can carry love, depth, and transformation. He moves from vivid inner figures like a thicket and blue bird, to a frightening black devil man, to subtler images shaped by work, lineage, place, and healing. The talk then widens into a careful discussion of how images function in Dharma practice, how they differ from fixed symbols, and why they can be individually real without being publicly verifiable.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 03:52 Opening the territory 05:46 Blue bird and thicket 09:08 Devil, train, and change 13:11 Embodied tradition 15:38 Phoenix and healing 18:14 Fantasy in life and place 24:20 Visionary seeing 28:55 Condensed meaning 34:22 Literalism and reality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/27490/

item.title: In Praise of Restlessness
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a long three-part talk by asking what it means to let the Dharma be shaped by uncertainty, myth, and imagination rather than by fixed claims. He distinguishes ordinary restlessness from the kind of restlessness that belongs to inquiry, creativity, and a life that keeps opening. The talk moves from critique of dogma and nostalgia for origins into a wider case for Dharma as art: open-ended, beautiful, and sustained by desire, longing, and changing ways of seeing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 06:06 Metaphysics and myth 15:14 Homelessness and inquiry 24:35 Creative unrest 30:38 Desire on the path 39:55 Pothos and endless work 50:43 Beauty and enchantment 01:03:37 Dharma as art
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26009/

item.title: Buddhism Beyond Modernism
item.description:
In this second part of a three-part talk, Rob Burbea examines the assumptions hidden inside Dharma practice: what counts as truth, what counts as real, and what gets left unasked. He moves from the role of ideas and inquiry, through Thomas Kuhn and modern science, into critiques of modernism, bare attention, and reductionist ways of explaining experience. The talk then opens toward imagination, myth, and fantasy as shaping forces in practice, including a careful questioning of the stories we tell about awakening, the Buddha, and liberation itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting up the inquiry 04:40 Ideas and their power 14:50 Freedom to inquire 21:36 Kuhn and the Dharma 28:41 Modernism examined 35:27 Breaking the vessels 43:36 Styles of inquiry 53:32 Multiple worlds of meaning 01:05:53 Liberation and fantasy
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26007/
