item.title: Questioning Awakening
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a three-part talk by questioning the assumptions that sit underneath Dharma language, practice, and the idea of awakening. He compares several different “packages” of Dharma—mindfulness as coping, existential acceptance, machine-like optimization, transcendence, emptiness, and more—to show how each one carries its own hidden view of reality. The talk keeps returning to the same pressure point: whether we are actually free to inquire into what we take for granted, or whether our views are quietly shaping what practice can be.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 05:09 Many versions of awakening 14:50 Four truths, many goals 21:08 What do we want? 32:50 Presence and the now 42:02 Mindfulness and reality 46:37 Ontology behind practice 54:14 Modern materialist models 01:06:06 Questioning hidden views
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26010/

item.title: Creative Samadhi
item.description:
Rob Burbea reopens the meaning of samadhi beyond narrow concentration practice, showing how it belongs within a larger path of insight and cultivation. He argues that practice is an art of deepening perception, nourishing the heart, and learning to work skillfully with attention, effort, and letting go. After a conceptual overview, he offers a brief guided exploration of whole-body awareness and energetic texture, then closes by reflecting on how openness, beauty, and everyday doing belong in the path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:56 The path goes somewhere 10:21 Two wings of practice 16:08 Samadhi as wholeness 19:01 Why focus is not enough 26:13 Ways to deepen 31:05 Whole-body experiment 39:45 Space, letting go, and looking 45:56 Open heart, open practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26008/

item.title: Ayya Santacitta, Ayya Santussika, David Loy, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 5
item.description:
This episode closes a five-part series on mindfulness, Dharma, and climate action with a practical question: how can Buddhist practice become collective response? David Loy frames the climate crisis as a challenge to reexamine Buddhism itself, while Thanissara and Ayya Santussika move the conversation toward organizing, public pressure, and faith-based networks. The talk stays grounded in both inner practice and concrete action, ending with reflections on joy, courage, and continuing the work together.
Location: Unknown
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the final call 01:24 What can we actually do? 06:15 Reexamining Buddhist practice 13:32 The bodhisattva path 20:31 Building a Buddhist movement 34:41 Precepts, urgency, and earth 47:20 Questions from the sangha 58:07 Joy, continuity, blessing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29723/

item.title: Bonnie Duran, Chas DiCapua, Thanissara, Vinny Ferraro: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 4
item.description:
This conversation moves from climate action as a practical response to climate harm into a deeper inquiry about interdependence, colonization, and embodiment. Bonnie Duran frames climate change through Indigenous knowledge and the Buddhist roots poisons, Chas DiCapua turns to patriarchy, technology, and the need to come back into the body, and Vinny Ferraro brings the discussion toward belonging, grief, and action that arises from listening inwardly. The talk closes with breakout reflections and a merit dedication that widens the circle to animals, human communities, and the earth itself.
Location: Unknown
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Introductions 02:12 Plant-Based Ethics 06:42 Colonization and Root Poisons 13:28 Rights of Nature 18:43 Technology and Patriarchy 27:10 Returning to the Body 32:24 Belonging and Grief 40:30 Listening and Action 51:19 Questions and Dedication
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29722/

item.title: Catherine McGee, Chris Cullen, James Baraz, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 3
item.description:
This conversation brings together Thanissara, Catherine McGee, Chris Cullen, and James Baraz to explore climate action as a Dharma practice. The talk moves from the urgency of the climate crisis into a steadier question: how to stay grounded, emotionally honest, and still willing to act. Across the episode, the teachers return to reconnection, gratitude, grief, and the kind of view that makes sustained action possible.
Location: Unknown
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and Welcome 04:25 Why This Matters 09:54 Action and View 13:34 Bone-Jangling Reality 17:17 Silence and Fire 20:38 Reconnecting With the Earth 30:50 From Practice to Action 36:04 James on Inspiration 39:53 Questions and Blessing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29721/

item.title: Ayya Anandabodhi, Rev. angel Kyodo williams, Susie Harrington, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 2
item.description:
This conversation brings climate change into the center of Dharma practice, treating it as a spiritual, ethical, and social reality rather than a separate concern. Thanissara moderates reflections from Ayya Anandabodhi, Reverend Angel Kyodo Williams, and Susie Harrington, who each return to the themes of interconnection, the three poisons, systemic harm, grief, and the need for skillful action. The talk moves from naming the crisis and its inequities to practical ways of engaging without collapse, denial, or hopelessness.
Location: Unknown

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29720/

item.title: The Four Noble Truths of Responding to Climate Change
item.description:
Donald Rothberg frames climate change through the Four Noble Truths, moving from an honest reckoning with suffering into the causes, possibilities, and pathways for response. He draws on Buddhist ethics, Thich Nhat Hanh, Joanna Macy, Bhikkhu Bodhi, Bill McKibben, Lester Brown, and the Dalai Lama to connect inner practice with collective action. The talk balances sober climate facts with practical encouragement, especially around staying connected, avoiding despair, and taking local and political steps.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Ethics and Integrity 04:25 Non-harming and Earth Care 06:33 Four Noble Truths Framework 10:44 The Bad News 23:22 Meeting Distress 25:53 Roots of the Crisis 32:40 Possibility of Transformation 40:29 The Path of Response 51:58 Questions and Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/25028/

Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/25028/

item.title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how images, fantasies, and inner figures shape practice far more than we often notice. He moves from common ways of treating imagery—ignoring it, psychologizing it, or folding it into a tidy self-story—toward a more imaginal approach in which images are met as meaningful, sometimes demanding presences. The talk keeps returning to a quiet test: what happens in the body, the heart, and the sense of self when an image is taken seriously without being literalized?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 02:27 Common responses 04:31 Psychological readings 07:07 Conceiving and experimenting 10:57 Image as living presence 16:20 From emptiness to freedom 21:26 Daemons and angels 28:32 Risk, myth, and ethics 36:19 What is not just ego
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/28027/

item.title: Jack Kornfield, Ruth King, Tara Brach, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 1
item.description:
This conversation begins with a shared pause and a framing of climate action as Dharma practice, then moves through Tara Brach’s emphasis on interdependence, grief, and love as the basis for response. Ruth King follows by naming climate change as a systemic issue tied to racism, exploitation, and collective responsibility, while Jack Kornfield widens the lens to dukkha, bodhisattva vows, and long-term, non-attached action. The talk closes with questions from participants and practical encouragement to stay connected, organize, and keep practicing together.
Location: Unknown
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and grounding 05:26 Climate as shared condition 13:24 Presence and belonging 22:38 Systems and justice 32:19 Dukkha and bodhisattva path 42:46 Act from love 46:28 Questions on practice 56:51 Organizing together 01:05:55 Closing blessings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29718/

item.title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues an exploration of imaginal practice, using retreat stories to show how inner figures can be met non-literally and with mindfulness. He moves from the inner critic to a wider inner theater of persons, then widens further to public figures, mythic energies, and the images that shape a life. The talk closes by relating image-work to emotional energy and to the steadiness of samadhi, suggesting that both upset and settledness can open the way to imagery.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Picking Up the Thread 00:47 Inner Critic, Inner Persons 04:14 Mindfulness and Image 07:25 Loosening a Solid Self 10:37 Images as Resources 17:06 Figures in Culture 21:38 Wildness and Shame 26:06 Working Deliberately
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/28028/

item.title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea questions a familiar Buddhist habit of treating the self as merely a bundle of aggregates, arguing that this can be useful but also narrow, both spiritually and psychologically. He then turns to a more imaginal way of working with experience, using the inner critic as a case study and showing how dialogue, kindness, and a different way of looking can transform what feels oppressive. The talk ends by widening again, with a story about grief and the image of a dead mother, suggesting that what is alive in the psyche may ask for a more spacious and beautiful kind of response.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Questioning the usual view 02:08 Limits of aggregates 03:41 A skillful way of looking 06:55 Why simplicity fails 11:01 Modern suffering and self 12:26 Meeting the inner critic 14:46 From deconstructing to relating 16:50 The clumsy protector 19:43 Grief and the mother image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/28026/

item.title: Meditation: Opening to Aliveness
item.description:
This guided meditation begins with settling posture, breath, and intention, then moves into a full-body scan that emphasizes softness, sensitivity, and aliveness. Tara Brach invites an embodied noticing of the senses, sound, and the background of awareness, with repeated reminders that the mind can drift and return without judgment. The practice closes by deepening into the felt earth-body and the spacious silence that holds changing experience.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the Body 01:56 Intention and Aum 05:02 Softening the Face 07:06 Heart and Belly 10:58 Pelvis and Earth Body 14:08 Sound and Presence 17:29 Returning Fresh 20:59 Deepening Into Silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/24680/?access_key=ltYFO7cMrx

item.title: Earth's Crisis - On the Edge of the Roof - (on climate change)
item.description:
Tara Brach explores climate crisis through Dharma, using Rumi, the Parsifal legend, and Joanna Macy to show how denial, grasping, and disconnection keep both people and the planet in a kind of trance. She moves from the scale of ecological loss to the inner work of feeling, presence, and belonging, then toward the question of what right action looks like without certainty about outcomes. The talk closes with a prayerful invitation to listen more deeply to the living earth and respond from care.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the crisis 09:02 Rumi and Parsifal 12:10 Trance and consumption 16:52 Separation and entitlement 25:00 Digital distance 28:52 Earth as belonging 33:03 Feeling and action 42:31 Collective awakening 50:33 Listening and prayer
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/24681/?access_key=jl2Rr7VAc5

item.title: Buddhist Perspective On Climate Change
item.description:
This talk examines climate change first through the lens of observed science, then through a Buddhist ethical frame. After summarizing major findings from the IPCC and a U.S. regional assessment, Sayadaw Vivekananda turns to Nepal as a vivid example of unequal impact, before widening into questions of non-harming, compassion, greed, responsibility, and future generations. The talk ends by linking mindfulness practice to a quieter, more restrained relationship with the natural world.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 01:17 What climate science shows 12:28 Human influence and local impacts 20:14 Nepal and the Himalayas 28:34 Causes of the crisis 32:19 Harmony with nature 40:05 Non-harming and metta 48:05 Greed and responsibility 59:59 Trees and future generations
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23813/

item.title: Practice & Climate Change
item.description:
Thanissara reflects on practice as preparation for the moments when fear, grief, and collective crisis press on the heart. Using climate change as the main example, she draws practice toward refuge, resilience, and the possibility of responding from a steadier and less separative mind. The talk moves from basic mindfulness and ethics into samadhi, renunciation, and the quiet capacity to meet uncertainty without collapsing into reactivity.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Practice as preparation 02:46 Climate change and activation 05:36 A shift in consciousness 10:50 Refuge in Buddha Dharma Sangha 16:18 Ethics and conscience 19:30 Courage and resilience 23:21 Knowing awareness directly 29:33 Simplify and let go 40:02 The peace that remains
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23290/

item.title: Wise Action for the Earth
item.description:
David Loy frames the ecological crisis through a Buddhist lens, arguing that the sense of a separate self is both the root of personal suffering and a collective source of environmental harm. He moves from dukkha and non-separation to bodhisattva practice, inner training, and forms of action that respond to climate crisis without losing balance. The talk closes with Rumi, Joanna Macy, Howard Thurman, and a brief Q&A that returns to resilience, systems change, and how language itself can hide or reveal our intimacy with the earth.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:04 Buddhism and crisis 02:56 Separate self, shared suffering 05:26 Forgetting the self 06:50 From awakening to action 14:14 A collective predicament 26:49 Three kinds of transformation 30:17 Questions and closing images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23259/

item.title: Right Action on Climate
item.description:
Ayya Anandabodhi speaks about climate change as an ethical and Dharma issue, beginning with the sense of Earth as sacred and moving through reasons Buddhists may feel hesitant to engage. She uses the Buddha’s care for a sick monk, Joanna Macy’s phrase about honoring pain for the world, and a series of concrete suggestions to show how grief can become steadiness and action. The talk then widens into practical organizing: speaking truthfully, joining groups, writing letters, divesting, and making personal and political commitments without losing compassion.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Earth as sacred 02:01 Why Buddhists hesitate 03:51 The sick monk 06:20 Climate as ethics 09:47 Pressure and renewal 13:28 Resilience through practice 16:32 Concrete commitments 19:58 What to do now 34:10 Questions and everyday choices
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23258/

item.title: Today’s Climate Reality
item.description:
Ayya Santussika offers a plainspoken Dharma framing for climate crisis: first look directly, then understand what is happening well enough to respond. She moves through current climate evidence, likely near- and long-term impacts, and the political and economic forces that keep emissions going. The talk ends by shifting from fear toward collective action, including emissions cuts, adaptation, and the role of education, poverty reduction, and restoring natural systems.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:10 Look directly 00:49 What is happening now 04:13 Warming and wildfires 06:39 Impacts on life and conflict 09:17 Maps of impact 15:38 California drought ahead 17:18 The carbon budget 20:14 Why action is blocked 24:53 What to do together
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23256/

item.title: How To Be an Earthling
item.description:
Wes Nisker offers a humorous Earth Day talk that moves from comic songs and playful scripture mashups into a serious reflection on ecological crisis and our place within evolution. He uses Gary Snyder, John Muir, Darwin, and his own satire to question human exceptionalism and suggest a more grounded, interdependent story. The talk closes with a simple practice of presence—here, now—paired with a call to care for the planet in ordinary, practical ways.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:05 Ecological pledge 01:26 John Muir and wonder 02:45 Crisis and comic relief 07:06 Liberation and Passover 09:11 Earth Day and Gaia 10:54 Evolution as creation myth 14:36 Made of earth 17:46 Enough stuff 23:57 Here and now
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23514/

item.title: Compassion As We Take Action
item.description:
Jack Kornfield speaks at a Spirit Rock Earth Day gathering about how compassion can carry action without being driven by guilt, anger, or exhaustion. He weaves in stories about Gary Snyder, Gabriela Mistral, Hawaiian voyaging, elephants, chimps, and Buddhist teachers to suggest a quieter kind of activism rooted in love, interconnection, and long horizons. The talk closes with a guided meditation and blessing-cord ceremony that turns intention into a lived practice of refuge, non-harming, and vow.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening questions 04:26 Act from love 09:11 Grief into song 11:38 Finding our way 18:01 Interbeing and seeds 22:44 Long horizons 27:25 A bodhisattva vow 30:36 Meditation on vastness 42:51 Blessing cords
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23513/

item.title: The Eco-Crisis as a Spiritual Crisis
item.description:
David Loy explores the ecological crisis as a spiritual crisis, arguing that the same sense of separation that Buddhism addresses in the individual self also appears at the level of human civilization. He moves from climate change, biodiversity loss, and industrial growth to Buddhist questions about awakening, activism, and why mindfulness can become a coping strategy rather than a response to suffering. The talk closes by reading poems from Rumi and David Whyte to point toward non-separation, intimacy, and a different way of living with the earth.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Fragile Earth 02:01 Seeing the Iceberg 08:50 Reading Extinction 11:20 Beyond Climate Alone 20:08 What Awakening Means 33:46 Growth and Alienation 43:10 Intimacy With the World 49:45 A Spiritual Crisis
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/22994/

item.title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: Earth As Witness: International Dharma Teachers' Statement on Climate Change
item.description:
This talk weaves climate science together with Dharma teaching, beginning with the simple fact of breath and dependence on Earth’s systems. Bob Doppelt and James Baraz then move from the scale of the climate crisis to a collaboratively written statement, "Earth as Witness," that frames suffering, ethics, mindfulness, and collective action as part of the response. The talk ends by returning to hope, practical change, and a brief loving-kindness practice for the planet.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 01:53 Opening the conversation 04:57 Breath and interdependence 12:39 The science of warming 17:11 Facing the suffering 19:09 Earth as Witness 31:53 Dharma principles for action 43:55 Hope through visible change 47:46 Individual and systemic shifts 56:57 Tipping points and metta
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21981/

item.title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Three)
item.description:
In this third talk of The Theatre of Selves, Rob Burbea turns from general framing to the conceptual assumptions underneath how we meet images, self, and practice. He argues that meditation and Dharma are not just about staying with what is, but about learning to notice and sometimes loosen the frameworks that shape what appears at all. The talk moves from the limits of bare attention into a more imaginal view in which figures, myths, and archetypes can be approached as active presences rather than as objects to be reduced or integrated.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the series 02:43 Less fabrication 05:32 Ways of looking 09:50 Concepts underneath experience 15:16 Images are not parts 25:07 Archetypes and autonomy 37:17 What the image wants 48:50 Myth and soulfulness 01:00:46 Cautions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21820/

item.title: Climate Change, Capitalism, and Buddhadharma
item.description:
(Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) An impassioned talk that includes a little known story from the suttas about the dangers of and suffering caused by the notion of private property. During the talk, winds of 45 mph buffeted the monastery, emergency sirens sounded in the distance, and three power outages occurred. Ends with a lively discussion with the sangha.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21552/

Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley. Source: Dharma Seed.

item.title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Two)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores imagination as a genuine part of practice, not as decoration, and keeps returning to the question of what different ways of looking make possible. He moves from the emptiness of self, through devotion, archetype, shame, and lineage, toward the sense that images can open a more soulful and less reductive way of meeting life. The talk stays practical throughout, emphasizing mindfulness of body, emotion, and relationship to the image itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice and emptiness 03:56 Images in practice 08:27 Archetype and presence 13:30 Love and shame reframed 21:47 Self as mythic field 29:51 Deliberate imaginal practice 39:17 Relationships with images 50:14 Iconic, not reductive 59:50 Concepts and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21821/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Rob Burbea explores imagination as a genuine part of practice, not as decoration, and keeps returning to the question of what different ways of looking make possible. He moves from the emptiness of self, through devotion, archetype, shame, and lineage, toward the sense that images can open a more soulful and less reductive way of meeting life. The talk stays practical throughout, emphasizing mindfulness of body, emotion, and relationship to the image itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice and emptiness 03:56 Images in practice 08:27 Archetype and presence 13:30 Love and shame reframed 21:47 Self as mythic field 29:51 Deliberate imaginal practice 39:17 Relationships with images 50:14 Iconic, not reductive 59:50 Concepts and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21821/

item.title: The Theatre of Selves (Part One)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a retreat series on the imagination as a serious part of Dharma practice, first situating it within emptiness, not-self, and the limits of a purely mindfulness-centered view. He then lays out several ways of seeing through self-construction, before turning to imaginal practice as a more nuanced and life-giving way to work with the body, inner criticism, devotion, and the plurality of self. The talk moves gradually from doctrinal framing into concrete examples, ending with the possibility that dialogue with images can loosen fixed identity and open new values and energies.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Imagination Matters 05:04 Emptiness and Not-Self 06:39 Seven Ways Through Self 12:18 Limits of Simplifying 18:04 The Imaginal Opening 21:54 Images in Practice 26:59 Working With Inner Critics 35:51 Myths, Parents, and Voices 46:46 Plurality and Dialogue
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21819/

item.title: Wise Responses to the Hindrances
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a practical and gentle exploration of the five hindrances, focusing less on defeating them than on changing the way they are met. He shows how sense desire, aversion, dullness, restlessness, and doubt can each be worked with through breath, posture, attention, kindness, and a wider view of practice. The talk keeps returning to a central theme: hindrances are not personal failures, and they can become a source of insight into how perception is shaped.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Frame 03:26 Not Personal Failure 07:22 Samadhi Beyond Focus 15:37 Working With Desire 20:24 Aversion and Ill Will 24:29 Listening Underneath 28:32 Dullness and Energy 32:42 Restlessness and Subtle Effort 45:25 Doubt and Perception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21822/

item.title: All In This Together
item.description:
Catherine McGee begins from Earth Care Week and uses it to open a wider question: how inner practice and outer responsibility belong to the same field. She moves through environmental examples, the experience of loneliness and resonance on retreat, and teachings on non-separation, showing how clear seeing can lead to compassionate but unforced action. The talk closes by widening into bodhisattva aspiration, with a strong emphasis on community, humility, and responding appropriately in changing conditions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Earth Care Frame 04:14 Inner and Outer 10:06 We Are the Environment 13:41 No Separation 20:10 Resonance and Loneliness 30:52 Emptying Out 37:23 Who Hears? 43:58 Community Awakening 53:58 Bodhisattva Vow
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21003/

item.title: The Anatomy of Ignorance 2 - The Social Roots of Ignorance and climate Disruption
item.description:
Donald Rothberg continues his "Anatomy of Ignorance" series by turning from personal and universal ignorance to the social ways a culture can fail to see clearly. He uses climate disruption as the central example, moving from scientific warnings and denial to the emotional work of taking the crisis in, and then to individual, community, and collective responses. The talk stays grounded in concrete action while also insisting that practice, compassion, and honesty are needed to meet the scale of the problem.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the Three Ignorances 06:47 Social Conditioning 14:35 Climate Disruption 24:59 How We Don’t Take It In 28:50 Grief and Processing 33:11 Personal and Local Action 40:09 Collective Mobilization 47:59 Practice and Motivation 51:33 Questions and Final Counsel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20859/

item.title: The Broader Context of Mindfulness
item.description:
Mark Coleman argues that mindfulness is more than paying attention: it sits inside the Eightfold Path and is supported by ethics, wisdom, kindness, and restraint. He uses mountain camping stories, examples from email and parenting, and reflections on military and political life to show how awareness can lead to wiser action rather than passivity. The talk closes by linking mindfulness to climate response and to the practical question of what we should do, or stop doing, once we see more clearly.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Mindfulness Beyond Attention 03:35 Rooted in Tradition 10:01 Clarity in Action 15:03 Path, Ethics, Wisdom 19:10 Unwise Attention 26:59 Qualities of Presence 33:03 From Awareness to Conduct 43:14 Stories of Intervention 54:31 Practice and Climate
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20771/

item.title: Practicing in Times of Big Changes
item.description:
This talk reflects on practicing through instability, with a long opening of chant followed by a teaching on how change can be met without panic or resignation. Ayya Santacitta uses the butterfly story, the Buddha under the Bodhi tree, and dependent origination to show how clear seeing, patience, and mindfulness can meet breakdown without being overwhelmed by it. The discussion ends with a close look at feeling, grasping, and the small gap where practice can interrupt reactivity.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening chant 20:10 Facing unavoidable change 21:37 Butterfly transformation 24:47 Seeing without forcing 26:07 Mara becomes flowers 27:28 Inner and outer climate 31:01 Paññā and insight 33:21 Where practice interrupts
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20660/

item.title: Follow Your Heartbreak
item.description:
James Baraz reflects on a recent self-retreat and uses Andrew Harvey’s sacred activism as a frame for understanding both personal practice and the crisis of the wider world. He moves from the hardship and sweetness of retreat into the classical progress of insight, showing how suffering, fear, and disorientation can become part of awakening rather than signs of failure. The talk closes by widening back out to a collective vision: heartbreak as a condition for action, and crisis as a possible seedbed for compassion, justice, and new consciousness.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and retreat context 03:06 Andrew Harvey's framing 07:11 Dark night of the species 13:13 Practice through hardship 20:52 Progress of insight 28:56 The hero's journey 33:21 Heartbreak and sacred activism 37:19 Signs of awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20497/

item.title: The Dharma Of Global Climate Change
item.description:
In this talk, Chas DiCapua brings climate change into the field of Dharma practice, asking how awareness, ethics, and interdependence can shape a more useful response. He moves from the shock and avoidance that surround the issue to the habits of craving, possession, and separate-self view that help drive it, then turns toward contentment, stewardship, and collective care. The talk stays grounded in lived experience and closes with a poem that treats difficulty itself as a path of awakening.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Topic 04:15 Smoke and Social Freeze 06:50 Why It Matters 11:55 Craving and Contentment 19:46 Possession and Stewardship 25:05 Interdependence and Story 29:12 Cause, Effect, and Avoidance 40:32 Mind and Separate Self 48:01 From Fear to Awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20250/

item.title: Planet Earth as Divine Messenger
item.description:
Ayya Santacitta begins with the Buddha’s basic diagnosis of dukkha as unsatisfactoriness, then moves into the four sights and the urgency they gave to practice. From there, she widens the lens to climate change, arguing that the same discipline used to meet aging, sickness, and death is needed to face the planet’s condition without turning away. The talk closes by linking non-self, ecological identity, and practical collective action, with brief reflections from Thomas Berry, Arne Naess, and Joanna Macy.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Marin Sangha
Chapters: 00:01 Arriving in the Present 02:24 Dukkha and Impermanence 06:31 The Four Sights 10:06 Extending the Contemplation 12:50 Climate as Context 19:29 Home, Ecology, and Ego 23:35 Turning Toward Dissonance 28:39 Earth as Sacred Text 35:57 Ecological Self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20403/

item.title: Learning from Climate Change
item.description:
Ayya Santacitta reflects on climate change as both a practical emergency and a spiritual challenge: a situation that asks for honest attention, emotional digestion, and a wider sense of belonging. She moves from the difficulty of speaking about climate without shutting people down, to a critique of the Enlightenment-era worldview, to teachings on grief, resilience, and ecological identity. The talk closes by placing the climate crisis in a larger evolutionary frame, where discomfort can become a path into deeper responsibility and practice.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery
Chapters: 00:00 Naming the dilemma 02:28 Shock and denial 06:00 Discomfort as guidance 08:25 Universe as scripture 14:17 Outgrown worldviews 19:58 Grief and ecological self 24:54 Attention and resilience 32:36 Learning from collapse 39:53 Closing appeal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20405/

item.title: The Brahma Viharas and the Climate Challenge
item.description:
Ayya Anandabodhi speaks about the Brahma-viharas as a practical response to climate anxiety and the wider turbulence of the world. She moves from calming the heart with loving-kindness, into compassion, appreciative joy, and equanimity, using nature images, a Buddha story, and the tension between ultimate and conventional truth. The talk ends with questions about sunflowers, monastic life, gender in the lineage, and her own path into ordination, followed by a sharing-of-blessings chant.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Homage 01:01 Practice in Turbulent Times 04:50 Awakening Is Human 06:35 Training Awareness 09:55 Facing Climate Anxiety 15:00 Metta and Compassion 17:09 Mudita and Equanimity 20:09 Ultimate and Conventional 26:26 Ethics and Renunciation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19967/

item.title: 400 and Rising
item.description:
Ayya Anandabodhi begins with travel and community impressions from monasteries in the Pacific Northwest, then turns toward the climate crisis as a way of seeing our place in the wider web of life. From there, the talk moves through grief, responsibility, and the four Brahma Viharas as practical supports for meeting fear without numbness or despair. She closes by bringing Buddhist teachings on karma, care, and the sick monk story into a clear argument for action, tenderness, and not turning away.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery
Chapters: 00:01 Arriving in community 02:23 Hermitage and the eagles 06:36 The illusion of control 12:27 400 parts per million 14:17 Listening for the feminine 19:18 Seeing the larger truth 22:44 Four Brahma Viharas 28:59 Equanimity and karma 33:56 Care for what is sick
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Reflections on the Day
item.description:
Nina Wise offers a short, embodied opening before moving into a larger reflection on climate grief, love, and collective responsibility. The talk weaves together a film about melting glaciers, a story of a prison garden, and a final image of interdependence that loosens the sense of a separate self. It ends quietly with an invitation to see what is alive, fragile, and connected right where one is.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening into the body 00:49 Motion theater and release 04:43 From me to we 10:27 Glaciers and grief 14:49 A prison garden begins 20:02 Planting and recognition 23:22 Stardust and non-self 25:04 Closing in the hills
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19234/

item.title: Active Hope
item.description:
Joanna Macy reflects on Active Hope as a practice of meeting ecological and social crisis without panic or paralysis. She reworks hope as something we do, then traces how uncertainty can deepen compassion, widen responsibility, and draw people into collective action. The talk moves between Bodhisattva language, personal memory, activism, grief, and a culminating reading from the Avatamsaka Sutra that expands the sense of time, body, and interconnection.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and the book 01:28 Hope as a verb 03:59 Bodhisattva and bodhicitta 06:59 The plankton shock 13:37 Panic and paralysis 18:41 Working together 24:42 Grief into action 34:07 Service across time 42:05 One hair, all worlds
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19251/

item.title: Connecting Inner and Outer Responses to Climate Change
item.description:
Donald Rothberg offers a grounded talk on climate change as a call to join inner practice with outer response. He moves from a short meditation invocation into a critique of spiritual escapism and reactive activism, then turns to the bodhisattva ideal as a model for sustained, compassionate engagement. The talk ends with practical reflections on personal, communal, and collective action, and with brief readings that hold urgency and tenderness together.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 02:14 Opening Invocation 05:32 Why Integration Matters 09:24 The Bodhisattva Ideal 14:29 Finding Your Calling 18:16 Mindfulness Must Act 19:13 Dharma and Climate Practice 24:27 Personal to Systemic Response 28:44 Reflective Exercise 36:07 Closing Readings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19240/

item.title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: From Me to We
item.description:
This Earth Day talk links climate change to core Dharma themes: interdependence, karma, moral conduct, trusteeship, and the freedom to choose differently. James Baraz opens by framing the gathering as sangha-based refuge and a call for teacher leadership, then Bob Doppelt offers a practical framework for responding to climate breakdown without being driven by fear. The talk includes a short guided reflection on breathing, the living systems that sustain it, and how awareness can widen into responsibility.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the gathering 02:13 Sangha and teacher request 09:50 Bob's framework 15:33 Climate as closed system 24:15 Breath and interdependence 30:06 Awareness and systems 35:50 Moral choice 40:32 Trusteeship and restoration 45:09 Free will and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19235/

item.title: Dharma practice and the climate Crisis
item.description:
Donald Rothberg frames the climate crisis as a question of how Dharma practice and worldly responsibility belong together. He begins with the felt tension between retreat life and climate action, then uses examples from Thich Nhat Hanh, Thomas Merton, Joanna Macy, and others to argue for a both-and path: inner cultivation, ethical clarity, and practical response. The talk closes with concrete suggestions for personal, community, and institutional climate work, while returning to the need to stay centered in love rather than panic.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat and urgency 04:43 The climate dilemma 14:18 Engaged Buddhism examples 17:31 Joanna Macy's framework 20:24 Vocation and critique 26:38 Practice needs ethics 36:08 Why activism needs practice 41:23 Concrete climate responses 50:18 Questions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19116/

item.title: The Necessity of Fantasy
item.description:
Rob Burbea questions the usual Buddhist emphasis on calm, non-attachment, and individual liberation, arguing that ethics, responsibility, and social engagement belong at the center of Dharma. He moves from the scale of climate change and species loss to the role of fantasy, image, and myth in shaping what we care about and how we act. The talk asks whether Western Buddhism has narrowed its imagination too far, and whether a wider range of images might allow a more responsive, living practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 06:29 Ethics as foundation 14:41 Climate and responsibility 22:18 Silence and Buddhist culture 28:33 Fantasy and image 32:52 Narrowed images 41:08 Dukkha beyond the self 50:31 Stories that shape culture 58:23 Widening the range
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/18111/

item.title: Approaching the Dharma: Part Two - Liberating Ways of Looking
item.description:
Rob Burbea develops the idea that insight is not mainly a special experience, but any understanding that lessens dukkha. He then shifts from insight as a result of practice to insight as a method: ways of looking through not-self, impermanence, unsatisfactoriness, metta, and awareness itself. The talk moves gradually toward a deeper claim that self, object, and even the sense of fabricating are empty and mutually dependent, so practice becomes learning how to use and release different ways of perceiving.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 03:17 Insight, loosely defined 08:03 Insight from experience 13:49 A way of looking 19:00 Impermanence and letting go 25:41 Metta and the Four Noble Truths 32:09 Repetition and deeper insight 39:41 Fabrication and emptiness 48:03 Practice as experimentation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/17961/

item.title: Approaching the Dharma: Part One - Unbinding the World
item.description:
Rob Burbea uses the Bahiya Sutta to open a longer inquiry into how perception, selfing, and dukkha are built together. Rather than treating the teaching as a simple instruction in bare attention, he explores how reactivity, comparison, labeling, and the sense of a witness all participate in fabricating experience. The talk moves from canonical passages and stories to a quieter question: what changes when perception is seen as conditioned, empty, and less solid than we usually assume?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the arc 03:02 The world of perception 08:54 Beyond elements and extremes 13:34 Bahiya's urgency 19:49 Just the seen 28:18 Desire and fabrication 35:45 A spectrum of construction 41:38 Mirage and illusion 49:09 What practice means
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/17960/

item.title: Thoughts and Images in Meditation
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how thoughts and images can be obstacles, tools, and, at subtler levels, doorways in meditation. He moves from calming scattered thought and working with effort, to using the body and vedana to loosen obsession, and then to open awareness where thoughts can be seen as passing phenomena. In the later part of the talk, he turns to the skillful use of imagination, image, and belief, and finally to the more radical question of conceiving itself as the root of suffering.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the topic 02:13 Scattered thought and effort 10:20 Labeling and relieving 17:10 Obsession and belief 23:38 Emotion in the body 29:00 Open awareness 33:18 Skillful use of image 43:48 Views and conceiving
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/17963/

item.title: Stewardship and Intention as Gaia Practices
item.description:
James Baraz offers a Dharma-inflected reading of ecological responsibility through Bob Doppelt’s book From Me to We, focusing on two commitments: acknowledging trustee obligations and choosing one’s destiny. He moves from interdependence, karma, and moral responsibility into a practical model of trusteeship, then closes with a change process for moving out of denial and into action. Along the way he uses Buddha references, Ram Dass, Julia Butterfly Hill, and a remembered Andreas Vollenweider concert to make the ethical stakes feel personal and concrete.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:35 From Me to We 05:13 The Law of Trusteeship 09:58 Benevolence and No Ownership 12:56 Power as Responsibility 16:39 Duties of a Trustee 25:17 Taking the Commitment 27:45 Choosing Destiny 32:00 Stages of Change
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16804/

item.title: Karma and Sila as Gaia Practices
item.description:
James Baraz uses Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We as a lens for reading Dharma through climate and social responsibility. He moves from interdependence and not-self to karma, then into a concrete look at how ordinary habits ripple through ecological systems and organizations. The talk ends with a quiet, practical invitation to mindfulness, accountability, and moral clarity without being overwhelmed by guilt.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:01 Framing the talk 01:21 From me to we 04:14 Seeing the systems 06:44 Karma and accountability 13:16 The Buddha’s example 16:40 Climate as consequence 26:11 Hidden impacts 32:01 Mindfulness over guilt 36:45 Three commitments
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16555/

item.title: Seeing the System You Are Part Of
item.description:
James Baraz frames this talk as a response to ecological and social crisis, beginning with a reading from Ernest Callenbach’s final essay and the idea that hope and cooperation matter in hard times. He then turns to Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We, using it to lay out a Dharma-informed shift from individualism toward systems awareness, mutual responsibility, and practical action. The talk moves steadily from broad vision to specific principles, ending with a close look at interdependence through Thich Nhat Hanh and Lewis Thomas.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:30 Framing the Tipping Point 03:26 Callenbach’s Final Vision 08:15 Hope and Mutual Support 13:30 Hard Times, Regeneration 24:22 From Me to We 30:16 Five Commitments 40:26 Interbeing in the Body
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16516/

item.title: Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point: Concern for the Planet as Dharma Practice
item.description:
James Baraz reflects on climate change as a Dharma question: how to respond without collapsing into denial, overwhelm, or apathy. He brings in Bill McKibben, Bhikkhu Bodhi, and Joanna Macy to frame ecological crisis as a practice of seeing clearly, widening time perspective, and acting with humility. The talk ends with a guided exercise from the standpoint of a future being, followed by a brief small-group sharing practice.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:29 The climate warning 04:21 From outrage to overwhelm 08:42 Systems change and Dharma 10:18 Institutions and responsibility 13:32 Bhikkhu Bodhi at the cliff edge 19:29 Joanna Macy and deep time 25:24 Seventh-generation ethics 27:20 Future self practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16313/

item.title: This Matter of Emptiness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores emptiness as more than a philosophical idea, contrasting it with a simple “be with whatever is happening” understanding of mindfulness. He moves from Nagarjuna and the Buddha’s teachings to a practical account of how self, object, and suffering are fabricated through reactivity, mood, assumptions, and the way we look. The talk closes by reframing practice as learning to see this fabrication clearly, so that experience can be held with more freedom, groundlessness, and warmth.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Footstep and map 05:59 Ultimate truth 12:59 Self and all things 17:53 How experience is built 26:27 Wolf and rabbit 34:12 Foam, mirage, magic 40:37 Realism and liberation 44:05 Practice as seeing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/15324/

item.title: The Beauty of Desire (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues an inquiry into desire by first revisiting a more classical Dharma approach: noticing craving, clinging, and the suffering that comes from moving toward and away from experience. He then turns to a different method, using case studies to explore what happens when desire is felt more deeply in the body, often revealing strength, love, joy, or spaciousness beneath what first seemed like lack. The talk ends by questioning some familiar assumptions about desire and pointing toward a more open, less fixed relationship to inquiry itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:34 Letting go of desire 08:51 A different approach 12:03 The bank story 20:18 Craving and hype 26:40 Desire beneath the pain 46:18 History and sacred desire 01:00:07 Methods and closing questions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14587/

item.title: The Beauty of Desire (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a two-part inquiry into desire by questioning the usual Dharma assumption that desire simply causes suffering. He leads a guided exploration through deathbed reflection, body awareness, and the voice of the inner critic, then turns to examples showing how desire can be both limiting and life-giving. The talk stays tentative rather than conclusive, asking whether some desires may need protection and nourishment rather than immediate release.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 04:26 The usual Dharma frame 09:24 Why letting go is hard 12:32 Inquiry on the cushion 20:15 What do we want? 28:21 Inner critic and fear 40:36 Desire as development 55:02 No quick conclusion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14586/

item.title: Instructions on Mindfulness of Mind States
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a practical talk on the third foundation of mindfulness, focusing on citta as mind-and-heart together and on how mind states shape thought, perception, speech, and action. He walks through ways to recognize a mind state through the body, thoughts, and felt texture of experience, then turns to gentle ways of working with difficult emotions without strengthening identification. The talk closes by widening into cultivation practices like metta, compassion, and concentration as ways of resetting habitual grooves and building capacity for both difficulty and joy.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Third Foundation Overview 03:20 Why Mind States Matter 06:18 Recognizing the State 10:36 Meeting Difficult Emotion 16:41 Insight and Non-Solidity 21:44 Skillful Responses 25:18 Cultivating Wholesome States 28:21 Freedom and Capacity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14585/

item.title: Instructions on Mindfulness of the Body
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a practical talk on mindfulness of the body as the first foundation of mindfulness, beginning with the question of what practice is and why ways of looking matter so much. He moves from basic body awareness and clear comprehension into close investigation of sensation, then into working skillfully with pain, pleasure, and ordinary bodily experience. The talk closes by showing how attention to impermanence and not-self can make the body less a problem to solve and more a refuge and a meditation theme.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Practice Is 06:06 Body as Foundation 09:44 Close Bodily Attention 13:02 Working with Pain 20:07 Pleasure and Collectedness 21:44 Ordinary Sensation 23:20 Impermanence and Not-Self 26:08 The Body as Theme
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14584/

item.title: Am I Free to Inquire?
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores inquiry as both intimacy and questioning: how attention draws close to experience, and how Dharma practice can keep asking what suffering is, how it is constructed, and what blocks freedom. He contrasts living inquiry with rote certainty, using the Buddha, Einstein, Jung, and others to show that real practice needs curiosity, passion, and a willingness to question assumptions. The talk widens from personal practice into ideas, culture, and frameworks, asking what we trust, what we ignore, and whether liberation itself may be more open-ended than we usually suppose.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Inquiry as intimacy 03:43 Questions that open 08:05 Experiment and possibility 17:01 What blocks questioning 24:53 Ideas shape experience 32:48 Passion for truth 41:05 Comfort, certainty, and dissonance 55:28 Frameworks and observation 01:02:53 Cracking open the vessel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13847/

item.title: A Mystical Kiss (...for Mystics, Anti-Mystics, Lovers and Realists...)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores mysticism as a matter of perception rather than belief, moving from dictionary definitions and devotional traditions to the role of sanctification, wonder, and gratitude. He contrasts ordinary consensus reality with Buddhist emptiness and modern physics, suggesting that different ways of looking shape what we call real. The talk closes by returning to practice: learning to see with new eyes, so that letting go, love, and reverence can change the heart and the world we inhabit.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 02:06 What mysticism means 04:21 Via negativa and via positiva 09:09 Sanctifying ordinary life 14:17 Gratitude and wonder 21:09 Bias, doubt, and science 30:33 Ways of looking 43:56 Devotion as deconstruction 53:32 New eyes and emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13846/

item.title: Working with the Emotional Body (Instructions: Day Five)
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a practical talk on resourcing difficult emotions through awareness, the body, silence, and the emotional field. He moves from revisiting the spacious awareness practice into a wide set of supports: friendship, calm states, compassion, warmth, and imagination. The talk then turns to subtle ways difficulty is maintained, including judgment, contraction, isolation, and a lack of contact with our own strength and life force.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing Spacious Awareness 05:21 External and Inner Resources 07:55 Space, Silence, and Body 10:43 Meeting Pain Directly 13:19 Warmth Around Difficulty 16:24 Allowing and Softening 18:40 Not Alone in Suffering 21:37 Strength, Power, Desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13851/

item.title: At Home in the Universe
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores what it might mean to feel at home in existence, starting from modern alienation and moving through self-relationship, community, the body, the earth, and the mind. He then widens the frame into death, big-picture views, and a participatory universe, using both Dharma and modern physics to question the sense that reality is separate from the way we see it. The talk closes with a quiet but radical claim: that non-separateness and emptiness can make home possible even in what seems difficult or impure.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Alienation Everywhere 04:16 Home With Experience 07:59 Sangha and Trust 13:52 Body and Elements 19:55 Earth, Convenience, Pilgrimage 27:34 Identity and Social Pressure 39:25 Death and Big Picture 49:15 Mystical Home 59:52 Space, Physics, Emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13848/

item.title: The Meditator as Revolutionary
item.description:
Rob Burbea asks what practice is actually for, then widens the question beyond private calm to the social and ecological conditions shaping suffering. He moves from spiritual archetypes and the fantasy of awakening to climate change, consumer culture, and the ethical responsibilities that come with meditation. By the end, the talk argues for a Dharma that can hold grief, resist numbness, and make room for a larger love and a longer view.
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed

item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Rob Burbea asks what practice is actually for, then widens the question beyond private calm to the social and ecological conditions shaping suffering. He moves from spiritual archetypes and the fantasy of awakening to climate change, consumer culture, and the ethical responsibilities that come with meditation. By the end, the talk argues for a Dharma that can hold grief, resist numbness, and make room for a larger love and a longer view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What practice is for 03:06 Archetypes of awakening 06:13 Sensitivity and suffering 12:15 Widening the picture 25:41 Values in a global age 33:58 Culture shapes the mind 43:14 Turning in and out 55:34 Justice, courage, and long view 01:08:05 Creative maladjustment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13850/

item.title: Reflections on the Retreat and on the Path
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this retreat by asking what it means to be genuinely on retreat: not self-improvement, but a gift of kindness, safety, and time to hear deeper longing. He moves through the tension between making practice special and keeping it ordinary, then turns to emotions as the retreat’s main field of work. Along the way he frames meditation as developing ways of seeing and relating that can be carried into daily life, and he closes by sketching the retreat’s mix of heart, intellect, imagination, and constructive questioning.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Welcome and Context 02:41 Practice as Kindness 05:18 Making It Sacred 08:57 Longing and Aspiration 13:33 Desire and Self-Care 16:58 Working with Emotion 21:28 What Meditation Is For 25:41 Portable Skill and Confidence 30:20 Views, Strife, and the Path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13849/

item.title: For the Sake of Life on Earth, part 2
item.description:
Joanna Macy continues a talk on the work that reconnects, moving from honoring grief for the world toward seeing the earth as a larger living body. She reads Rilke and Big Bill Neidjie, then shifts into a guided outdoor sensing practice meant to soften separation and renew belonging. In the final movement, she turns to urgency, discipline, and collective action, arguing that our love for the world asks for serious change rather than comfort.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Spiral 00:44 Honoring Pain 04:21 Great Unraveling 07:17 Seeing With New Eyes 12:35 Through the Separate Self 17:51 Earth as Kin 22:03 Guided Sensing Practice 28:10 Going Forth 36:32 Courage and Discipline
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13572/

item.title: For The Sake of Life on Earth, part 1
item.description:
Joanna Macy begins with gratitude that does not depend on things going well, then moves from personal appreciation into the larger work of facing ecological and social breakdown without turning away. She shares stories from Rocky Flats and the tar sands, and uses the Work That Reconnects and the Shambhala warrior prophecy to frame courage, compassion, and interdependence as practical responses to our time. The talk closes with a reading of Rilke that holds darkness as something that can be transformed into strength.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Gratitude Beyond Circumstance 01:33 Facing the World Clearly 08:25 Grounded in the Work 11:54 Shambhala Prophecy 20:10 Suffering with the World 24:38 Tar Sands and Interdependence 35:51 Compassion Is Strength 39:12 Rilke's Bell Tower
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13571/

item.title: Healing Ecology - A Buddhist Perspective on the Eco Crisis
item.description:
David Loy explores Buddhism and ecology through a sustained parallel between personal suffering and the eco-crisis. He moves from the Buddha’s relationship to trees and the idea of anatta, into social construction, technological growth, and the way modern civilization tries to secure itself against lack. The talk ends by widening into evolution, cosmic creativity, and a question of whether humanity can become a collective bodhisattva for the biosphere.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 03:50 Trees and early Buddhism 09:22 Self and separation 14:56 Nonduality with earth 24:46 Civilization as construct 36:35 Growth and control 55:03 Cosmic creativity 01:05:05 Practice and transformation 01:19:46 Questions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13394/

item.title: Questioning Reality (Question and Answer Session 5)
item.description:
In this question-and-answer session, Rob Burbea explores how emptiness practice can feel freeing and unsettling at the same time, especially when it touches thought, emotion, and the sense of self. He keeps returning to balance: when to soften with metta, when to stay with anatta practice, and how to avoid confusing emptiness with flatness or disconnection. The talk also widens into how perception, knowing, and clinging all shape the way reality is built in experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the questions 05:07 Balancing with metta 11:46 What disidentifying means 17:17 Perception and knowing 24:12 Anatta and ownership 28:45 Trees, beauty, and fading 33:13 Flatness or aversion 46:22 Healthy clinging and desire 59:59 Three angles of practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12517/

item.title: Ways of Looking: (3) Anatta
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces anatta as a practical way of looking rather than a philosophy to believe. He moves from the Buddha’s refusal to answer self/non-self questions directly, into the five aggregates, and then into a series of hands-on contemplations showing how identification loosens with body sensations, feelings, thoughts, and even awareness itself. The talk closes with cautions about not turning the practice into aversion or self-suppression, and with a light, experimental tone about trying it in daily life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The middle way 03:03 How self gets built 07:19 Practicing disidentification 12:26 Five aggregates map 16:02 Light labels and practice 20:16 Body and ownership 27:15 Expanding the range 34:06 Common pitfalls 40:36 Playful experimentation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12530/

item.title: Deepening Into Emptiness (Question and Answer Session 4)
item.description:
In this Q&A, Rob Burbea works through vedana, aversion, clinging, and the felt sense of self, using concrete examples from sitting, walking, and pain. He keeps returning to how allowing, curiosity, and metta reveal deeper layers of experience, including space, love, and a quieter sense of being held. The talk then opens into emptiness: not as a doctrine to cling to, but as a way of seeing how self and phenomena arise dependently and can soften or dissolve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and metta 03:08 Allowing the whole package 07:29 Loops of aversion 11:55 Clinging and pain 16:45 Lack and delusion 24:49 Body, self, and spaciousness 35:12 What emptiness means 48:19 Constructing self and phenomena 01:03:29 Dependent co-arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12520/

item.title: Ways of Looking: (2) Dukkha
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues the series on ways of looking by turning to dukkha, or unsatisfactoriness, and showing how this lens supports letting go rather than aversion. He moves from the basic idea that conditioned things cannot fully satisfy us, to more practical instructions: noticing bodily contraction, relaxing grasping, using impermanence and dissolution, and then working with allowing or welcoming experience. The talk closes by contrasting direct and open modes of practice, and by framing release as a kind of non-doing that is held in balance with metta.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing ways of looking 01:44 Dukkha as unsatisfactory 04:22 Meister Eckhart's parallel 06:48 Not limiting life 12:55 Feeling push and pull 17:47 Using impermanence 19:49 Allowing and welcoming 25:17 Release and subtle dukkha 29:20 Direct or open practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12531/

item.title: Impermanence, Love, Emptiness (Question and Answer Session 3)
item.description:
In this question-and-answer session, Rob Burbea responds to a listener’s fear that deepening insight into impermanence and emptiness can feel like the ground dropping away. He works carefully with that fear, then turns to how love, consciousness, and space can be experienced as vast, while still remaining provisional ways of looking rather than final answers. The talk closes with practical guidance on effort, including how to notice self-definitions, use inquiry, and soften practice when it becomes too tight.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Fear at the edge 03:03 Groundlessness and fear 08:08 Kindness as cushion 14:21 Love and consciousness 27:20 Concepts as rafts 37:33 The question of effort 47:04 Turning inquiry inward 52:19 Self, moments, and change 01:01:18 Naming the self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12528/

item.title: Ways of Looking: (1) Anicca
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces anicca, impermanence, as a practice rather than a concept: a deliberate way of looking that notices flux in body, sound, taste, sight, feeling tone, thought, and intention. He shows how this kind of attention can soften the sense of a solid world and a fixed self, opening more space, letting go, and sometimes revealing metta as something already present rather than something manufactured. The talk includes several brief guided experiments, a short reference to the Buddha’s hesitation to teach dependent origination, and a Q&A about balancing impermanence practice with metta and direct contact.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 07:54 Three characteristics 11:40 Why this is subtle 14:22 Learning to notice change 23:55 Sense-door experiments 29:55 Seeing the visual field 33:30 Subtle change and intention 43:21 Letting go and self 50:42 Hard to overturn
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12529/

item.title: Contact and The Construction of Self
item.description:
Rob Burbea frames this talk around emptiness as the seeing of self-construction: how suffering, comparison, bodily contraction, and belief all participate in building a self. He then lays out two practical responses—loosening the views that feed construction, and staying at contact with bare attention—using simple examples from sound, bodily pain, and the retreat setting. Later he turns to vedana, craving, and the way labels and reactivity can thicken experience, while also pointing to the quieter relief and luminosity that can appear when the mind stops adding so much.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 01:35 Noticing construction 04:04 Views and attention 09:59 Body and aversion 15:28 Comparison and conceit 20:32 Bare contact practice 25:51 Openness and luminosity 31:15 Addiction to self-making 41:50 Vedana and listening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12513/

item.title: Question and Answer Session 2
item.description:
In this question-and-answer session, Rob Burbea works gently with the practical realities of retreat: irritation, self-judgment, and how to meet difficulty without making it harder. He keeps returning to a few linked themes—metta as listening, compassion as a natural response when metta meets pain, and the way spaciousness changes what can be held. In the second half, the talk opens into emptiness, fabrication, and dependent origination, with metta, gratitude, and even jhana presented as ways of loosening fixed views and softening the self-sense.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 05:16 Working with difficulty 15:52 Metta as listening 21:43 Intensity and spaciousness 28:43 From pain to compassion 38:20 Devotion to kindness 43:53 Metta and perception 01:02:05 Emptiness and jhana 01:09:16 Fabrication and dependent arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12525/

item.title: Ending Blame
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores how blame narrows experience into a self-centered story, and how looking more closely reveals a web of conditions instead. He moves through everyday examples—mistakes, tiredness, food, mindfulness, relationships, and even difficult therapy experiences—to show how self-blame and blame of others can be softened by a wider view. The talk gradually brings together emptiness and metta, suggesting that repeated practice can loosen shame and make the heart and mind more flexible.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the problem 07:22 Conditions, not self 15:59 Food and fear 19:22 Relationships as dynamics 27:34 Re-viewing the past 38:05 Avijja and meaning 41:50 Metta softens the view 47:48 Practice, shame, and power
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12514/

item.title: Freeing the Self
item.description:
Rob Burbea frames the talk around emptiness as something to be approached through lived practice rather than abstract philosophy. He explores how rigid self-definitions create suffering, how loosening them can bring relief and more love, and how the sense of self changes across situations, moods, and meditation states. The talk ends by holding self and emptiness as complementary ways of seeing, each useful when applied with care.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 00:59 Four Noble Truths 04:55 Middle way and emptiness 06:58 Practicing with selflessness 09:47 Responses to emptiness 13:33 Working with self-definitions 21:53 Love and relief 31:34 Stories of hidden dimensions 41:53 Sense of self and balance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12521/

item.title: Opening Talk for Metta and Emptiness (Level 1): Being on Retreat
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a retreat on metta and emptiness by framing practice as a way of seeing existence differently, not as a project of becoming someone special. He moves from retreat logistics into a careful exploration of support, silence, community, and the subtle ways practice can create splits if kindness and emptiness are not fully inclusive. The talk ends in a call-and-response inquiry into the words emptiness, metta, kindness, and love, followed by a short settling meditation that treats receptivity itself as a form of kindness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:24 Opening Welcome 01:53 Freedom Through Seeing 04:51 Two Wings Of Practice 05:57 Emptiness And Inclusion 11:29 Retreat As Holding 17:20 No Splitting 25:01 Community And Form 38:48 Practical Grounding 54:46 Inquiry And Sitting
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12523/

item.title: Instructions on the Mindfulness of Thought
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers practical instructions for working with thought in meditation without demonizing it. He distinguishes helpful and unhelpful uses of thinking, shows how thought and mind state reinforce each other, and points to ways of loosening the grip of obsessive or self-critical loops. The talk ends with a short guided sitting that returns to the body, the breath, and a more spacious way of noticing thoughts arise and pass.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Thought Matters 04:25 Mind States and Feedback 07:36 Returning to the Anchor 09:03 Metta and Emotion 12:07 Body as Vantage Point 15:22 From Content to Process 17:19 Thoughts Arise and Pass 19:43 Space and Freedom 22:43 Guided Sitting
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11967/

item.title: Ending the Inner Critic (3)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of the inner critic by mapping it as a conditioned loop of self-view, selective attention, and interpretation. He then turns to several practical shifts: taking in beauty and ethics, aligning with deepest aspiration, seeing experience as a web of conditions rather than a fixed self, and loosening the authority we give to thought. The talk moves from diagnosis to practice and ends with a quiet confidence that these patterns can be weakened and, over time, ended.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing the methods 03:02 The self-reinforcing loop 03:39 Shifting attention 04:47 Beauty and ethics 11:01 Deep aspiration 15:11 Seeing conditions 18:25 The web of causes 25:01 Thought and gullibility 31:44 Freedom is possible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/35160/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of the inner critic by mapping it as a conditioned loop of self-view, selective attention, and interpretation. He then turns to several practical shifts: taking in beauty and ethics, aligning with deepest aspiration, seeing experience as a web of conditions rather than a fixed self, and loosening the authority we give to thought. The talk moves from diagnosis to practice and ends with a quiet confidence that these patterns can be weakened and, over time, ended.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing the methods 03:02 The self-reinforcing loop 03:39 Shifting attention 04:47 Beauty and ethics 11:01 Deep aspiration 15:11 Seeing conditions 18:25 The web of causes 25:01 Thought and gullibility 31:44 Freedom is possible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/35160/

item.title: Ending the Inner Critic (2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues an exploration of ways to meet the inner critic without feeding it more aversion. He moves from loving-kindness and mindful befriending into space, bodily tenderness, and investigation, then shows how questioning can tease apart the critic’s parts and beliefs. In the latter half, he turns to dialogue, insight, and reclaiming power through inner rebellion, embodied strength, and sometimes through opening the heart rather than fighting harder.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the approach list 00:45 Mindfulness with kindness 03:25 Space and bodily tenderness 06:16 Investigating what is there 10:33 What the critic believes 15:49 Teasing apart the dots 19:48 Dialogue and skeptical inquiry 25:33 Protection, insight, and practice 30:59 Reclaiming power
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/35161/

item.title: Ending the Inner Critic (1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the inner critic as a learned pattern of shame, pressure, and self-judgment that can quietly shape practice, relationships, and the questions we bring to life. He argues that this pattern is understandable in family, schooling, and consumer culture, but not fixed, and he shows how it can be challenged through kinder questioning and metta. The talk then turns toward practical ways of loosening the critic, including loving-kindness, receiving support from others, and learning to see oneself more truly.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What the critic is 02:49 Not alone in this 05:49 Where it comes from 08:45 How practice gets pressured 12:07 Judgment blocks inquiry 15:00 Acceptance before inquiry 19:15 Challenging the critic 23:50 Metta as first practice 30:19 Love from others
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/35159/

item.title: The Psychodynamics in Meditation (Part 3 - Dimensions of the Personal Journey)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how meditation can work with emotion, view, and identity in a flexible way rather than a single fixed method. He moves from practical ways of meeting difficult feelings, through emptiness and the Four Noble Truths, to a larger question: whether the personality itself can be a site of ongoing unfoldment and freedom. The talk closes by contrasting traditional not-self practice with a more affirmative way of relating to the personality as a living thread of discovery.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:05 Flexibility with emotion 05:22 Resources already in the field 08:58 Fabrication and emptiness 13:42 Milarepa's example 16:15 Four Noble Truths 18:24 The power of emptiness 26:53 Personality as journey 34:47 What locks it down 47:23 What unlocks it
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11504/

item.title: The Psychodynamics in Meditation (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues his exploration of psychodynamics in meditation by reviewing a set of ways emotional life can become blocked and then opened again. He moves from differentiation and questioning into resourcing through cultivation, inner images, and story, with a strong emphasis on feeling experience in the body and lingering with what arises. The talk ends by widening the sense of practice itself: not only staying with emotions, but letting inquiry, love, and the whole life story become part of the path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:17 Reviewing the framework 04:12 Differentiation and attention 06:47 Questioning assumptions 10:23 Why inquiry needs love 17:22 Cultivating qualities 20:18 Finding inner resources 28:01 Yearning and hidden strength 36:55 Working with imagery 47:31 Using story and seeing anew
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11505/

item.title: The Psychodynamics in Meditation (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how emotional life is conditioned and dynamic, arguing that stuckness usually means some combination of attention, meaning, belief, and bodily response is locking experience in place. He then offers several practical principles for loosening that knot: differentiating strands of feeling, sustaining focus, broadening awareness, questioning assumptions, and exploring what is actually happening in the body and heart. The talk moves through concrete teaching stories—a difficult inner rant, a bike ride that softens sadness, and a romance shaped by fear—to show how inquiry can reveal energy, tenderness, and a wider sense of self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Emotions as dynamic 06:37 What blocks unfolding 20:01 Gathering attention 25:30 Broadening the field 33:48 Ranting and voice 40:01 Questioning assumptions 47:02 Self-view and fear 56:58 Body and self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11503/

item.title: The Mystery of Vedana: A Key to Liberation
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores vedana, the felt tone present in every moment of experience, and shows how attention to it can interrupt the usual drift into grasping, aversion, and story-making. He moves from a practical instruction to stay with bare sensation into subtler questions about impermanence, dependent origination, and whether experience is as fixed or separate as it seems. The talk closes by widening from skillful simplification to a more radical inquiry into emptiness and the nature of reality.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why vedana matters 05:28 Stationing attention 10:21 Three feeling tones 16:25 Reactivity and the second arrow 31:22 Stories that proliferate 39:45 Staying with bare feeling 47:55 Changing the relationship 55:17 From experience to emptiness 01:06:13 Come and see
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11502/
