item.title: Selflessness and a Life of Love
item.description:
Rob Burbea reflects on mortality, apathy, and the question of how to live a life of love. He moves through social and historical examples of responsibility and care, then turns toward emptiness as a way of loosening fixed self-view and allowing love to become steadier and less fearful. The talk ends by showing how metta, spaciousness, and the perception of emptiness can support a more radical and enduring compassion.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Mortality and the question 05:38 Against docility 11:26 Radical teachers of love 16:49 Responsibility in the world 23:42 Hope, long view, and urgency 31:09 Emptiness and perception 41:23 Loosening self and other 52:38 Immeasurable heart 01:01:47 Radical love, quietly possible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9989/

item.title: Heart Work
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores two sides of caring for ourselves: learning to meet difficult emotions skillfully, and learning how metta can be nourished, deepened, and made more spacious. He treats mechanical practice as normal, then moves into a detailed discussion of fear, vulnerability, judgment, body-based softening, and practical ways to reframe the inner response to suffering. Later he shifts toward what feeds loving-kindness — wonder, spaciousness, intention, and everyday action — before closing with Roz Savage’s story as a concrete example of how small, repeated choices can reshape a life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Metta Has Nuance 03:24 Three Practice Modes 06:08 Caring for Suffering 12:48 Fear and Opening 18:44 Judgment and Space 23:14 Seven Skillful Responses 32:25 Story and Sensation 40:37 Feeding Metta 54:16 Intention and Change
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9982/

item.title: About Metta Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces a retreat on metta by placing it inside the wider Buddhist path: awakening, the roots of suffering, and the cultivation of beautiful qualities. He explains loving-kindness as boundless, unconditional well-wishing that can nourish the heart, soften self-judgment, and gradually change how we see the world. The talk moves from a conceptual overview into a communal reflection and then into a long opening practice that begins with the body, expands to others in the room, and settles into gentle wishing well.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Welcome to Retreat 01:37 Awakening and Suffering 04:42 Two Wings of the Path 06:28 What Metta Means 11:16 Boundless and Strong 18:37 Nourishment and Skill 29:00 Cultivation and Intention 37:35 Bodhicitta and Widening 46:03 Shared Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9988/

item.title: The Union of Appearance and Emptiness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores emptiness as something that changes how experience is built, seen, and valued. Using food, mindfulness, and equanimity as everyday examples, he shows how solidity can fade and how appearances may be recognized as empty without becoming meaningless. The talk then turns to the philosophical tensions this raises in the Buddhist traditions, especially the middle way between nihilism and reification.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the spiral 02:11 How solidity is built 05:15 Mindfulness and fading 12:02 What does fading mean? 17:49 Canon and cessation 23:34 No rebirth of appearances 31:47 Theravada and Mahayana 44:32 The razor's edge 59:07 Freedom with cause and effect
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9554/

item.title: Dependent Cessation and the Unconditioned
item.description:
Rob Burbea uses this talk to distinguish deep insight into dependent cessation from simply having unusual meditative experiences. He moves from the place of emptiness as a tool in practice to a careful reading of nibbana as the unconditioned, the unfabricated, and the deathless, drawing on repeated sutta passages and a Nagarjuna citation. The talk stays grounded in practice: understanding, fading, non-clinging, and the gradual unbinding of self, world, and time.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Practice as tools 05:48 Many strands of practice 11:19 Reasoning for freedom 14:05 What nibbana means 19:47 Transcendent or not 29:50 Fading and samadhi 38:09 Dependent cessation 49:10 Language and footing 56:33 Unbinding and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9541/

item.title: The Subtlety of Dependent Origination
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores dependent origination through the lens of time, showing how past, future, and even the present can lose their apparent solidity in meditation. He connects this to selfing, object-perception, attention, and the subtle ways dukkha is built into experience, then uses Nagarjuna, Gampopa, Ajahn Mun, Mipham, and the Buddha’s awakening story to press the analysis deeper. The talk stays practical throughout: the aim is not abstraction, but usable ways of seeing emptiness that soften grasping and open freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Map 02:17 Why Time Matters 04:08 Time and Clinging 09:40 Past, Future, Present 13:21 Seeing Through Now 20:44 Nagarjuna on Arising 26:13 Self, Thing, Time 33:41 The Tripod of Dukkha 46:09 Attention and Conceiving
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9553/

item.title: No Mind
item.description:
Rob Burbea lays out a map of practice centered on whether awareness itself is being reified. He moves from the ordinary sense of mind as a mirror or vast space, through passages from the Pali Canon and Mahayana texts, toward a more searching inquiry into how knowing and the known depend on each other. The talk stays close to meditation practice: what helps at one stage, what is only provisional, and how deeper seeing can lead to less clinging, more freedom, and more love.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the map 07:38 Levels of practice 11:46 Awareness and suffering 17:51 The center of knowing 27:31 Luminous but not final 35:31 Translations and suttas 48:16 Looking for mind 01:01:04 Mutual dependence 01:09:48 Letting consciousness fade
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9547/

item.title: Love, Healing, and Emptiness
item.description:
This talk explores how emptiness and love belong together rather than opposing each other. Rob Burbea moves from the practical feel of metta and compassion into a fuller account of dependent origination, showing how self-view, perception, and karma shape both suffering and the world we inhabit. He also revisits healing, anger, and the Mahayana language of bodhicitta to suggest that letting go can make care more spacious, not less.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Emptiness and love 03:26 Rumi and purification 08:19 Heart practices and self 14:47 Giving back and anger 23:56 Categories and perception 33:12 Mara and dependent arising 47:08 Healing and fabrication 58:37 Three levels of compassion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9545/

item.title: 'To see no-thing is to see excellently'
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the emptiness of phenomena, moving from the felt fading of body, feeling, perception, and emotion into more analytic Madhyamaka reasoning. He repeatedly returns to how experience is fabricated by the mind, why that matters in meditation, and how seeing the interdependence of parts, wholes, and consciousness can loosen suffering. The talk stays practical even when it becomes philosophically rigorous, ending with the quiet claim that seeing 'no thing' can be a deeper way of seeing reality.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the field 02:20 Phenomena are empty 09:43 Dependent arising deepens 18:47 Fading and fabrication 33:27 Porridge, body, and parts 40:24 Ways of looking 44:40 Aggregates arise together 50:50 Walking and motion 01:02:36 Seeing no thing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9556/

item.title: Maps for the Journey (A Brief Overview)
item.description:
This talk is a compact review of the retreat’s main thread: samatha, the three characteristics, open awareness, and the move into emptiness. Rob Burbea keeps returning to the same practical question—what, exactly, are we doing in meditation, and how does that change suffering, perception, and self-sense? He then sketches a larger map, from ordinary selfing to the subtler fading of objects, time, and duality, while stressing that wholesome and unwholesome still matter.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the Review 02:25 Samatha and Looking 05:51 Open Awareness and Methods 11:20 Finding What Works 15:13 Levels of Selfing 20:32 Phenomenology and Emptiness 26:29 Maps of Meditative Absorption 34:06 Pain, Pīti, and Sound 41:24 Grass, Field, and Projection
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9546/

item.title: Non-Duality and the Fading of Perception
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how dependent origination can be understood through the fading of perception, not just through the usual language of impermanence. He moves from the Buddha’s riddle about the end of the world into examples of dualities, clinging, and the way experience becomes more or less solid in meditation. The talk stays close to practice throughout, testing the claims against bodily pain, fear, attention, and the subtle sense of self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening intention 01:19 The end of the world 03:20 Dependent origination broadened 08:00 Dualities in experience 17:38 Practice without preferences 21:33 Clinging and solidity 28:35 From non-clinging to fading 41:37 Web of causes 50:34 Spectrum of fading
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9548/

He moves from the Buddha’s riddle about the end of the world into examples of dualities, clinging, and the way experience becomes more or less solid in meditation. The talk stays close to practice throughout, testing the claims against bodily pain, fear, attention, and the subtle sense of self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening intention 01:19 The end of the world 03:20 Dependent origination broadened 08:00 Dualities in experience 17:38 Practice without preferences 21:33 Clinging and solidity 28:35 From non-clinging to fading 41:37 Web of causes 50:34 Spectrum of fading
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9548/

item.title: Emptiness and the Vastness of Awareness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores emptiness as a practical path to less suffering, starting from the quieting of selfing and moving toward the emptiness of phenomena, awareness, and space itself. He repeatedly returns to the morning meditation, using it as a basis for showing how letting go can reveal a more spacious, less solid way of experiencing. The talk also distinguishes helpful provisional views from final claims, especially in the discussion of Chittamatra or mind-only teaching, while warning against reifying any one meditative state.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:14 Retreat context 09:45 Dependence and solidity 12:14 Three characteristics 15:58 Fabrication and space 22:55 Awareness as backdrop 34:18 Flavors and conditioning 44:55 Mind-only lens 59:28 Not the final truth 01:09:31 Questions and application
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9542/

item.title: Concepts, Views, Reality
item.description:
Rob Burbea examines how concepts, views, and assumptions shape experience, then asks whether bare attention and “not thinking” are enough for genuine insight. He contrasts simplified Dharma readings with a more careful account of emptiness, dependent arising, and the way self and world are constructed through clinging. The talk moves between canonical references, examples from Western Dharma, and practical cautions about balance, ethics, and the heart’s response to inquiry.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Terrain 01:51 Views Shape Experience 06:44 Bare Attention And Suchness 16:37 Beyond Impermanence 27:32 Concepts That Undo Themselves 41:56 Right View, Not No View 54:02 Ethics And The Limits Of Relativism 01:04:43 Emptiness And Dependent Arising 01:16:18 Mutual Dependence And Reverence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9540/

item.title: The Seven-Fold Reasoning (The self cannot be found anywhere)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces the sevenfold reasoning as a practical Madhyamaka method for investigating the self, starting from the felt sense of an inherently existing “I” and moving through seven ways it cannot be found. He emphasizes that this is not philosophy alone: the reasoning is meant to be taken into meditation until it gives way to a quiet, meaningful emptiness rather than a blank or abstract conclusion. Along the way he contrasts this approach with other ways of working with anatta, touches Chandrakirti, the Pali Canon, Nagarjuna, and related Indian and Tibetan self-theories, and keeps returning to the question of what actually holds up in direct experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why This Matters 02:02 From Anatta to Inquiry 04:58 Chandrakirti and the Chariot 09:38 Preparing the Mind 20:20 Three Steps of Practice 29:43 The First Two Reasonings 42:13 Body, Feeling, and Possession 01:06:44 Continuity and Consciousness 01:28:05 Emptiness, Not Blankness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9552/

item.title: A Wise Relationship To Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how the attitude we bring to practice shapes everything that follows: whether practice feels like kindness, self-measurement, effort, or a living relationship. He moves through balanced effort, aspiration, confidence, joy, rest, and the ways practice can be distorted by impatience, pride, fear, or a cramped sense of progress. Later he turns to emptiness and anatta as ways of looking, emphasizing that the point is not technical correctness but whether the practice brings relief, reduces clinging, and opens more freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 04:20 Practice as kindness 06:43 What effort means 08:54 Aspiration and balance 13:37 Confidence and progress 17:38 Deepening without strain 30:17 Difficulty as fertilizer 46:36 Keeping practice alive 01:07:36 Anatta as a way of looking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9538/

item.title: Three Characteristics - Three Avenues to Freedom and Joy
item.description:
Rob Burbea frames the Buddha’s teaching as a practical response to unnecessary suffering, then shows how the three characteristics function as meditative lenses rather than bleak doctrines. He moves through impermanence, dukkha, and not-self as ways of seeing that loosen grasping, reduce selfing, and reveal more freedom and joy. Later he distinguishes this approach from darker commentarial readings and from a purely philosophical reading of emptiness, returning again and again to the felt effect of letting go.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Practice Matters 04:20 Three Lenses 08:24 Dukkha and Letting Go 11:51 Not-Self in Practice 21:28 Working With Difficulty 30:25 Impermanence, Emptiness, Suffering 37:39 Relational Craving 51:55 Self, Strategy, and Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9555/

item.title: The Experience Of Self (Personality and Beyond)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores personal selflessness as a practical inquiry rather than a metaphysical claim. He traces how self is built through story, comparison, inner criticism, and clinging, then shows how meditation can reveal a wide spectrum from gross selfing to quieter, more spacious experience. The talk stays grounded in everyday relationship, psychotherapy, and retreat practice, while returning repeatedly to the Buddha’s shift from self-view to actions and conditions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing selflessness 01:26 Self and suffering 05:11 Healing the person 08:16 Story and identity 12:25 Personality-level suffering 17:11 Questioning definitions 20:30 The spectrum of self 25:34 Meditation as selfing 33:45 Appreciation and familiarization
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9551/

item.title: Samadhi and Insight (a few pointers)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces samadhi as more than narrow concentration: a felt sense of unification, steadiness, well-being, warmth, and bodily ease. He places it alongside emptiness practice on the retreat, arguing that the two support each other—samadhi makes the mind subtler and more pliable, while insight loosens fabrication and helps samadhi deepen. The talk is practical throughout, with advice about hindrances, tightness, body awareness, metta, and how to keep practice from shrinking into self-judgment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 What Samadhi Means 02:45 Parallel Retreat Practices 05:13 Why It Matters 09:42 Well-Being and Cushioning 17:50 Working With Hindrances 26:13 Attention and the Body 34:45 Body, Energy, and Letting Go 42:09 Balancing Samadhi and Insight 46:54 Samadhi and Emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9550/

item.title: An Introduction to Emptiness
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces emptiness as something enlivening rather than bleak, and spends the talk showing how the mind adds solidity to self, emotion, relationship, and even social convention. He distinguishes simple mindfulness from deliberate contemplation, then uses many concrete examples to show how experience is imputed and how suffering loosens when that fabrication is seen. The talk moves from early Buddhist and Madhyamaka sources toward a careful middle way: not nihilism, not reification, but a freedom that should deepen compassion, ethics, and love.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 05:46 Why emptiness matters 12:44 What emptiness means 19:45 Root delusion 24:15 Everyday fabrication 31:18 Relationship and attention 39:14 Dot-to-dot experience 49:00 Dependent arising and mind 01:04:39 Middle way and practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9539/

item.title: Opening Talk - Orienting and Relating to the Emptiness Retreat
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a four-week emptiness retreat by orienting participants to the tone, purpose, and practical shape of the retreat. He frames emptiness as central to the Buddha’s teaching, then spends much of the talk on how to meet the retreat: with experiment, patience, humility, and a willingness to find what actually works. He repeatedly links emptiness with love, compassion, aspiration, the precepts, and the shared atmosphere of the retreat, including a candle ritual meant to keep heartfulness present.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Welcome and logistics 02:49 Emptiness at the center 05:57 How to meet retreat 13:42 Finding your approach 19:03 Difficulty and openness 28:05 Keeping the heart juicy 33:17 Freedom and love 38:28 The retreat container 42:52 Aspirations and precepts
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9549/

item.title: 'There are hundreds of ways to kneel and kiss the ground' (A Talk on Devotion)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores devotion as a broad human capacity rather than a narrow religious style, moving from bows, ritual, and devotional imagery into questions of aspiration, longing, and surrender. He draws on Buddhism, Christianity, Mary Oliver, Rumi, and a story from the Copenhagen climate talks to show how devotion can become a way of seeing, serving, and aligning with what matters most. The talk keeps returning to a simple question: what are we bowing to, and how does that change the life we live?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Heart 03:07 Many Forms of Devotion 12:38 Ritual and Culture 18:19 Attention as Prayer 23:15 Pilgrimage in Action 31:44 Objects and Embodiment 41:13 Longing as Message 45:50 Feeding the Flame 53:00 Letting Go of Forms
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10029/

item.title: Emptiness and Insight Meditation
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores emptiness as a practical way of seeing how suffering is built, not just as philosophy. He begins with the Buddha’s focus on dukkha and its root in delusion, then shows how mindfulness can reveal the mind’s habit of constructing selves, feelings, objects, and even time as if they had fixed essence. The talk gradually moves from ordinary examples and retreat life into a subtler view: letting go can make experience less solid, and emptiness is presented as opening toward joy, compassion, and freedom rather than bleakness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The root of suffering 03:51 Delusion and inherent existence 10:18 What emptiness means 15:41 Insight and convention 22:08 Construction as practice 27:22 The golden question 30:10 Five ways in 39:37 From presence to letting go 49:20 Joy, compassion, and service
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10025/

item.title: The Wisdom of Non-Duality
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores non-duality as a family of teachings rather than a single doctrine, moving from ordinary dualities like pleasure and pain to subtler questions of subject and object, self and no-self, existence and non-existence. He repeatedly returns to a pragmatic test: whether a practice brings more freedom, ethics, compassion, ease, and clarity, and whether it reduces suffering rather than merely sounding profound. The talk also uses early Buddhist passages, Buddha nature language, and Rumi to show how different levels of non-dual understanding can function as stepping stones rather than final answers.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Non-Duality Means 04:17 Pragmatic Early Buddhism 07:47 What a Good Path Shows 12:16 Doing and Non-Doing 19:00 Buddha Nature and Longing 28:51 No-Self and Vastness 43:27 Rumi and the Middle Way 50:06 Dependent Arising Deepens 58:49 Training the View
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10028/

item.title: Emotions and Freedom
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores emotions and mind states as part of the Buddhist third foundation of mindfulness, using citta as both mind and heart. He moves from simple observation of feeling tone and bodily cues into a deeper inquiry into how relationship, aversion, craving, and interpretation shape what we experience. The talk gradually widens into the idea that emotions are conditioned and malleable, and that practice includes both being with experience and learning to cultivate wholesome states.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Citta and Mind States 01:31 What Mind States Include 04:13 Mind as Forerunner 08:49 Reading the Body 15:56 Intimacy and Delicacy 24:14 Feedback Loops 31:49 How We Relate 39:38 Fabrication and Cultivation 50:44 Freedom Through Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10024/

item.title: Truthfulness and Authenticity
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores truthfulness as something broader than simply not lying: a steady willingness to see clearly, speak carefully, and notice where fear, self-protection, or habit bends experience away from what is real. He moves from ordinary dishonesty and social pressure into self-deception, perception, and the ways the inner critic can quietly hijack practice. The talk closes by balancing compassion with discernment, and by asking what we really want, what we can trust, and how honesty can become a path into freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 02:15 Speaking Truly 08:25 Self-Deception and Bullshit 13:35 Perception and Context 20:18 Slow Changes, Shifting Minds 28:07 What Practice Is For 35:51 Deep Desire and Intention 44:06 The Inner Critic 51:51 Honesty, Care, and Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10030/

item.title: Mindfulness, Insight, Liberation
item.description:
Rob Burbea examines what mindfulness is for, and argues that it is not simply bare awareness, calm presence, or accepting whatever arises. He contrasts “bare attention” with the Buddha’s emphasis on appropriate attention, then shows how mindfulness works inside the larger frame of the path, the Four Noble Truths, and liberation. The talk gradually moves from practical distinctions to a subtler claim: the way we look helps fabricate experience, so insight practice is learning ways of looking that release clinging and reduce suffering.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What mindfulness means 03:23 Bare attention and its limits 08:13 Appropriate attention 12:40 Not the same as awakening 18:30 The Satipatthana Sutta 24:41 Mindfulness with an agenda 31:52 Four Noble Truths in practice 39:51 How perception is built 49:41 What real insight requires
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10027/

item.title: Joyful Effort
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores effort as something much subtler than grim willpower. He moves from aspiration and faith, through the Buddha’s teaching on right effort, to the ways self-judgment, rest, and skillful adjustment shape practice. The talk widens into bodhisattva-style dedication, then closes by returning to a balanced, quiet joy that comes from wholehearted engagement without pressure.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Effort Matters 04:18 Aspiration and Meaning 09:45 Faith and Capacity 18:49 Skill, Not Self 23:35 Heroic Dedication 27:16 Right Effort in Practice 36:48 Balancing the Energy 50:22 Joyful, Irreversible Effort
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10026/

item.title: Freedom, Reality, and the Razor's Edge
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how practice can move from obvious self-identification toward much subtler ways the mind builds reality. He contrasts immediacy, impermanence, spacious awareness, and finally dependent origination, returning again and again to the question of what is being fabricated and what it means to see through it. The talk is careful about its own limits: these are presented as skillful ways of looking, not final answers, and he repeatedly warns against turning them into nihilism or a new metaphysical ground.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Inquiry 02:05 Delusion Beneath Clinging 06:52 Quieting Story and Self 09:51 Ordinary Radiance 12:16 Impermanence and Groundlessness 18:56 Awareness as a Ground 30:21 Dependent Origination 44:00 Beyond Nihilism 50:32 The Razor's Edge
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9822/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Rob Burbea explores how practice can move from obvious self-identification toward much subtler ways the mind builds reality. He contrasts immediacy, impermanence, spacious awareness, and finally dependent origination, returning again and again to the question of what is being fabricated and what it means to see through it. The talk is careful about its own limits: these are presented as skillful ways of looking, not final answers, and he repeatedly warns against turning them into nihilism or a new metaphysical ground.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Inquiry 02:05 Delusion Beneath Clinging 06:52 Quieting Story and Self 09:51 Ordinary Radiance 12:16 Impermanence and Groundlessness 18:56 Awareness as a Ground 30:21 Dependent Origination 44:00 Beyond Nihilism 50:32 The Razor's Edge
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9822/

item.title: Breath and Space - Reactivity, Emptiness, Piti (4th Instructions)
item.description:
Rob Burbea first lays out how pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral experience routinely trigger grasping, aversion, and dullness, and he shows how that reactivity is where much of suffering actually lives. He then turns to breath practice as a way of cultivating comfort, ease, and pīti as a trainable resource, emphasizing steadiness, spreading, and a balanced relationship with pleasure rather than chasing intensity. Near the end he ties the whole practice back to dependent origination and emptiness, suggesting that even bodily pain can be seen differently when reactivity relaxes.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reactivity and suffering 02:30 Space and emptiness 05:28 Breath as resource 09:00 Colours of pleasure 12:05 Developing pīti 15:48 Enjoyment and skill 20:06 Dependent arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9814/

item.title: The Self and Its Search for Security
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the self’s search for security through money, self-view, and the deeper habit of solidifying identity. He moves from the obvious ways money can hook fear and status to a more subtle inquiry into scarcity, stewardship, and the possibility of a wise search for security in qualities of heart. The talk then turns inward, into how self-view is built from memory, mood, and conditions, and how practice can loosen that construction without denying ordinary usefulness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 03:03 Groundlessness and Money 07:58 Money as Flow 12:59 Scarcity and Distortion 21:44 Frog, Flood, and Stewardship 29:29 Wise Security 36:13 Self-View and Story 45:29 How Self Is Built 53:27 Putting It Down
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9819/

item.title: Breath Meditation (3rd Instructions)
item.description:
Rob Burbea frames breath meditation as an adaptable art shaped by changing mind states, not a fixed technique. He moves through common hindrances like dullness, restlessness, and thinking, then offers practical ways to respond through posture, breath, attention, and effort. The talk closes by widening the sense of the breath into a subtler energy field and inviting a lighter, more receptive way of practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Breath as an Art 02:50 Kindness and Orientation 04:05 Working with Hindrances 07:34 Responding in Practice 10:23 Adjusting Effort 14:14 Thought and Breath 20:08 Guided Practice 22:31 Settling Into Space
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9821/

item.title: Blessing Life - The Power of the Generous Heart
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores generosity as a full spiritual practice, not just a moral habit or a way of giving money. He moves from the Buddha’s guidance to laypeople, through the classical three kinds of giving, into the ways generosity can soften fear, support healing, and loosen the sense of self. The talk also draws on Jesus, Ajahn Chah, Shantideva, and other references to show how giving can become a path into freedom, abundance, and emptiness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Generosity in context 03:20 Three dimensions of giving 07:10 Giving beyond self-preoccupation 13:42 Fear, culture, and trust 24:19 Happiness and deepening 31:55 Training the heart 39:38 Practice, wisdom, and self 52:46 Freedom, emptiness, and release 01:05:26 Leap of trust
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9810/

item.title: Liberation From the Inner Critic
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the inner critic as a deeply conditioned pattern of shame, aversion, and self-judgment, especially in Western individualistic culture. He traces how it distorts practice into “should,” blocks inquiry, and narrows life into fear and perfectionism. The talk then turns toward practical ways of loosening it: loving-kindness, spacious and affectionate mindfulness, precise investigation, and seeing actions as arising from conditions rather than from a flawed essence.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Naming the critic 04:05 Where it comes from 07:01 How practice gets distorted 12:03 Metta as an ally 17:02 Friendly mindfulness 24:23 Precision and deconstruction 34:49 Questioning the story 47:00 From shame to action 59:59 Freedom and what remains
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9815/

item.title: Opening the Sense of What's Possible
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens a retreat talk by asking what it means to live from our deepest aspirations rather than from the many smaller intentions that quietly run our days. He balances the familiar Insight Meditation emphasis on meeting difficulty with a wider invitation toward freedom, bodhicitta, and a more expansive sense of what may be possible. Along the way he brings in the realities of retreat life, silence, ethical restraint, and the urgency of practice in relation to the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:05 Welcome and tone 01:38 Retreat as devotion 05:14 Aspirations and intentions 11:08 Insight and its limits 15:54 Freedom as duty 19:38 Beyond the self 27:41 World and responsibility 32:44 Blockages and confidence 40:16 Cultivation and investigation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9816/

item.title: Realizing the Nature of Mind
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores several ways meditators may experience the nature of mind: as mirror-like awareness, as rapidly arising moments, as vast space, and finally as something groundless and dependently made. He keeps returning to the practical question of how to investigate these views without freezing them into fixed beliefs, and how insight deepens when practice includes patience, precision, and doubt. The talk moves from useful meditative models to a more unsettling inquiry into how perception, time, and awareness are fabricated together.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:30 Mind as mirror 09:27 Momentary consciousness 11:34 Awareness as space 17:11 Attractive but incomplete 23:29 Questioning the view 33:18 Dependence and emptiness 41:29 Reasoning about time 53:19 Groundless mind
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6349/

item.title: No Self, No World
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores anatta as a practical way of loosening suffering, not as a metaphysical claim to win. He moves from selfing in story and memory to the subtler ways self and world are mutually constructed, showing how mindfulness, less clinging, and careful inquiry can reveal greater freedom. The talk ends by widening the frame to dependent origination, the fabricated nature of experience, and a non-nihilistic sense of beauty and reverence.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:42 Anatta as practice 02:24 Self and not-self 05:54 Healing through self 12:37 Examining identity 17:53 Mindfulness and story 21:41 Ways of conceiving 29:08 Self and world together 45:13 Fabrication and lessening 53:32 Emptiness beyond extremes
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6300/

item.title: Into the depths of Silence
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores silence as more than the absence of speech or sound: as something that can reveal what we truly care about, soften fear, and bring buried memories into a more healing relationship. He traces how silence can deepen into sensitivity, love, spaciousness, and eventually a loosening of the self’s usual grip on experience. The talk moves from practical ways of listening to silence toward a more radical inquiry into nothingness, freedom, and what is actually real.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 05:09 Silence as Threshold 11:16 Missing the Vastness 17:47 Listening to Silence 27:30 Neruda and Collective Stillness 29:26 Practice in the Field 39:53 Self, Reality, and Nothing 52:45 Beyond Measurement 57:47 Closing Silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6275/

item.title: What is ‘Insight’?
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a map of insight meditation, starting with a simple definition and then widening into personal, universal, and ultimate insight. He emphasizes that insight is not only something that arises in meditation, but something to be used, repeated, and developed as a way of seeing that reduces suffering. The talk moves from everyday examples and ethical practice into impermanence, unsatisfactoriness, not-self, emptiness, and the middle way beyond fixed views.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:14 Opening the map 03:36 What insight means 12:33 Universal truths 15:53 Relative and ultimate 20:49 Using and consolidating 26:09 Learning ways of seeing 31:25 Three characteristics 42:19 Beyond fabrication 52:52 Middle way and emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6254/

item.title: The Unfabricated, The Deathless...
item.description:
Rob Burbea maps the relationship between emptiness, the unfabricated, and the deathless by first widening the sense of practice itself: mindfulness, healing, relationship, creativity, and cultivated qualities all belong on the path. From there he turns to a careful distinction between ordinary open awareness, deep samadhi, and the more radical fading of subject, object, and time that the early texts gesture toward. The talk repeatedly returns to a practical warning: don’t pre-decide metaphysical questions or chase special experiences; instead, understand how clinging, selfing, and dependent origination shape what is known.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice as a wide path 01:28 What the path includes 06:47 Nirvana and the deathless 13:20 Not pre-deciding truth 17:00 Fading and samadhi 23:19 Dependent arising deepens 31:45 Reed, house, and cessation 39:25 Beyond language and footing 48:17 Questions at the edge
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11135/

item.title: Dependent Origination, Awareness, and Time
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores time, consciousness, and dependent origination as a practice question rather than a philosophy to admire. He starts with the felt solidity of the present moment, then uses Madhyamaka-style reasoning to show why the moment, consciousness, and the sense of time cannot be inherently real. Later he turns to the mutual dependence of subject, object, attention, and intention, and ends by pointing back to practice: using concepts carefully so they help perception loosen rather than harden.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 A Map of Practice 02:25 Time Feels Solid 05:37 Past and Future 10:05 Awareness and Time 13:26 One or Many 18:49 Arising From None 23:16 The Tripod of Self 36:00 Dependent Arising Deepens 44:22 Subtle Mind Factors
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11120/

item.title: On the Nature of Mind
item.description:
Rob Burbea maps a progression of practice around the nature of mind, beginning with why awareness matters at the level of suffering and compassion, and then moving through mirror-like awareness, luminous mind, and the limits of those images. He draws on the Buddha, Zen, Mahamudra, and Dzogchen sources to show how each helpful view can still be provisional. The talk gradually turns toward the subtler question of how knowing and known arise together, and why even consciousness itself is said to be empty.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 05:35 Why emptiness matters 12:57 From self to center 20:55 The mirror image 23:59 Provisional luminous states 28:36 Luminous mind revisited 38:52 What cannot be found 49:18 Knowing and known 59:45 Practice beyond the view
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11128/

item.title: The Freedom of No-thing-ness
item.description:
Rob Burbea uses this talk to unpack emptiness through a series of close, practical analyses: how the mind imputes wholes and parts, how labels and reactions harden experience, and how walking, pain, and the body can all be examined for inherent existence. He threads in Nagarjuna, a Prajnaparamita citation, and a brief Thich Nhat Hanh reference, but keeps returning to meditation practice and the felt freedom that comes when solidity begins to soften. The talk ends by balancing analytic reasoning with samadhi and metta, with an emphasis on using these reflections quietly and carefully.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Taking Care of Heart 03:22 Seeing No Thing 05:12 Wholes and Parts 16:11 Applying the Analysis 19:59 Body Boundaries 25:55 Aggregates and Labels 30:07 Dependence in Experience 38:05 Walking and Motion 44:58 Portable Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11133/

item.title: Love, Karma, and Healing
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how emptiness, love, karma, and healing are not separate themes but different ways of seeing the same process. He moves from the fear that emptiness leads to nihilism into a quieter argument that less clinging, less selfing, and clearer insight naturally open the heart. The talk then turns to practice: working with compassion, anger, difficult emotions, and even the idea of healing itself without turning the self into the center of the story.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why emptiness opens love 03:17 Less clinging, less separation 08:13 Mindfulness and metta together 12:19 Heroic practice and release 17:16 Compassion without burden 24:36 Seeing the world as malleable 32:26 Karma, reasoning, and fading 40:16 Healing, storms, and clinging 54:01 Awakening and fabrication
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11127/

item.title: Two Talks: 1- Mindfulness and Emptiness - The Foundations; 2 - The Ending of the World
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins with mindfulness as a way of seeing how suffering gets made out of fleeting sensations and mental labeling, then widens into emptiness as the deeper question of what is really here. From there he moves through dependent origination, mutual dependence, and the way clinging helps fabricate both self and world, using retreat examples, Buddhist texts, and a few Zen and Christian references. The talk ends with practical ways to work with fading, fear, and grasping without turning emptiness into aversion or an achievement.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Mindfulness and suffering 04:43 Fabrication as root 09:05 Dualities arise together 11:00 Context and clinging 19:41 Practice without preferences 28:48 What clinging means 32:09 Objects fading away 43:24 Dependent arising in depth 51:49 Warnings and fear
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11132/

item.title: Cittamatra
item.description:
In this retreat talk, Rob Burbea returns to the question of what the practice is for, and then re-centers it on reducing suffering and opening to love and compassion. From there he unfolds a meditation on Cittamatra: first through the felt sense of vast awareness, then through the more analytic view that subject and object are not separately existing things. He keeps emphasizing that these are provisional ways of working, useful as practice, but not to be confused with the final view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions 03:27 What Practice Is For 05:38 Self and Suffering 10:03 From Things To Experience 20:02 Three Characteristics To Space 23:31 Impressions In Awareness 33:32 Awareness, Names, And Rigpa 45:02 Mind-Only Reasoning 53:00 Provisional View
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11119/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
In this retreat talk, Rob Burbea returns to the question of what the practice is for, and then re-centers it on reducing suffering and opening to love and compassion. From there he unfolds a meditation on Cittamatra: first through the felt sense of vast awareness, then through the more analytic view that subject and object are not separately existing things. He keeps emphasizing that these are provisional ways of working, useful as practice, but not to be confused with the final view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions 03:27 What Practice Is For 05:38 Self and Suffering 10:03 From Things To Experience 20:02 Three Characteristics To Space 23:31 Impressions In Awareness 33:32 Awareness, Names, And Rigpa 45:02 Mind-Only Reasoning 53:00 Provisional View
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11119/

item.title: Integrity and the Poetics of Unknowing
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how mindfulness, conceptuality, and the sense of self are intertwined, and why simply noticing change is not yet the same as seeing emptiness. He questions popular Dharma ideas about “going with the flow,” agnosticism, and neutral openness, then moves toward a more precise account of dependent origination as something that can be known directly. The talk gradually turns from critique to practice: ethics, collectedness, wisdom, and the repeated seeing through fabrication are presented as the conditions for release.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Mindfulness and thought 05:12 Beyond vivid appearances 12:13 Impermanence is not enough 16:12 Non-construction of reality 24:46 Concepts shape suffering 34:36 No other cause to peace 47:01 Agnosticism and discernment 55:46 How self is built 01:07:55 Beyond words
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11126/

item.title: Chandrakirti's Chariot and the Unfindable Self
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces Chandrakirti’s sevenfold chariot analysis as a practical way to investigate the self, beginning with the felt sense of inherent existence and then testing whether the self can be found in the aggregates, apart from them, or as their collection, possession, shape, or continuum. He keeps returning to the difference between empty, dead conceptualization and a living meditative inquiry that can soften into a direct sense of unfindability. The talk moves from general framing into detailed reasoning, then toward how the practice can alternate with samadhi and be used not only with self but with emotions and experience more broadly.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the practice 03:10 Inherent existence 11:00 Why the chariot matters 20:52 Sevenfold analysis 39:25 Perceiver and perceived 46:23 Continuum and time 54:00 Analysis and samadhi 56:28 Using the method
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11118/

item.title: Exploring the Relationship with Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how our attitude toward practice shapes everything that follows: effort, goals, not knowing, conceptuality, and the way we meet difficulty. He emphasizes that wise effort needs aspiration, confidence, joy, and rest, and then keeps returning to the question of whether practice feels too tight, too vague, too self-critical, or too settled. The talk moves gradually from general orientation into a more subtle challenge: to keep questioning, stay responsive, and not stop short of a deeper understanding of emptiness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Frame 05:58 Wise Effort 22:08 Progress and Patience 31:05 Learning to Understand 37:21 Seeds and Depth 46:46 Risk and Balance 54:01 Tightness and Sailing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11123/

item.title: Practicing with the Three Characteristics
item.description:
Rob Burbea develops the three characteristics as practical lenses for meditation: impermanence, dukkha, and not-self. He keeps returning to the same point that these are not doctrines to believe, but ways of seeing that support letting go, especially when meditation meets difficulty. Later he connects the practice to dependent arising, the five khandhas, and a more subtle release from identification altogether.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the framework 03:17 The three characteristics 07:06 Balancing samadhi and insight 12:02 Working with difficulty 17:04 Impermanence and dukkha 23:41 Not-self through letting go 34:23 The anatta practice 50:59 Subtlety and support 01:03:57 Nothing owned
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11130/

item.title: The Sense of Self
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the sense of self as a practical meditation issue rather than a metaphysical one. He distinguishes useful selfing from the suffering that comes when identity becomes fixed, self-referential, and subtly solid. The talk moves from everyday roles, story, and inner criticism into methods for loosening identification, including anatta practice, looking at actions instead of self-view, and noticing how samadhi changes the felt sense of self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing emptiness 02:36 Self as a tool 10:37 Story and identity 16:40 Seeing self-definitions 22:31 Inner critic and the early texts 27:46 Spectrum of selfing 35:44 Samadhi and fabrication 41:19 Conceiving self differently 54:11 Dependent arising and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11134/

item.title: Samadhi in the Practice of Emptiness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores samadhi as collectedness, unification, and a workable climate for emptiness practice, rather than as a narrow kind of concentration. He keeps returning to the practical consequences: how samadhi supports subtle investigation, cushions fear and nihilism, and helps practice become less tight and self-judging. He also distinguishes breath and metta practice, open samadhi, and the role of the whole body in steadying attention.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 What Samadhi Means 03:39 Why It Matters 09:49 Climate for Inquiry 11:57 Beyond Tight Focusing 18:57 A Learnable Skill 21:48 Working With Hindrances 28:33 Body and Tightness 32:42 Subtle Body and Insight 39:23 Practical Guidance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11131/

item.title: Emptiness
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers an introduction to emptiness that is careful to separate it from blankness, depression, or a mere meditative state. Using the Heart Sutra, everyday examples, and repeated returns to dependent arising, he shows how the mind imputes solidity to selves, relationships, nations, time, and even perception itself. The talk keeps pointing back to practice: seeing things as empty is meant to reduce suffering, loosen fixation, and deepen compassion rather than become a philosophical position.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What emptiness is not 03:00 Heart Sutra frame 04:38 Everyday dependence 14:07 Retreat and conditions 27:47 Mind imputes objects 37:51 Nothing is exempt 48:05 Practice over theory 55:56 Compassion and liberation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11121/

item.title: Beyond Impermanence
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores impermanence first as something ordinary and then as a practice of seeing, asking what changes when the heart and mind are trained to notice it directly. From daily moods and aging to death, relationships, and the constructed feel of time and objects, he keeps widening the frame while returning to the need for well-being, compassion, and clarity. In the final movement, he turns toward the possibility of freedom beyond the usual sense of change, including the language of the unconditioned and unbinding.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Impermanence as practice 02:35 Tracking ordinary change 05:41 Beauty, grief, and loss 10:05 The scale of disappearing 15:48 Aging and inheritance 25:09 Death, urgency, compassion 33:20 Changing the way we see 43:02 Beyond the flow 50:56 Constructed time and objects
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10830/

item.title: The Meaning of Refuge
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores refuge as something much deeper than a Buddhist ritual or a comforting idea. He asks what we actually take refuge in—money, relationships, comfort, fantasy, practice—and gently but firmly examines how unreliable those supports can be. The talk turns toward the Three Refuges as lived orientations: confidence in awakening, commitment to practice, and the support of Sangha, with an emphasis on right view, responsibility, and the courage to meet difficulty directly.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What refuge means 03:23 Ordinary refuges fail 09:36 Fear and impermanence 20:24 Awakening and practice 24:12 The whole path matters 30:54 Right view as refuge 38:34 Freedom is possible 44:17 Sangha and admirable friends 48:34 Courage to take risks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10834/

item.title: Maya and Nirvana (Beyond the Measure of Mind)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the Buddha’s teaching that suffering arises through the mind’s measuring, conceiving, and proliferating, and that the world we take for granted is partly fabricated through that process. Using passages on maya, dependent origination, Sāriputta, Mahākaccāyana, and a closing Zen poem, he traces how perception, selfing, and duality reinforce each other and how practice can unwind them. The talk moves from obvious forms of suffering into subtler questions of how experience itself is built, and ends by pointing toward the unmade, beyond measure and beyond time.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 03:25 Maya and the magic show 07:15 Papancha and fabrication 12:16 Signs and measurement 23:42 Dependent origination 28:59 Three characteristics 33:12 No preferences, no duality 43:33 The subtler prison 56:55 Beyond measure
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10832/

item.title: Living in the Truth
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores truth as something lived, not merely believed, and asks how Dharma practice can be oriented toward what frees the deepest. He moves from personal truth and the inner critic, to universal truths like impermanence and not-self, and then into more subtle questions about perception, mystical experience, and ultimate truth. Cross-traditional references to the Buddha, Jesus, Basho, and Nagarjuna help him test how far language, view, and sincerity can take practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Truth and freedom 02:19 What truth means 06:09 Mindfulness reveals patterns 11:58 Personal truth 22:52 A quieter story 25:13 Universal truths 32:49 Views and perception 45:01 Refining view 59:12 Living unburdened
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10831/

item.title: Sense Desire
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores sense desire as a practical and spiritual question, not a moral problem to be judged. He contrasts the Buddha’s hard warnings with a more careful inquiry: what actually happens to the heart, to freedom, and to love when we chase pleasure or practice restraint? The talk moves from everyday indulgence to renunciation, inner resource, and the possibility of a deeper happiness that makes sense desire look smaller by comparison.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 04:10 Danger and renunciation 06:41 Where the loop lives 12:08 Inner resource first 18:27 Joy and connection 24:06 Exploring pleasure directly 31:54 Mind shapes experience 41:24 Love, contentment, restraint 49:53 Beyond the senses
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10833/

item.title: Working with Thought in Meditation
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how meditation can meet thought without treating it as an enemy. He distinguishes ordinary planning and reflection from obsessive proliferation, drawing on Buddha’s teaching on thoughts that lead toward suffering or away from it, and he offers practical ways to work with thought through calm, the body, metta, and spacious awareness. The talk gradually shifts from discerning unhelpful thinking to seeing thought as fleeting, insubstantial, and not to be taken as self or authority.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Reframing thought 05:53 Three aims with thought 16:39 Working with harmful loops 20:14 What papanca does 30:58 Finding the hook 37:39 Emotion and well-being 50:41 Thought as process
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10835/

item.title: Approaches to the Hindrances
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the five hindrances as ordinary human patterns that can be worked with skillfully rather than simply endured. He frames meditation as an art and craft, while also making room for non-doing, open awareness, and the subtle question of when to intervene and when to let experience settle. The talk moves from broad orientation into practical approaches for sloth and torpor, restlessness, craving, aversion, and doubt, then ends with questions about personalized practice, imagery, and how to find steadiness without forcing it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the subject 03:38 Meditation as art 07:13 Questioning practice views 14:20 Mindfulness and release 20:56 Sloth and torpor 27:02 Restlessness and craving 36:54 Aversion and doubt 45:15 Personalizing practice 54:32 Not taking the ride
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10836/

item.title: The Unshakeable Heart (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores equanimity as something that grows out of warmth rather than self-criticism. He moves from meeting suffering with kindness, through a more nuanced account of karma and aversion, into the way generosity, selfing, and the eight worldly conditions shape the felt world. The talk closes by turning toward not-self and the Buddha’s “house-builder” image, suggesting that practice gradually learns to construct less and rest in what is unmade.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Warmth Before Equanimity 01:54 Mind Sucked Into Suffering 04:04 Not Underestimating Practice 06:58 Karma Without Simplification 10:52 Generosity Changes the World 15:57 Praise, Blame, and Identity 18:56 Seeing Through Self 25:06 Letting Dualities Unwind 28:29 House-Building Ends
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10644/

The talk closes by turning toward not-self and the Buddha’s “house-builder” image, suggesting that practice gradually learns to construct less and rest in what is unmade.

item.title: The Unshakeable Heart (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores equanimity as something that is gradually trained, not merely understood. Starting with the eight worldly conditions—praise and blame, success and failure, gain and loss, pleasure and pain—he shows how impermanence, inner happiness, and a quieter relationship to craving and aversion can loosen the heart's dependence on changing conditions. The talk then widens into cultivation of wholesome qualities, the role of concentration, the shaping power of perspective, and a brief poetic ending with T.S. Eliot on the still point within the turning world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 01:04 The eight worldly conditions 02:59 Practicing impermanence 07:21 Why insight is not enough 11:01 Qualities that support joy 14:45 Collectedness and conviction 24:33 The heart shapes perception 33:46 Working with reactivity 48:16 Simplifying and letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10643/

item.title: Second Day Instructions - Vedana
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces vedana, the second foundation of mindfulness, as a simple but often overlooked way of tuning attention to the basic tone of experience: pleasant, unpleasant, or neither. He explains how craving, aversion, and boredom are usually layered on top of that feeling tone, and how practice can soften those reactions so suffering drains away. The talk closes by linking this with the earlier impermanence practice and pointing listeners toward the sitting meditation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Two Practice Directions 00:54 Mindfulness Foundations 02:19 What Vedana Means 06:26 Why It Is Overlooked 10:30 Practicing Sensitivity 12:41 Feeling the Reaction 15:41 Relaxing the Push-Pull 19:27 Two Retreat Practices
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10642/

item.title: The Development of Equanimity: Introduction and First Instructions
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this retreat talk by framing equanimity as a living response to the changing conditions of life, not as cold detachment. He moves from the Buddha’s eight worldly conditions and the image of the unshakable stone slab to a careful distinction between equanimity and indifference, then situates the whole retreat within the wider Dharma path and the possibility of awakening. The latter half turns practical, with instructions for a meditation on impermanence, bodily sensation, and the phrase, “May I be at peace with the flow of experience.”
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 02:26 What equanimity means 04:53 Eight worldly conditions 09:14 The stone slab image 11:58 Not indifference 14:23 A spectrum of practice 21:01 Path, faith, and priority 30:54 Silence, trust, and practice 41:18 Guided meditation on change
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10641/

item.title: On the Essence of Being
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the idea of essence by tracing how it appears in ordinary self-view, in meditation, and in Buddhist and Western philosophy. He contrasts essence-seeking with the Buddha’s emphasis on conditions, actions, and the ending of suffering, then turns to common reifications of Buddha nature, awareness, and being. The talk closes by pointing toward a freedom that is less about finding an ultimate thing and more about letting go of the need to conceive experience in essence terms.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 03:23 Upanishadic Background 09:13 The Buddha's Shift 15:00 Meditation and Perception 24:32 Seeing Conditions 30:54 Razor's Edge of Views 43:12 Ground and Awareness 48:00 Being and Self 54:48 Freedom Beyond Conception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10640/

item.title: Working with Anger and Aversion
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores anger and aversion as deeply human forces that can range from open hostility to subtle irritability, fear, and withdrawal. He distinguishes harmful anger from the need to respond clearly to harm, and then works through reflective practices that help soften reactivity without denying boundaries or truth. The talk moves from the consequences of anger into empathy, equanimity, mindful attention to the body, and the question of how perception itself gets built by aversion.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the topic 02:48 Harm and restraint 06:49 Anger's many forms 13:23 Three kilesas 16:11 Consequences and stories 24:35 Empathy and death 29:08 Reflection and courage 36:04 Body, story, and letting go 44:17 How aversion builds reality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10645/

item.title: Talk Five: Samatha, Nibbana, and the Emptiness of Perception - The Relationship Between Concentration and Insight
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores samatha as more than calm: a way of seeing how the mind builds suffering, self, and world. He moves from ordinary agitation and the inner critic into the relationship between concentration and insight, then into jhana, dependent origination, emptiness, and nibbana as progressively less fabricated experience. The talk closes with Rumi’s “Wean Yourself,” used as a quiet image of gradual release rather than sudden escape.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Critic 02:50 Samatha and Insight 10:59 The Mind Builds 19:45 Jhana as Perception 25:12 Release and Relief 33:15 Perception Depends 40:36 Fabricating Experience 45:13 Nibbana and the Unfabricated 58:04 Rumi and Weaning
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11513/

item.title: Fourth Morning Instructions
item.description:
In this short review talk, Rob Burbea returns to the morning concentration instructions and reframes them in very practical terms: adjust the breath, shift how the breath is conceived, and experiment with the tone of attention. He then turns toward perception itself, showing how labels and perspective can reshape experience, even around pain, restlessness, and tiredness. The talk closes by emphasizing steadiness as something that nourishes samadhi over time, both on the cushion and through the day.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Review the practice 05:33 Breath and attention 09:06 Perception and labels 11:28 Working with discomfort 13:44 Seeing differently 15:24 Steadiness feeds samadhi
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11517/

item.title: Third Morning Instructions
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers practical instructions for working with concentration when the meditation goes through ups and downs. He emphasizes attitude, appreciation, patience, and contentment, then turns to how to meet thoughts, subtle restlessness, boredom, and the balance between too much effort and too little. The talk closes by opening into a more refined sense of breath energy in and around the body, leading into a sitting practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Expect the waves 02:50 Appreciation and patience 04:19 Working with thoughts 07:10 Restlessness and boredom 11:18 Lightness of attention 15:25 Breath energy field
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11518/

item.title: Talk Four: Jhanas One to Four
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a map of the four jhanas as a gradual deepening of concentration, pleasure, happiness, peacefulness, and stillness. He spends much of the talk distinguishing what actually matters in each stage: suffusion, steadiness, refinement, and the relationship between samadhi and insight, rather than dramatic intensity or special effects. In the second half, he answers questions about precision, preconceptions, escapism, compassion, and how these states can fit into ordinary life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the map 01:21 Listening and attitude 05:23 Pleasure gathers 09:37 First and second jhana 13:50 Contentment and peace 17:24 Fourth jhana 23:07 Signs and mastery 27:16 Escapes, not evasion 36:43 Prayer of practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11515/

item.title: Talk Three: Wise Effort and Wise Attachment
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores wise effort as a nuanced middle way between striving and passivity, starting with our relationship to goals and spiritual aspiration. He then turns to the four right efforts, wise attachment to ethics, concentration, and insight, and the inner critic that so often distorts practice into self-judgment. The talk ends by reframing effort as something responsive, light, and balanced, with practical images from the bird, the raft, and the potter.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 06:49 Striving and aspiration 11:34 Craving as leverage 18:37 Wise attachment 24:58 Working with the inner critic 38:31 Learning the bigger picture 45:13 Conditions for success 50:24 Balancing the effort
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11516/

item.title: Talk Two: Understanding the Heart
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a wide-ranging talk on samatha as a way of understanding and caring for the heart, not just settling the mind. He frames practice through mindfulness, cultivation, and insight, then turns to generosity, ethics, sense restraint, and the role of happiness as a real basis for letting go. The talk ends by showing how steadiness can change our relationship to suffering and open a more reliable, less story-bound happiness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Understanding the heart 02:44 Three strands of practice 05:42 Happiness as foundation 08:28 Generosity and ethics 15:11 Guarding the senses 19:02 Progress and the path 25:04 Working with hindrances 30:26 Not taking them personally 40:01 Sensitivity and emotion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11514/

item.title: Talk One: Introduction to the Art of Concentration (Samatha Meditation)
item.description:
Rob Burbea introduces a samatha retreat as an exploration of concentration, calm, and the art of working with breath, body, and perception. He frames the practice less as a fixed technique than as a lived experiment shaped by play, patience, generosity, and appreciation. The talk also places concentration in a wider Dharma context, drawing on the Buddha, Ajahn Buddhadasa, and retreat silence as supports for a deeper, less self-preoccupied practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 03:05 Why samatha matters 07:11 The art of practice 10:41 Play and patience 15:01 Breath and body energy 21:39 Pleasant abiding 23:44 Practice for all beings 29:33 Joy, gratitude, and enough 36:52 Retreat discipline and silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11511/

item.title: Nirvana and Transcendence
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the Buddha’s language of nirvana, the deathless, and the unfabricated, asking what these words point to beyond ordinary ideas of mindfulness, life, and spiritual fulfillment. He draws on early Buddhist passages, Huang Po, and a closing Rumi poem to question common assumptions about awareness, experience, and the goal of practice. The talk stays close to the tension between intimacy with life and transcendence, while also noticing the reactions that these ideas awaken in listeners.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:58 The deathless question 04:32 Sutta passages 09:39 Huang Po and the void 11:18 Reactions and assumptions 21:21 Mindfulness and life 29:33 Life or transcendence 36:41 Awareness and language 47:50 Fabrication and unmaking 58:48 Beyond existence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11974/

item.title: Awakening
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores what awakening means in the Buddha’s teachings, and why the word can’t be reduced to merely being present, calm, or alive. He traces how modern Dharma communities have sometimes softened or lost the radical aim of liberation, then returns to the suttas to show awakening as knowing the deathless, seeing through self-view, and understanding the fabricated nature of experience. The talk moves between practical questions about motivation and view, canonical quotations, and a careful explanation of stream entry as a real but subtle shift in orientation and freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Shifting Dharma Emphases 03:33 Lay Life and Radical Aim 08:35 Why Awakening Matters 11:57 What Is on Offer 15:47 Views, Images, and Assumptions 23:01 Stream Entry Explained 30:55 Self-View and the Aggregates 40:07 Deathless and Emptiness 56:45 Practice, Depth, and Possibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11862/

item.title: Love/The Difficulties of Love
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores why love can become painful, confusing, and overburdened, especially when romantic love is treated as the main source of happiness or validation. He questions the assumptions behind needing to feel loved, fear of rejection, self-criticism, and the way feelings can be mistaken for truth. The talk gradually widens into a Dharma view of love as something shaped by perception, practice, inner resources, and the loosening of self-view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions About Love 05:28 Romance and Cultural Pressure 10:42 Self-Love and the Inner Critic 18:36 Worthiness and Hidden Shame 26:44 Feelings, Intention, and Love 35:31 Falling In Love 47:13 Relationship as Skill 54:12 Metta, Perception, and Spaciousness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11860/

item.title: Death
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores death as a direct part of a free and honest life, not a morbid topic to avoid. He moves from the fear and cultural avoidance around mortality into practices for remembering death, weighing choices, and sensing what really matters. Along the way he uses personal stories, poetry, and Buddhist sources to point toward urgency, compassion, and the possibility of something deathless.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Contemplate Death 04:12 Not Depressing, But Liberating 09:00 Preciousness And Impermanence 17:02 Urgency And What Matters 25:59 Choices, Ethics, And Insight 32:17 Refuge In The Deathless 39:14 Body, Earth, And Recycling 48:14 Impermanence In Practice 53:02 Experience, Emptiness, And Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11859/

item.title: Opening Talk for Work Retreat - Working and Awakening
item.description:
In this opening talk for a work retreat, Rob Burbea invites listeners to question the usual split between retreat life and everyday life. He presents the work retreat as an experiment in balancing formal meditation, service, and ordinary activity, while keeping awareness, intention, and view at the center. The talk gradually turns practical: how intentions slip, how to renew them, how to relate to difficulty as an opportunity, and how silence, simplicity, gratitude, and the precepts support an open heart.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What a retreat is 03:02 Work and meditation 07:54 Service as practice 12:16 Awareness and intention 17:13 Renewing the aim 20:40 View and difficulty 25:14 Caring for the heart 29:43 Silence and simplicity 34:08 Precepts and trust
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11861/

item.title: The Birth of a Bodhisattva
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the bodhisattva ideal as a way of uniting compassion, wisdom, and equanimity. He moves from Shantideva’s vows and the feel of radical service into the practical question of what makes compassion sustainable when suffering does not resolve. The talk then widens into emptiness, faith, and examples from Christian and Buddhist traditions to show how a heart can stay open without becoming overwhelmed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Bodhisattva Meaning 02:56 Shantideva's Vows 05:55 What Sustains Compassion 10:29 Balance in Practice 17:00 Self, Doing, and Space 25:17 Holding Suffering 33:59 Equanimity and Karma 46:41 Faith and Devotion 59:14 Emptiness and Compassion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11954/

item.title: Love and the Emptiness of Things
item.description:
Rob Burbea begins by reviewing how loving-kindness, compassion, and samadhi function as a gradual reconditioning of the heart, not as self-improvement but as the creation of a wholesome climate for practice. He then turns to a subtler theme: how love can be directed not only toward beings, but toward experiences, and how this changes the felt solidity of things. From there the talk moves into emptiness, dependent origination, and the claim that love and non-grasping help reveal that experience, self, and even fear are not as independent as they seem.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the path 03:31 Nourishment and insight 08:31 From practice to perception 16:25 Love beyond beings 27:25 Technique and freedom 40:08 Perception through relationship 54:39 Love and emptiness 01:08:03 Not stopping at states 01:18:49 Questions and practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11953/

item.title: Compassion Part Two (Compassion for Ourselves)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a talk on compassion by turning from compassion for others to compassion for oneself. He explores common resistance, the role of tenderness and empathy, how self-compassion can support insight, and why it need not feed ego. The talk closes by linking compassion to non-self and offering a few practical ways to keep the practice simple and deep.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and transition 01:29 Why self-compassion feels difficult 03:10 Learning from our own pain 05:06 Balancing empathy and giving 07:08 How self-compassion works 08:43 Ego, phrases, and delicacy 11:26 Compassion as insight lens 15:25 Compassion and non-self 17:37 Choose one well
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11949/

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11949/

item.title: Compassion
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores compassion as a living practice rather than a feeling to be waited for. He unpacks its parts—empathy, giving, steadiness, wisdom, acceptance, humor, listening, joy, and faith—while also naming the ways it gets distorted by fear, anger, pity, and grief. The talk moves from personal examples and classic Dharma teachings into a broader reflection on shared vulnerability, selflessness, and the spaciousness that can make compassion sustainable.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What compassion is 02:35 Many strands of compassion 06:25 Ambivalence and longing 15:45 Questions and practices 21:22 Conditions that nurture it 27:51 Four near enemies 44:04 Empathy and giving 53:49 Shared vulnerability 01:04:55 Emptiness and interpenetration
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11950/

item.title: Using Insight To Deepen Love and Compassion
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores loving-kindness and compassion as practices that can be deepened by insight rather than weakened by it. He moves from the role of body sensitivity and samadhi, through fear and clinging, into the three characteristics—impermanence, suffering, and not-self—as ways of seeing that can soften fear and open love. The talk closes with practical guidance on how to work flexibly, choosing simpler or subtler approaches depending on what actually helps in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Cultivating the heart 04:50 Insight as release 08:28 Fear and its grip 12:29 Working with fear 30:35 Impermanence in metta 43:13 Dukkha and selfing 58:33 Not-self and practice 01:35:29 Ways of seeing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11956/

item.title: Exploring the World of Loving Kindness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores loving-kindness as something more than a pleasant feeling: a practice of intention, sensitivity, and widening perception. He moves from the textures of metta, to the overlap with compassion, to the ways selfing, attachment, and anger can narrow the heart. The talk then opens into friendship, communication, body-based tenderness, silence, and the possibility that love is already surrounding experience and can change how the world is perceived.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Metta Is 02:25 Compassion and Boundaries 05:05 Self-Love as Foundation 08:52 Friend, Benefactor, Near-Enemy 12:11 Friendship and Reparenting 17:12 Self, Clinging, and Identity 22:39 Gibran on Friendship 26:08 Anger and Skillful Speech 38:58 Conflict Without Harm
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11951/

item.title: The Place of Samadhi in Metta Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores samadhi as more than narrow concentration: steadiness, unification, depth, softness, and a dwelling place for the mind and heart. Speaking to a metta retreat, he shows how lovingkindness and compassion can support concentration, and how happiness, appreciation, and receptivity help samadhi grow. He also works through common doubts about effort, attachment, suppression, and whether difficult emotions are really being hidden or simply not being built in the present moment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Orientation 03:00 Metta and Concentration 12:02 Why Samadhi Matters 21:40 Steadiness and Well-Being 32:20 Whole-Body Softness 41:19 Dwelling Places of the Heart 53:12 Pleasure and Fear 01:03:02 Emptiness in Experience 01:15:01 Questions and Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11955/
