item.title: Joy
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores joy as a real and trainable part of the Dharma path, not a contradiction to it. He distinguishes ordinary pleasures from spiritual joy, then traces how joy can arise from appreciation, ethics, generosity, mindfulness, beauty, and the way we relate to difficulty. The talk also keeps returning to a practical question: what nourishes joy, what quietly drains it, and how does attention change the heart over time?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Joy on the path 02:31 Kinds of joy 06:07 Joy as nourishment 10:16 Training the mind 16:50 Relating differently 24:56 Cultivating joy 32:19 Ethics and appreciation 40:51 What feeds joy 49:55 Attention and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11971/

item.title: What is Insight?
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a wide-ranging map of insight meditation, beginning with a simple definition of insight as anything that lessens suffering and moving through personal, universal, and ultimate levels of understanding. He then shows how insight can arise naturally, be strengthened through practice, or be used deliberately as a lens on experience, especially through impermanence, dukkha, and not-self. The talk closes by widening into dependent arising and the middle way, with a warning that the deepest insight is not nihilistic but freeing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 03:14 What insight means 10:02 Personal and story 14:07 Universal truths 20:12 Insighт deepens 26:46 Using insight as a lens 35:11 Three characteristics 43:47 Fading and release 52:24 Dependent arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11972/

item.title: Time and the Emptiness of Time
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the ordinary sense of time and then steadily questions whether past, future, and even the present have any inherent reality. He moves from conventional clock time and Newton, through Einstein, Meister Eckhart, and Simone Weil, into meditative reasonings from Nagarjuna and Tibetan traditions. The talk keeps returning to practice: how craving, selfing, and measurement build time, and how insight into their emptiness can open into freedom, timelessness, and compassion.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Ordinary time 04:33 Questioning the obvious 08:25 Newton and Einstein 14:35 Time and seeking 20:26 Now is not enough 28:24 Reasoning in meditation 36:40 Self, things, and time 48:10 Timeless unbinding 54:06 Living from insight
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11929/

item.title: Into the Depths of Silence
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores silence as more than the absence of speech: something central to contemplative practice, and a medium through which priorities, remorse, tenderness, and insight can surface. He moves from everyday resistance to silence, through stories and poetic references, toward a quieter sense of awareness in which sounds, thoughts, and sensations can be let be. The talk gradually opens into the possibility that silence is not empty but spacious, receptive, and in some way loving.
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11928/

item.title: A Question of Faith
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores faith not as belief, but as the often-hidden way we orient ourselves in life. He asks what we really trust, how suffering can either deepen confusion or open inquiry, and how practice can gradually shift faith toward what is beautiful, true, and possible. The talk moves from everyday choices and personal stories to the Four Noble Truths, ending with a quiet invitation to examine where confidence in the path can come from.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 04:18 The human predicament 11:06 What we trust 17:14 Faith in care 22:50 Cultivating the beautiful 29:21 When forms break 40:04 Path of questions 45:54 What is possible 50:25 Experience-based confidence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11927/

item.title: "A Body At Peace With Itself"
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a grounded exploration of mindfulness of the body as a path away from self-judgment and toward a more careful relationship with experience. He moves from direct bodily sensation, pain, and comfort into how perception shapes what the body seems to be, and then into the three characteristics: impermanence, unsatisfactoriness, and not-self. The talk closes by widening the frame to nature, death, and the possibility that body practice can open toward freedom rather than fixating on the body as identity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 05:40 Body at peace 06:53 See body as body 11:22 Bare attention 16:07 Working with pain 22:16 Pleasure and metta 28:54 Body as deep refuge 40:07 Letting body go 49:19 Foam and deathless
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11926/

item.title: The Self and the World
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the relation between self and world as a practical Dharma question, not a metaphysical one. He moves between therapeutic uses of self-story, the Buddha’s not-me-not-mine approach, and a subtler inquiry into how self and world co-arise through fabrication, mood, and attention. The talk ends by pointing toward dependent origination and nibbana as unbinding rather than nihilism, with increasing freedom as the felt result.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the question 02:12 When self-story helps 07:57 Choosing the right lens 10:09 Mindfulness loosens identity 19:05 Not me, not mine 25:55 Self and world co-arise 30:35 Helpful concepts, harmful concepts 41:47 Fabrication and empty experience 48:47 Dependent origination and unbinding
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12223/

item.title: Ocean of Patience
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores patience as both a steady perseverance in practice and a calm willingness to endure difficulty without tightening around it. He moves from retreat life and ordinary impatience into a broader inquiry into causes and conditions, using stories, classical quotations, and concrete examples from meditation, work, and relationships. The talk keeps returning to the question of how impatience is built, what feeds it, and how a more honest view can soften it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Reflections 02:53 Practice Needs Patience 04:53 Joyful Acceptance 09:00 Long View of Effort 13:12 Gradual Transformation 18:43 Urgency and Wisdom 23:22 Investigating Impatience 30:55 Present Moment and Allowing 43:27 Time, Solidity, and Release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12222/

item.title: Freedom From Fear And Anxiety
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores fear and anxiety as ordinary human experiences that can still be worked with skillfully. He moves from the Buddha’s own fear in the forest to practical methods: mindfulness of sensation, noticing reactions of aversion, bringing kindness, and challenging fear more directly when needed. The talk closes by widening the frame to self-judgment, future anxiety, and the cultivation of inner resources as a real way of caring for what comes next.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Fear is universal 03:23 Many forms of fear 11:37 Three approaches 15:58 What fear feels like 24:12 Kindness and metta 33:18 Opening the space 39:55 Questioning assumptions 49:28 Cultivating the future
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/868/

item.title: Jhana Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a wide-ranging introduction to jhana practice, beginning with the place of samatha within the larger field of Dharma and then moving through the eight absorptions as a map of deepening calm, pleasure, stillness, and spaciousness. He emphasizes that the point is not attainment for its own sake, but the fruits of practice: less clinging, more confidence, insight, and a quieter relation to self and experience. The talk also turns practical and candid about pitfalls, including attachment, self-inflation, and the backlash of hindrances as practice deepens.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice in broad view 02:40 Why jhana matters 04:39 What jhana means 08:11 First two absorptions 12:31 From contentment to space 17:13 Fruits of the states 24:52 What counts as jhana 30:20 Confidence and insight 39:40 Pitfalls, questions, and conditions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12311/

item.title: Developing Samatha/Concentration
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores samatha as more than narrow concentration: a deepening of calm, collectedness, brightness, and bodily well-being. He moves through the value of pleasure, receptivity, and gratitude, then addresses common doubts about goals, attachment, suppression, and tight effort in practice. The talk closes by linking samatha to insight and to the emptiness of emotions and self, suggesting that deep calm makes both healing and release more available.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Samatha Means 05:25 A Lifelong Practice 10:18 Why It Matters 15:13 Insight Needs Support 20:45 Happiness as Basis 26:16 Working with Hindrances 32:56 Softness, Not Strain 39:16 Goals and Attachment 46:45 Pīti and Fear
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12309/

item.title: Working with the Hindrances
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a practical talk on the five hindrances as part of retreat life and ordinary life: craving, aversion, sloth and torpor, restlessness and worry, and doubt. Rather than treating them as signs of failure, he shows how they can be met as part of samatha practice, using the body, the breath, posture, and attention itself as tools. The talk moves from a broad framing of difficulty to detailed ways of working with each hindrance, returning again and again to the question of how practice can become more nourishing, spacious, and clear.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat and difficulty 02:00 The five hindrances 05:58 Active practice 10:20 Craving and hunger 15:13 Pleasure and nourishment 22:52 Aversion and anger 27:40 Boredom and energy 40:22 Sloth and torpor 46:25 Worry, doubt, and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12315/

item.title: Samatha (Concentration Practice) - Introductory Talk
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens this introductory retreat talk by placing samatha in a broad human and Buddhist stream: a practice of calm that predates Buddhism, yet is central to the path. He then shifts from inspiration to practical orientation, explaining why calmness matters, how it relates to insight and the heart practices, and what kind of retreat conditions support it. Much of the talk is about setting the right frame for five days of practice: intention, simplicity, silence, ethics, and a willingness to work carefully with the breath and body.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Retreat 02:18 Samatha in Human Life 05:24 Why Calmness Matters 08:13 Three Practice Paths 09:21 Inner Resource and Method 16:17 Breath, Control, and Letting Go 19:52 Intentions on Retreat 25:11 Gratitude, Silence, Simplicity 31:26 How to Support Calm
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12312/

item.title: God and the Ultimate Truth
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the idea of God through a Dharma lens, moving from belief and devotion into questions of fear, compassion, and what actually shapes a life. He compares personal and impersonal forms of God, brings in figures like Krishnamurti, Mother Teresa, Thomas Keating, and St. John of the Cross, and keeps returning to the practical test of whether a view opens the heart or not. The talk gradually shifts toward emptiness, silence, and the possibility that what is most ultimate may be beyond the usual opposites of belief and non-belief.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 03:38 Devotion and belief 08:40 Images of God 12:57 What lies beneath belief 17:24 Bodhisattvas and practice 21:36 Surrender and discernment 26:45 The heart as divine 31:14 Mystical perception 36:22 Silence and letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12342/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Rob Burbea explores the idea of God through a Dharma lens, moving from belief and devotion into questions of fear, compassion, and what actually shapes a life. He compares personal and impersonal forms of God, brings in figures like Krishnamurti, Mother Teresa, Thomas Keating, and St. John of the Cross, and keeps returning to the practical test of whether a view opens the heart or not. The talk gradually shifts toward emptiness, silence, and the possibility that what is most ultimate may be beyond the usual opposites of belief and non-belief.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 03:38 Devotion and belief 08:40 Images of God 12:57 What lies beneath belief 17:24 Bodhisattvas and practice 21:36 Surrender and discernment 26:45 The heart as divine 31:14 Mystical perception 36:22 Silence and letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12342/

item.title: Passion, Desire, and the Path
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores why passion and desire are not simply obstacles on the Buddhist path, but can also be the energy that sustains practice. He moves from renunciation and mortality, through balanced effort and aspiration, into sexuality, retreat life, and the subtle ways desire shapes experience. The talk ends by contrasting cultivation with letting go, and by pointing toward a freedom that is not built on grasping for results.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Passion on the path 02:12 Renunciation with fire 06:36 Urgency and possibility 09:59 Quiet passion sustained 13:32 Goals, aspiration, and effort 20:10 Where desire moves 25:47 Sex, romance, and retreat 34:10 What feeds experience 43:14 Energy, tiredness, and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12343/

item.title: Home and Homelessness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores the deep human need for home and security, then turns that question toward practice: what we protect, what we fear losing, and what kinds of rootedness actually free the heart. He moves from the early Buddhist ideal of leaving home through the risks of worldly attachment, then into more subtle homes in ethics, refuge, loving-kindness, awareness, and finally the possibility of non-abiding freedom. The talk is reflective and unsparing, but it keeps returning to a practical question: where, if anywhere, can we truly rest?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 01:28 Leaving household life 05:04 Lay life and its risks 11:20 Money and self-protection 18:01 Rooted in the Dharma 23:53 Seeing groundlessness 29:41 Changing experience, changing homes 38:38 Inner resources and refuge 44:38 Security, emptiness, and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12344/

item.title: Equanimity in Compassion
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores equanimity as the fourth Brahma Vihara, focusing first on its role within compassion and then widening the frame to samadhi, dependent origination, emptiness, and the eight worldly conditions. He distinguishes equanimity from indifference, showing how steadiness can remain tender, spacious, and responsive without being overwhelmed by suffering. The talk moves from practice instructions into a wide reflection on relationship, responsibility, and the way wisdom and compassion deepen together.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The Brahma Viharas 03:21 What Equanimity Means 06:09 Compassion and Balance 11:45 Samadhi and Spaciousness 19:48 Needs and Conditions 29:33 Karma and Non-Separation 42:18 All Conditions 52:16 Subtle Letting Go 01:01:41 Questions and Integration
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12289/

item.title: Love and Emptiness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how mettā and compassion can be practiced not only toward beings, but toward experiences, perceptions, and the very conditions of mind. He moves from the practical effects of love as nourishment and skillful means into a deeper inquiry into emptiness, showing how love, non-grasping, and the fading of fixed views can reveal freedom. The talk includes references to the Lotus Sutra, the Aksayamati Sutra, the Metta Sutta, Ram Dass, and a brief exchange with questions about how to work with the new practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Review 03:27 Nourishment and Insight 07:19 Conditions and the Unconditioned 11:48 Seeing Things with Compassion 17:47 Acceptance and Perception 29:18 Ease and Relationship 40:32 Fear and Present Time 48:10 Grasping and Fading 01:01:11 Questions and Practice Guidance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12293/

item.title: The Practice of Compassion
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores compassion as a natural response to suffering when the heart is not contracted around self. He distinguishes compassion from pity, fear, anger, and grief, and looks closely at what supports it in practice: willingness, wisdom, wise association, action, and a measure of happiness. Later he turns to compassion for oneself, the role of emptiness and anatta, and how compassion can open into a sense of spaciousness and non-separation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What compassion is 02:19 Compassion's qualities 07:21 Why the heart resists 12:27 How it develops 18:31 What nurtures compassion 31:04 Near enemies 38:58 Balance and spaciousness 47:08 Self, emptiness, and oneness 58:32 Interpenetration and practice questions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12295/

item.title: From Insight to Love
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how insight can nourish metta and compassion rather than pull them apart. He moves from a simple definition of insight as the release of clinging, through a story of Baba Amte and Gandhi, into practical ways of working with fear, impermanence, dukkha, and not-self inside lovingkindness practice. The talk stays grounded in ordinary experience: how fear tightens, how comparison becomes a habit, and how the heart opens when clinging relaxes.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Insight and metta 03:59 What insight means 06:31 Baba Amte's example 10:47 Working with fear 24:53 Insight in practice 26:55 Three characteristics 36:17 Not-self and openness 48:45 Unhooking me and mine 57:42 Habits, grooves, and trust
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12291/

item.title: Expressions of Metta
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores metta as more than a single feeling, showing it as a changing constellation that can include tenderness, warmth, clarity, compassion, silence, and even boundaries. He moves from self-kindness and the social habit of self-neglect into friendship, neutrality, and the difficult terrain of anger, while keeping the talk grounded in ordinary relationships and practice. In the last third, he opens metta into the body, silence, and a sense of love as something already holding experience, then returns to practice instructions and the possibility of oneness and questioning separation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Metta Has Many Flavors 02:43 Self-Kindness and Boundlessness 07:52 Attachment and Friendship 13:24 Uniqueness in Context 18:52 Neutrality and Selfing 25:22 Anger with Love 36:12 Hearing Hurt Well 45:40 The Body and Everyday Life 55:05 Silence and Held Experience
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12290/

item.title: Samadhi in Metta Practice
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores samadhi as collectedness, unification, and depth of meditation rather than a narrow, effortful concentration. He shows how metta can nourish that depth through happiness, gratitude, steadiness, and a softer relationship to experience, while also addressing common doubts about tightness, suppression, fear, and self-view. The talk ends by linking samadhi with emptiness: as the mind creates less, the heart can dwell more freely in love and receptivity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 What samadhi means 03:07 Collectedness and depth 07:41 Why it matters 12:14 How it develops 17:21 Happiness as a support 21:02 Working with obstacles 29:51 Effort, softness, and enjoyment 39:06 Rapture and fear 53:26 Samadhi and emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12294/

item.title: Views In Practice (...They make all the difference)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how views quietly shape experience, from obvious opinions to the subtle self-story that builds suffering. He moves from the Buddha’s centrality of right view, through the Four Noble Truths, impermanence, and not-self, to the question of whether our views actually lead toward freedom. The talk keeps returning to a practical test: when there is contraction or difficulty, what is being added in the present moment, and can it be seen differently?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question of view 02:34 Attached or unsteady views 07:10 Self-story and culture 11:26 Four Noble Truths 17:24 Happiness and cultivation 20:30 Long-term practice 28:26 Ways of looking 34:14 How suffering is built 43:26 Beyond views
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12308/

item.title: Equanimity
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores equanimity as steadiness of heart and mind, not indifference, grayness, or a shutdown of feeling. He works through the eight worldly conditions, the role of impermanence, and the way happiness can be cultivated through metta, compassion, and spiritual joy. The talk gradually widens into a quieter vision: when reactivity softens, experience is seen as less fixed and less powerful than it first appears.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Equanimity Means 02:01 The Penguin Story 03:34 The Eight Worldly Conditions 06:06 Open Heart, Not Indifference 09:09 Cultivation and Practice 15:49 Impermanence and Death 21:48 Happiness From Inner Qualities 31:09 Wonder and Spiritual Joy 39:55 Reactivity, Pain, and Spaciousness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12307/

item.title: The Nature of Awareness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores awareness as a central Dharma question, moving from familiar images of mind as a clean mirror or vast space into a more searching examination of whether awareness itself can be found as something independent. He traces how practice can reveal first clarity, then spaciousness, then the possibility that even the witness or pure consciousness is conditioned, empty, and inseparable from perception. The talk stays close to practical inquiry, using not-self and dependent origination to point toward a freedom that does not rest on any fixed view of mind.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 02:06 Practice over theory 04:02 The mirror metaphor 07:11 Clean but still seen 11:02 Spacious awareness 16:31 Vastness and oneness 24:10 Not to stop there 32:15 Undercutting the witness 40:00 Groundless and free
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12484/

item.title: Emotional Healing
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores emotional healing as a Dharma question rather than a fixed psychological method. He looks at how attention, kindness, grief, and cultivation shape our relationship to difficult emotion, and he argues that healing often depends as much on how we meet experience as on what we uncover. The talk also questions common assumptions about depth, trauma, happiness, and whether old material must be endlessly purged before there can be freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 02:23 Attention and presence 06:45 Healing as relationship 11:36 Kindness and accommodation 18:59 Seeing without self 20:57 Two wings of practice 24:51 Happiness and self-view 31:09 Therapy and psychologizing 43:02 Emptiness and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12481/

item.title: Samadhi
item.description:
In this talk, Rob Burbea explores samadhi as collectedness, depth, and unification of mind rather than narrow concentration. He moves through its relation to breath, metta, vipassana, happiness, and the hindrances, while also addressing common worries about effort, attachment, and emotional suppression. The talk gradually opens into a larger view in which samadhi supports insight, steadiness, love, and a softer relationship to experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What samadhi means 03:49 Right samadhi in context 12:03 A path to awakening 17:35 Happiness and appreciation 25:20 Tightness and balance 31:29 Whole-body practice 38:13 Effort, goals, and pīti 45:57 Fear and self-softening 54:40 Less doing, less self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12483/

item.title: From Feelings to Freedom (Exploring Vedana)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how bodily pain and the more subtle feeling tone of experience can be worked with in meditation without turning practice into an endurance test. He moves from practical instructions for pain into vedana as pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral, then into how sensitivity, non-attachment, and careful inquiry reveal impermanence, not-self, and the dependently arisen nature of experience. The talk ends by widening the frame to how calm, focused investigation can either open awareness or be applied deliberately to a chosen theme.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Working with bodily pain 02:52 Turning toward difficulty 04:19 Relaxing the whole body 06:11 Microscope and space 09:21 What vedana means 15:08 Sensitivity to feeling tone 21:44 Seeing vedana as not self 28:43 Grasping and aversion 33:03 Conditions and groundlessness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12482/

item.title: Letting Go of Judgement
item.description:
Rob Burbea distinguishes judgment from discernment and keeps returning to the way self-referential thinking tightens suffering. He offers practical ways to loosen judgment: reflecting on what we actually know, deliberately noticing positive qualities, meeting the pain of judgment with kindness, and using imagination to see thoughts as transient. The talk gradually moves from everyday self-criticism into a broader view of practice, metta, conditions, and finally a more non-dual sense of freedom beyond preferences.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 02:19 Practice and freedom 05:26 Judgment versus discernment 10:20 Working with judgment 14:53 Imagination and perspective 18:57 Metta and kindness 25:28 Views and self-making 31:20 Conditions over self 44:40 Beyond preferences
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21014/

item.title: Aspects of Mindfulness (Part 2)
item.description:
Rob Burbea places mindfulness back inside the full arc of Dharma practice, starting from the question of why we practice at all. He moves from freedom from suffering, to truth, to love, and then shows how mindfulness is only one part of a broader path that includes view, intention, speech, livelihood, effort, and collectedness. Later he offers a practical way to check practice when it feels stuck—recognition, acceptance, investigation, and non-identification—and ends by linking deep mindfulness with kindness, receptivity, and a softer relation to life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why practice mindfulness 01:08 Freedom from suffering 05:39 Truth and love 09:39 Mindfulness within the path 13:20 What mindfulness is not 19:37 Presence and sensitivity 29:42 RAN: a practice check 36:44 Embodiment and heartfulness 42:46 Active inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21011/

item.title: Aspects of Mindfulness (Part 1)
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores mindfulness as a practical part of the Buddhist path: not a vague ideal, but a way of paying careful attention to body, feeling, mind, and intention. He distinguishes simple presence from the deeper work of seeing what is actually happening, including our patterns of thought and self-judgment. The talk ends by emphasizing patience, non-judgment, and the repeated act of returning as the real practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 03:43 Mindfulness and awareness 06:57 What to attend to 11:55 Presence, not emptiness 15:50 Holding one thing in mind 19:06 Bare attention and clarity 22:47 Practice and repetition 26:24 Patience with wandering
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21012/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Rob Burbea explores mindfulness as a practical part of the Buddhist path: not a vague ideal, but a way of paying careful attention to body, feeling, mind, and intention. He distinguishes simple presence from the deeper work of seeing what is actually happening, including our patterns of thought and self-judgment. The talk ends by emphasizing patience, non-judgment, and the repeated act of returning as the real practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 03:43 Mindfulness and awareness 06:57 What to attend to 11:55 Presence, not emptiness 15:50 Holding one thing in mind 19:06 Bare attention and clarity 22:47 Practice and repetition 26:24 Patience with wandering
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21012/

item.title: Beyond the Prison of the Self
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how the sense of self quietly structures suffering, and why Buddhist practice is not about destroying the self but understanding it well enough to loosen its grip. He moves from generosity, compassion, and service into a close inquiry into grasping, measuring, comparing, and self-view, showing how these conditions build the feeling of “me” and “mine.” The talk ends by bringing not-self into immediate experience—breath, body, thought, and awareness—toward a lighter, less burdened way of living.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why practice turns inward 03:40 Not-self, not nihilism 08:30 Cultivating the heart 13:26 Generosity as release 18:51 Service beyond self 24:47 Looking without possession 28:35 Self-views and conditions 32:46 Nothing to identify with 37:32 Breath, grasping, and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12445/

item.title: Renunciation and Joy
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores renunciation as something much more alive than grim self-denial, arguing that it is closely bound up with joy, love, spaciousness, and freedom. He moves from the Buddha’s place on the Eightfold Path to Christian, Sufi, and poetic references, then turns to ordinary examples of consumption, identity, and desire. The talk keeps asking what we are really willing to give up, and what deeper happiness may become available when we do.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 01:56 Path and tradition 06:13 Beyond stereotypes 09:41 Freedom, not self-control 13:27 Consumerism and the path 16:59 What renunciation gives 21:58 Hunger and depth 27:04 Joy in ordinary life 37:30 Wise company and amazement
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12860/

item.title: The Place of Love in Insight Meditation
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores how love and kindness belong at the center of insight practice, not as decoration but as part of how the mind learns. He contrasts a narrow, self-improving approach with a wider practice that includes acceptance, community, and concern for all beings, then shows how that changes both suffering and perception. The talk gradually moves from metta as nourishment into a deeper view in which love, mindfulness, and emptiness are closely connected.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Nourishment and inquiry 02:17 Kindness as climate 05:41 Love beyond feeling 07:18 Sangha and silence 12:25 Beyond self-concern 21:49 Compassionate seeing 24:24 Welcoming resistance 33:33 Love and emptiness 38:50 Changing the view
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21006/

item.title: The Art of Letting Go
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores letting go as the heart of Dharma practice, but repeatedly insists that non-attachment is not cold detachment. He traces attachment through body, feelings, views, roles, ownership, and relationships, then turns toward mindfulness, kindness, and equanimity as the conditions that make real release possible. The talk ends by pointing to a deeper shift: seeing how suffering is co-created by grasping, and how the sense of separation itself can begin to fall away.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Letting Go Begins 03:51 Where Attachment Lands 06:11 What It Feels Like 09:11 Kindness Over Self-Judgment 13:48 Mindfulness And Training 20:12 Relationship And Strength 27:45 Roles, Ownership, Love 37:20 Suffering As Teacher 47:44 Welcoming And Emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21013/

item.title: Compassion and Emptiness
item.description:
Rob Burbea explores compassion as a natural response of the heart, then opens that idea out into its conditions, distortions, and deeper contemplative roots. He moves from practical reflections on empathy, fear, anger, pity, and self-view toward the way compassion grows through insight into emptiness, common humanity, and interdependence. The talk closes by widening into a more spacious view of love as something mysterious, universal, and not separate from seeing clearly.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Definition 02:30 Compassion as a Composite 05:55 Why We Value It 10:48 Questioning the Right Way 13:24 What Nurtures Compassion 19:46 Fear and Anger 24:59 From Blame to Understanding 31:29 Empathy and Spaciousness 39:53 Emptiness and Oneness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21007/

item.title: Contemplating The 3 Characteristics (Pt 2) - Anatta (Not-Self)
item.description:
Rob Burbea continues a talk on the three characteristics by taking up anatta, or not-self, as a practical way of looking rather than a metaphysical claim. He traces how selfing shows up in comparison, fear, pain, and self-view, then moves through the five aggregates, the shift from objects to awareness, and the subtler clinging that can remain there. The talk ends by showing how loosening the self-agenda can change perception, soften judgment, and open creativity, love, and equanimity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Framing Anatta 02:34 Why Self Matters 06:22 Pain And Self-View 14:12 Fear And Appreciation 20:12 Buddha And Practical View 24:10 Five Aggregates Practice 30:04 Avoiding Experience Clinging 35:48 Objects And Awareness 48:14 Mutuality And The Self Agenda
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21009/

item.title: Contemplating The 3 Characteristics (Pt 1) - Impermanence and Dukkha
item.description:
Rob Burbea opens with the Buddha’s three characteristics and keeps returning to the practical question of how they change the way practice is done. He moves from impermanence into dukkha, showing how clinging, aversion, and the wish for satisfaction shape suffering, and then broadens the frame with poems, a Diamond Sutra image, and warnings about turning insight into rejection. In the later part of the talk he points toward a quieter, wider way of seeing: steady awareness, less grasping, a sense of space, and the possibility of a love that does not divide experience into favourites and enemies.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Three Characteristics 03:21 Why Practice Must Be Deliberate 06:56 Facing Impermanence 11:46 Grief, Loss, and Time 16:48 Working With Change 26:38 Dukkha and Letting Go 31:46 Avoiding Aversion 39:44 Beyond the Conditioned 49:26 Closing Reflections
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21008/

item.title: Mindfulness of Mind States
item.description:
Rob Burbea offers a careful, practical talk on mindfulness of mind states as the third foundation of mindfulness. He explains how to notice not only emotions, but also the body sensations, thoughts, feeling tone, texture, and perceptual effects that shape a state of mind. The talk then turns toward working skillfully with difficult states, cultivating wholesome ones, and seeing mind states through the lens of impermanence and non-self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 From body to mind 04:07 What mind states include 10:11 Reading the whole constellation 15:00 Self-belief and view 20:09 Accommodating difficulty 24:49 When to rest and return 30:18 Cultivating the wholesome 35:50 Insight into change 43:25 Letting the talk settle
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21010/
